author: Denis Phillips
text: FORECAST | Good morning! Mostly cloudy and dry this morning. Sct'd storms return this afternoon with highs in the 90s. Coverage looks to be around 40-60% which is typical for this time of year.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Wednesday Tropical Thoughts:
1. Euro still brings a weak system in the Bahamas and skirts it along the East coast of Florida. Little impact to our area with this scenario. 
2. GFS (American model) is still the wild card. (And the outlier). It goes in the Eastern Gulf and would bring squally weather to the West coast of Florida over the weekend and early next week. 
3. The wave seems to be getting better organized today while the NHC still has it at a 60% chance of developing. 
4. The Orange hatched area still covers Florida but is leaning East coast track. So am I. 
5. 🐢

author: Denis Phillips
text: Cool shot of lightning strike and developing funnel cloud in Hudson Tuesday night. 📸 Debbie Mick Kolbrick

author: Denis Phillips
text: 12z Euro now trending a ragged system more to the West. Keeps a pretty sloppy low impacting Florida late in the weekend. Models will continue to flip flop for another day or 2. LOTS of different possibilities in play. Tonight would be a great night to check out DPL at 7:30.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Wednesday Tropical Thoughts:
1. The latest Euro is showing more of a West track. Remember, the "windshield wiper" effect often shows models will flip flop every 6 hours. However, our general rule of thumb is that the weaker a system is, the more West it will go. This is expected to stay weak for several days, so a little more West makes sense. 
2. Florida impacts from the Bay Area Southward is rain on Sunday and Monday. If it's a depression or a weak TS (Debby), maybe some breezy weather as well.  
3. Areas North (as we mentioned a few days ago) have a bit more of an opportunity for development IF the system would stay far enough offshore and tap that very warm Gulf water. So I would say the Northern Gulf coast needs to keep a bit closer eye on this.  
4. Based on the current thinking, the rain is never a bad thing for Florida.  So much going against development with dry air and land, but that very warm water is obviously the biggest wild card. 
5. Nothing to freak out about. We need the rain...we're gonna get some Sunday and Monday. 
6. Again, this is only the beginning of the season. We will be dealing with these type of systems for the next 3 months. There is nothing ominous about this one. Make your plans and keep an eye on things in case they change. 
7. 🐢 Rule #7  Don't freak out until I do, we're fine.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Important Tropical Update. Debby on the way?
Things are starting to come together...so is our "Orange Blob". Looks like Florida will get some rain from it, maybe a good bit. Let's talk about it. That's right now on DPL.

Should be able to pinpoint details by Friday. Rainfall amounts could very well be extreme across much of the state. We'll be updating 24/7 as always.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | A heat advisory has been issued for our area today. The combination of high temperatures in the low to mid-90s and high humidity will mean the heat index may reach up to 111F at times between noon and 6 pm. Sct'd storms will bring cooler temps for some during the afternoon.

author: Denis Phillips
text: FORECAST | Good morning! Sct'd storms return this afternoon providing some relief from the heat. Rain coverage could be a little higher than yesterday with the storms beginning a little earlier compared to Wednesday. Have a great day!

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Thursday tropical thoughts.
1. Time frame on whatever happens is Sunday-Tuesday. No impacts before then. 
2. A weak system in the Eastern Gulf would still bring a lot of rain to Florida early next week. 
3. The NHC still gives this a 60% chance of development.  Dry air, land and some shear are still impacting its development. 
4. As we've been saying for a few days, IF this develops, it seems the Northern Gulf coast would be the area of concern. Lots of warm water and more time to tap the energy. 
5. Obviously, exact track and proximity to the coast is still yet to be determined.  But the current thinking is a weaker system from the Keys to the Bay Area as it tries to organize. IF it is able to do so, the threat of a stronger system in the panhandle can't be ignored. However,  it is by NO means, a sure thing.  We'll be updating 24/7 as always. 
6. Rule #7. Nothing to freak out about. Just stay abreast of any possible changes or impacts for the Northern Gulf coast. Don't freak out over one run of a particular model. That will drive you crazy before this hurricane season is over and is wrong 90% of the time when it shows a stronger storm when others don't. 
7. 🐢

author: Denis Phillips
text: TROPICS | NHC continues to update the area they expect development with this tropical wave as the models shift west. After highlighting areas east of Florida yesterday, the latest model shift west has the NHC now looking into the eastern Gulf. Any development likely not occurring until the weekend at the earliest. (Note: I mentioned a comparison to Elena in 1985 on DPL last night.  Too early to know for sure, but whatever forms could easily stall and possibly even loop in the Northern Gulf. We could be dealing with a messy rainmaker for quite awhile next week)

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Afternoon Tropical Update:
1. No surprises or changes to our thinking. Now up to 70% chance of development this weekend or early next week. 
2. Models still show a ragged system moving along the Eastern Gulf heading North Sunday and early next week. 
3. There is certainly the possibility this could get stronger, especially in the Northern Gulf. The water is very warm. You already know that.
4. So it's continue to be stalked by this turtle until we reach the weekend and see how it shapes up. 🐢
5. I'll say it again. Lots of rain for Florida. The threat of a tropical storm or even a hurricane can't be ruled out for the Northern Gulf.
6. For the Bay Area, I see Saturday being fine. Sunday will see rain increasing from the South with breezy weather along the coast. Way too early to know if surge will be an issue as that depends how close it actually get to us. Again, risk is higher to the North. 
7. Rule #7. Nothing to freak out about. Just keep an eye out in things and make your plans as needed. Watch DPL and ABC Action News tonight for the latest.
8. I don't know if your flight is cancelled yet...but probably not. Yes, it's probably gonna rain at Disney next week. That's an answer to eleventy billion messages so far today. 🤣

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Important Update: 
1. If you missed DPL, I talked about what I call the "whack-a-mole" effect. Remember the carnival game? Those little guys pop up all over the place without much notice.  Well, when a disorganized storm is over land, it sometimes tries to form a new center over water...away from the existing one. With Invest 97, this is a possibility. What does that mean?  If a new center forms NORTH of Cuba, it would develop faster and move a bit more to the East. 
2/ I'm not saying it will happen, but I've seen this before. It still won't have much time to develop, but the track could be closer to the Bay area and the impacts would be felt sooner. (Saturday night or Sunday morning)
3. THIS is why you really need to have an actual low center to track...otherwise, it's still guesswork.  So, hopefully, by the morning we will have that and can get a more definitive answer for timing. (And if your flights will be canceled🤣. )
4.  Just throwing that out there so we won't get caught by surprise if a new center forms overnight.  Either way, we'll update you 24/7 as always.  The season is just beginning. It's a marathon, not a sprint. 
5.  Rule #7  Nothing to freak out about...just be prepared and have a plan as always.  🐢

author: Denis Phillips

author: Denis Phillips
text: Oz GFS continues to bring Tropical Storm Debby (to be) close to the coastline Sunday am. Other models more offshore. It won't have a ton of time to develop, but if this is accurate,  expect some squally weather late Saturday and into Sunday morning. We should know track by Friday am once the center forms. As always, we'll be updating 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 2am advisory is out. Up to 80% chance wave becomes a depression or a tropical storm. Red hatched area includes the West coast of Florida and extends into the Gulf. Rainy and squally weather along the coast late Saturday and into Sunday. Might get a track on Friday if NHC decides to pull the trigger on a PTC advisory.  (Potential Tropical Cyclone).

author: Denis Phillips
text: TROPICS | NHC increases chance of development near Florida to 80% as low pressure tracks along the northern Cuban coast toward the eastern Gulf.

author: Denis Phillips
text: TODAY | A heat advisory is back today for a heat index of around 110F at times during the afternoon. Take plenty of breaks if working outside and remember to hydrate.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | Chance of development now up to 60% over the next 2 days, 90% beyond that. NHC says watches/warnings will likely be needed for Florida later today so also expect advisories on a Potential Tropical Cyclone. This is all to be expected and is "on track" with the forecast.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Friday Tropical Thoughts. I think we finally have a pretty good handle on what's going to happen with our "Stalking Turtle" aka Debby. 
1. I suspect the NHC puts us in a Tropical Storm Warning later today or tonight. They will likely issue a Potential Tropical Cyclone advisory. This allows them to put up warnings and offer a track.
2. Speaking of track, it looks like what likely will be Debby will make landfall somewhere between Pinellas and Levy County on Sunday. 
3. The storm should either be a Tropical Depression or a Tropical Storm at that time. That is really the only question mark left on the table. The longer it stays over water, the stronger it could become. So, the sooner the landfall the better.
4. Impacts?  Winds between 40 and 50 mph seem likely...with higher gusts. That sounds about like one of our afternoon thunderstorms, right?  We will also be in the Northeast part of the storm so a "Tornado Watch" on Sunday is also possible. 
5. Surge. This will be a decent-sized system that is getting stronger at landfall.  There is a chance of some coastal flooding and water rise on Sunday. The water will already be higher (new moon) on Sunday so areas that are prone to flooding with storms should keep a close eye on this and plan accordingly. 
6. Lots of people are asking if this could become a hurricane. Look, anything is possible, but I'm not playing the "what if" game.  That spreads misinformation and its hard enough to get the forecast right with these ragged developing storms. At this point, I'm predicting a Tropical Storm with winds of 40 to 50 mph on Sunday. If it changes, you know we'll be updating 24/7 and let you know. 
7. It doesn't look like it will stall so the scenario of this thing sitting in Florida for several days no longer looks like its going to happen. Rainfall of 4-6" seems likely.
8. Let's recap.  Timing is late Saturday through early Monday...from our Southern counties to our Northern counties. This IS going to cut across the state so inland areas will also get wind. A Tropical Storm Warning seems likely later today as we see a PTC issued from the NHC.  
9.  Nothing to freak out about. We get 40 to 50 mph winds many times in the Summer. There could be some isolated power outages.  Wind-wise, it's an afternoon thunderstorm that will last for several hours. But as always, for folks who flood along the coast, high tide could bring a bit of water as well. Water is always the trickiest part of the forecast because it depends on the angle the storm approaches our area of who sees the push and who doesn't. 
10. I don't know if your flight is canceled and yes, it's going to rain at Disney.  (That should cover 95% of the questions I get)  We got this folks. This is probably good practice for what's on the way later this season.  Rule #7. (Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out, we're fine)  We live in Florida, this goes with the territory.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Our system now has a track... the NHC has designated the tropical wave as PTC 4. The track shows a tropical storm somewhere along our coast by Sunday morning. Tropical Storm Warnings and Watches are now in effect. I'll post that map next.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 5pm Advisory update for PTC #4:  No changes to winds or track. The Tropical Storm Watch has been extended North to Levy county. We'll have complete updates on ABC Action News and we will post our thoughts throughout the evening on social media.

author: Denis Phillips

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Friday Evening  Tropical Update:
1. No changes to our earlier post this morning. Finally, things are proceeding as forecasted. 
2. The timing of our squally weather will be Sunday morning through the evening. Track ranges from landfall around Sarasota to the Big Bend Area. Regardless of the exact track, wind and rain will be about the same. Think afternoon thunderstorm. Winds of 40ish mph with higher gusts. (Yes, IF the storm were to get stronger the winds would be higher, but let's cross that bridge IF it were to happen. Ok?)
3. Surge of 1-3 feet is possible and unfortunately, yet again, the tide is high when the storm approaches.  The exact track DOES matter on this. If it stays offshore longer, the storm will get stronger and the surge could be a bit higher in Citrus and Levy counties. If "Debby to be" goes onshore sooner, the resulting surge would be lower across the entire area. 
4. Expect rainfall of anywhere from 2 to 6 inches with some spots higher and some lower. That's just the nature of any tropical system that comes through Florida.
5. We will be in the NE quadrant so there is always the chance we could see a waterspout (or tornado) or 2. 
6. Bottom line, this is more of a nuisance for most people. There could be some power outages...just as we often see during our thunderstorm season. Do I expect widespread issues?  I don't. 
7. Bottom line, nothing to freak out about. We get 40 to 50 mph winds many times in the Summer.  Wind-wise, it's an afternoon thunderstorm that will last for several hours.  
8. Surge is always our biggest concern with this kind of system. NHC is saying 1 to 3 foot surge in the Bay.  It might be "just a tropical storm" with wind, but water can still be a big issue. The exact location and timing of landfall is the biggest factor with surge. 
9. Lastly, I don't know if your flight is canceled and yes, it's going to rain at Disney.  (That should cover 95% of the questions I get)  We got this folks. This is good practice for what's on the way later this season.  Rule #7. (Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out, we're fine)  We live in Florida, this goes with the territory.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Friday Evening Tropical Update:
1. No changes to our post earlier today.  Things are proceeding as forecast. Tropical Storm force winds for our area. (Think afternoon tstorms)  If the NHC track is accurate,  the storm would stay over water longer and could even become a low end hurricane before making landfall in the Big Bend area. Our winds would be the same although Citrus could be higher.
2. The timing of our squally weather will be Sunday morning through the evening. Track ranges from landfall around Sarasota to the Big Bend Area. Regardless of the exact track, wind and rain will be about the same. (30 to 40ish)
3. Surge of 1-3 feet is possible and unfortunately, yet again, the tide is high when the storm approaches.  The exact track DOES matter on this. If it stays offshore longer, the storm will get stronger and the surge could be a bit higher in Citrus and Levy counties. If "Debby to be" goes onshore sooner, the resulting surge would be lower across the entire area. 
4. Expect rainfall of anywhere from 2 to 6 inches with some spots higher and some lower. That's just the nature of any tropical system that comes through Florida.
5. We will be in the NE quadrant so there is always the chance we could see a waterspout (or tornado) or 2. 
6. Bottom line, this is more of a nuisance for most people. There could be some power outages...just as we often see during our thunderstorm season. Do I expect widespread issues?  I don't...at least not for the Bay Area.
7. Rule #7. (Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out, we're fine)  We live in Florida, this goes with the territory.  We got this.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Remember last night I gave the "whack-a-mole-example" with the center of the storm moving around and redeveloping in different spots? (Like the carnival game)
  The center has indeed developed a bit more to the South and West this evening.  Models will shift back and forth, but this is slightly different as a new center would mean a track more West.  It was a possibility we talked about extensively last night. 
  I would expect the NHC track to be nudged a bit to the West at 11 pm.  That would be good news for the Bay Area but not so good for areas up in the Big Bend as it would allow the storm to get stronger before landfall. A landfall that would be increasingly likely in a spot near where Idalia ended up last year. 
 We'll have the new update shortly.

author: Denis Phillips
text: WOW!  As we just posted, the NHC has indeed moved the track of what is now Tropical Depression #4 quite a bit to the West. The center has reformed South of Cuba and West of its original spot. This is certainly good news for our area yet it also increases the chance of a stronger storm in the Big Bend Area. Updates to come shortly after the newscast. Check us out at 11 on ABC Action News.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Overnight Tropical Depression #4 Update:
1. If you've been following my posts, you'd know we strongly suggested the center of this system would shift West and South. (The ole "Whack-a-mole" example). 🤣  That is exactly what has happened in the past 12 hours.  This has led to a track more to the West.
2. The models haven't changed. The location of the storm center has...and thereby, changing the path. (It's like if someone picked you up, and sat you down 30 miles to the West...your trip path would differ in the direction you magically were relocated) 
3. What does that mean?  It's good and bad.  It really doesn't change OUR forecast.  Still rainy on Sunday with gusty winds.  It MIGHT change the storm surge issue...although that's still dependent on how strong Debby (to be) will become along with its location.
4. Unfortunately, what this does change is it now gives the storm much longer time over water which will inevitably make it stronger. While the NHC keeps this just below hurricane status at landfall, I think the chances are high it will end up being a hurricane before impacting the Big Bend area. 
5. Ironically, back in 2012, Tropical Storm Debby hit the Big Bend area and caused significant flooding. It looks like it might happen again.
6. As for our area...the timing and intensity really haven't changed much. Scroll down to see my previous forecast..it remains about the same. 
7. As always, we'll be updating 24/7.  I don't expect the models to shift back East. Again, the models didn't change, the location of the storm did. I suspect this location sticks and we now have our center of circulation that will begin to strengthen and move North. 
8. Rule #7.  It's a LONG hurricane season and this is just the beginning.  Before we're done, I suspect we go through this at least another 2 or 3 times...but with stronger storms.  Florida weather is not for the faint of heart.

author: Denis Phillips
text: No real changes overnight. Saturday will be a typical August day with impacts from Debby (to be) arriving on Sunday.

author: Denis Phillips
text: For those nervous about an unexpected "hard right turn", don't be. Now that there's a well-defined center of circulation, the models will have a pretty easy job. No changes to impact on our area. The updated NHC track will come out around five.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hello Debby. The National Hurricane Center has upgraded Debby to a Tropical Storm. No real changes to the track, but they do bump up winds 5 mph upon landfall which would upgrade Debby to a hurricane. They also slow it down as it approaches land not making landfall until Monday afternoon.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Storm Surge updates for Sunday afternoon and evening. We'll be updating graphics and information all night long and I'll have a DPL starting at 7pm. Link will be posted. LIVE update on ABC Action News at 6.

author: Denis Phillips
text: (***Update: My son at FSU just told me classes are cancelled on Monday) For folks with kids at FSU or FAMU, you're probably gonna want to keep an eye on this storm. While the official forecast barely has it becoming a hurricane at landfall, there is a ton of warm water and energy out there and it is certainly possible it could be stronger. Could be close enough to Tally for impacts. Strong Tropical Storm force winds and some power outages on Monday. Lots of time to update but keep your kids in the loop. We'll update 24/7 as always.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Here are my thoughts on Tropical Storm Debby:
1. No changes to impacts for our area. Gusty winds along the coast with heavy rain. Think afternoon thunderstorm. The storm surge threat continues for Sunday afternoon and evening. 
2. A track to the Big Bend area is likely with landfall on Monday morning. The NHC has a minimal hurricane with winds of 75 mph but it is certainly possible it could be stronger. There is a TON of warm water out there and that's potential fuel for Debby.
3. Rainfall will be 2" inland and up to 8" along the coast. A Flood Watch remains in effect. Rain will continue through Monday as Debby will slow down after landfall. It wouldn't shock me one bit if some areas see more than a foot of rain.
4. Ironically, back in 2012, Tropical Storm Debby hit the Big Bend area and caused significant flooding. It looks like it might happen again. 
5. As for our area...intensity really hasn't changed much but the timing has slowed down a bit. The worst of the rain and wind will be Sunday afternoon and evening. 
6. Rule #7.  It's a LONG hurricane season and this is just the beginning.  Before we're done, I suspect we go through this at least another 2 or 3 times...but with stronger storms.  Florida weather is not for the faint of heart. Hang in there. We got this. And make sure friends and family in the BIg Bend area keep a VERY close eye on this one. There is the potential it could be a lot stronger than the NHC is forecasting.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Here are my thoughts on Tropical Storm Debby

author: Denis Phillips
text: Amazing how models will agree when there's actually a center to track!  This is a Big Bend storm. Our coastal areas will get pretty gusty tomorrow with much less rain and wind inland. I'll give updated thoughts after the 11pm NHC update.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm Update: Tropical Storm Debby now has winds of 45 mph. Notice the track, it really hasn't changed much. Maybe just a touch to the East. The models are in great agreement and we're confident of this track. The big question is just how strong. NHC now has 85 mph before landfall and with all that warm water, it could be stronger. Updates 24/7 and the latest right now on ABC Action News.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Overnight Debby thoughts:
1. No meaningful changes to the track. Recon found a slightly repositioned center and thus shifted the track just a touch to the East. The forecast remains solid.
2. When Debby passes our area, it will be getting stronger. Coastal areas should see winds around 40 mph on Sunday afternoon and evening. The winds could be higher in Hernando and Citrus counties. Storm surge is still a big concern for areas along the Gulf and Bay.  We will continue to update any changes, but the timing of high tide will be in the afternoon and early evening.
3. Landfall will occur in the Big Bend area on Monday morning. Latest guidance has winds of around 85 mph. The odds of Rapid Intensification according to models is about 30%. So not likely, but certainly possible. Either way, the impacts on our area will remain the same.  Windy, gusty along the coast with heavy downpours. The closer you are to the beach, the heavier the rains will be.
4. My suggestion?  Hang at home on Sunday and keep up to date with any changes we may see. It's gonna be a pretty nasty day and difficult to drive in the squally weather. Rainfall totals will be 4 to 8 inches along the coast with much lower totals inland.
5. Nothing to freak out about in the Bay Area. Unfortunately, the Big Bend will see much more wind and surge than we do, however, it's a pretty rural coastal area up there and shouldn't be nearly as strong as Idalia was last year when it hit pretty much the same area.
6. Keep it here 24/7. No one updates social media like we do during a storm. It's our passion. Hope you find it useful.  Stay safe on Sunday!

author: Denis Phillips
text: 2am update is in. No changes to intensity. Track is updated every 6 hours so the next advisory will have a new track. It won't change. This track seems relatively LOCKED IN. Intensity is still a question mark for landfall though.  Hopefully Debby just won't have enough time to get tremendously organized and strengthen quickly. Look for a rainy and windy day along the coast with much less wind and rain inland.  Have a great night! 🐢

author: Denis Phillips
text: DEBBY 5 AM | No changes. Debby is slowly strengthening with 50 mph winds. No changes to our forecast with the core of the storm staying well west of our coast. author: Denis Phillips
text:
Afternoon update:
 1. Lots of folks just got a Storm Surge Warning alert. High tides will rise through the afternoon. A surge between 2 and 4 feet seems reasonable in some spots at high tide. 
2. No changes to track. Rain bands will move through the area all day and night. Again, inland gets much less. Total amounts along the coast will be in the 4 to 8 inch range. 
3. Tornado threat with outer bands will continue this afternoon and evening. 
4. As for landfall, the Big Bend area will see Debby move onshore around 8am Monday morning. Winds will be in the 90 mph range. Not as strong as Idalia, but definitely packing a punch. I expect Tallahassee to see winds around 65 mph. 
5. Folks with kids at FSU and FAMU, expect power outages on Monday. With winds of 65-75 mph, there will be trees down in some spots, but the worst will be to the East.
6. Our weather will begin improving on Monday. We'll be on tv all day and updating online as well.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Overnight Tropical Update: This is exactly the time you'll start to see the models doing their "flip flop" thing. 0Z GFS back East with the "Big Bend" idea. This graphic shows GFS ensemble with numerous possibilities.  As we're been saying this weekend, all of the Florida Gulf Coast needs to be on guard. Highest probabilities are from the panhandle to the "Big Bend" but the Bay Area certainly not out of the woods yet. 97L is organizing quickly now. Hurricane Hunters fly in on Monday.  Don't take this storm lightly...especially if you live from the Nature Coast North. We'll know specifics later Monday.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | NHC increases chance of development in the Gulf. A tropical system in the Gulf is now likely by mid-week. All interests in the eastern half of the Gulf of Mexico need to monitor the progress of this storm closely.

author: Denis Phillips
text: FORECAST | Weather looks good today and tomorrow and much of Wednesday too. It will get a bit hot today and tomorrow and we could see a couple of pop-up afternoon showers.

author: Denis Phillips
text: The spaghetti plots this morning are pretty clustered which indicates a high amount of agreement between the models. This track can still change if the center of the storm forms in an unexpected location, thus throwing off the forecasts. Looking at the lines you may be thinking "oh, it's going to stay west. We'll miss this one." That's not quite true. The lines here show where the center of the storm will track. The center is only a small portion of a much larger storm that will extend hundreds of miles from that center. Yes, the most destructive winds will stay off-shore from us BUT we will be well into the "envelope of impacts," on the eastern, stronger side of the storm. Another way to look at this is to compare the forecast tracks to that of Debby (which is in red on this map). Debby was actually forecast to track almost the identical distance & ended up a little closer to us. We certainly got a ton from that storm in terms of rain, gusty winds & multiple tornado warnings. Flooding was a huge issue due to heavy rain. It's very likely that some of these impacts will again be felt in our area from the storm this week. As for winds, a storm where the center is over a 100 miles away usually brings sustained tropical storm force winds, not hurricane force winds. But again, the exact track is not set in stone. As the center forms & we get a better idea of the exact track & more importantly, a broad idea of potential intensity we will then be able to better speak about the impacts to us.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | Minor update from the NHC increasing the 2 day development chances to 80%. Advisories could be issued on this as early as tomorrow in order to facilitate watches/warnings for the Gulf coast.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tropical Thoughts for Monday: 1. No real changes from our thoughts on Sunday. We still don't have a "trackable" center for models to pinpoint for forecasting. We DO have a broad area and one they do their best with. So, it's possible a center forms away from the current thinking. Obviously, one to the East would move these lines to the right and a center to the left would move it to the West. 2. Hurricane hunters will be flying into what will become Helene today. Once this data is loaded into the models, there should be more confidence to track and intensity. Even still, there's a surprising amount of agreement with this track...especially for a system that isn't well formed yet. 3. Timing. This will be a fast-moving storm. Some good things come from this. Less time to intensify. Less time for rainfall totals. However, there is absolutely time for "Helene to be" to become a strong hurricane before landfall.  Right now, landfall looks to occur on Friday... possibly Thursday night. 4. Intensity is still a BIG question mark. Honestly, this could be anywhere from a strong Tropical Storm to a Major Hurricane. And this is why folks need to take it seriously. It will intensify quickly. Don't get caught with your pants down...so to speak, as my mom used to say. Impacts of heavy rain, gusty winds (possibly to hurricane force along the coast, and surge are the most likely. With a storm like this, based on the current model data, a surge of 3 or 4 feet for the Bay Area is possible. Obviously, if the track is closer, those numbers will go up) 5.  Folks with kids at Florida State University and FAMU. Since the track isn't set in stone, we don't know the exact impacts yet. We will within 24 hours.  IF you decide it gets to the point you want them to come home, they would still be ok to leave Wednesday morning. Anything later might be too late. I am NOT suggesting they leave. I am just saying IF conditions are expected to get bad enough for you to want them to leave, that's the time frame. 6.  Bottom line, while the models look more like a panhandle or Big Bend landfall, that isn't set in stone yet. I suspect we are in a Tropical Storm Watch by Tuesday morning. Could we see a Hurricane Watch?  We could...although if the spaghetti models are close to being correct, the sustained hurricane-force winds would stay offshore for the Bay Area. 7. Rule #7. Nothing to freak out about. It's the time to make preps as needed and keep a close eye on any changes to the forecast. If you live in the Panhandle or the Big Bend, you'll want to keep a closer eye on this and be prepared to act accordingly when (not if) this gets stronger and probably becomes a hurricane. Again, nothing is set in stone, but we're certainly leaning toward a high-impact storm hitting Florida later this week. #8.  I STILL don't know if your flight will be canceled, your Disney trip will be ruined, your cruise will be scratched or you'll be able to walk your dog at 4:23 Thursday afternoon.  But I promise,  when I figure it out you'll be the first to know.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11am Update: PTC 9 is forecast to strengthen over the next few days as it moves northward into the Gulf. More updates on the way for potential impacts on Florida.

author: Denis Phillips
text: (Update! Official track just issued) Tropical Update (Separating Fact from Fiction) 1. "Helene to be" will be a significant threat to Florida (Fact). We've been talking about this for well over a week. There are no surprises here. The spaghetti models you see are "best guess" at this point given there is still not a well-defined center. (Even still it is rare to see such an agreement this early)  Bottom line, chances are extremely high Florida will see a hurricane on Thursday. Impacts on the Bay Area will start as early as Wednesday night) Based on the current track, the worst wind will again be "Big Bend" to the panhandle, however, some models are closer to us. I have no doubt we will be a PTC (Potential Tropical Cyclone) advisory later today that will give an actual NHC track.  I also think we will be in a Tropical Storm or Hurricane Watch as early as Tuesday morning. 2. Surge. Some surge models are showing a significant surge along our coastline on Thursday night. (Fact)  These models are very much dependent on track, so if the track gets closer, the surge will be higher. Remember, surge can impact our area even when a storm is well over 150 miles away. It's a bit too early to give exact numbers, but slosh models for Thursday night are showing a surge even higher than some have seen in the past couple of years. You will probably see surge numbers floating around the internet. Remember, these numbers from the NHC are "reasonable worst-case scenario" forecasts. They want to give you the worst case so you can prepare in case it plays out that way. If you live in flood-prone areas, DO NOT take this storm lightly. It may very well stay well offshore, but water can (and likely will) build up along the coast. 3. The worst of the wind will be within about 15 miles of the center. So, as is usually the case, areas of extreme wind impact are quite small. For this, the exact track is obviously a huge factor. At this point, that track seems like one we have seen often lately. Idailia being the strongest last year. So to be clear, if these spaghetti models are correct, the Bay Area will likely NOT see sustained hurricane force winds, but could see gusts along the coast that high. 4, I have little doubt there will be a lot of closings and cancellations starting on Wednesday..but mostly on Thursday and Friday. Keep it right here for the latest. 5. Intensity.  It is very likely this will become a hurricane. (Fact)  It will be a major hurricane. (NOT factual, but certainly possible) I suspect the NHC forecast at least a Cat 2 upon landfall, and possibly even higher. We don't deal in worst-case scenarios around here, but yes, in this case, where landfall occurs, it's a real possibility.  So, from the panhandle to the "Big Bend", don't let your guard down on this one.  And even the Bay Area could see hurricane-force gusts.  If I were to compare it to a storm of late for impacts on our area, winds from Irma were 65 to 75 with higher gusts. IF the models are right, that's about what we would see along the coast. INLAND AREAS WILL SEE LESS.  This storm will NOT cut across the state so areas East of I-75 will see lower impacts from wind. 6. Again, if you have kids at Florida State University and FAMU, keep a very close eye on this one. I expect a track to come out later today.  If this track is within 20 miles of Tally, you can decide whether you want your kids to ride out the storm. Some folks suggest it might be more dangerous driving home than riding it out. That might very well be true for some kids. Dorms are usually built really well...sometimes apartments, not so much.  That is a personal decision for you and your family to make.  But again, IF that track is forecast close to Tally when it comes out later today, take it seriously. 7. You may note my "tone" seems a bit different with this one. You're right. While the current data suggests the worst of this storm is North and West of the Bay Area, a subtle change to the East could bring stronger impacts to our area. That is NOT the forecast, but it's something that can't be completely ruled out. In my opinion, an NHC track will probably show a track from just West of Tally into the Big Bend. That, as is often the case, brings the worst of this into the panhandle.  So, make the preps you need to ride out another one.  I'll be posting my list of preps our family uses shortly.  It can be pretty helpful. To put it simply, prepare for the worst, hope for the best. You can never be overprepared in this type of situation. 8. If you live in the panhandle, the Big Bend, or even the Bay Area, now is NOT the time to freak out. It's the time to make decisions and plans you need to keep your family safe. Rule #7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Lots of information on the way. I'll be posting as much useful information as I can come up with. Here is a look at the most likely time tropical storm force winds will arrive in your area. Remember, winds will be stronger along the coast and less inland. Hurricane gusts are certainly possible along the coast. IF the official NHC forecast verifies, most (if not all) of the Bay Area will NOT see sustained hurricane-force winds. However, if the track goes East around 50 miles, which IS in the cone of uncertainty, our winds will be higher. Keep a close eye on any changes.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Here is a look at the chances of Tropical Storm force winds vs Hurricane-force winds. Remember, ALL maps posted from the NHC are based on their CURRENT forecast. If that forecast changes one way or the other, the area of winds will also adjust accordingly. Obviously, the current track keeps the strongest winds away from the Bay Area, however, we are still in the cone of uncertainty.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Prep List: Here's a list I've compiled over the years. Our family has found it helpful. 1. Charge any device that provides light. Laptops, tablets, cameras, video cameras, and old phones. Old cell phones can still used for dialing 911. Charge external battery backups. ( Glow sticks also provide light for a few hours. Stand them upright in a glass or jar to make a lantern.) 2. Wash all trash cans, big and small, and fill them with water for flushing toilets. Line outdoor trash cans with trash bags, fill them with water, and store them in the garage. Add bleach to sterilize. 3. Fill every tub and sink with water. Cover sinks with Saran Wrap to keep it from collecting dust. Fill the washing machine and leave the lid up to store water. 4. Fill old empty water bottles and other containers with water and keep them near sinks for washing hands. 5. Fill every Tupperware with water and store in the freezer. These will help keep food cold longer and serve as a backup water supply. 6. Fill drinking cups with water and cover with Saran Wrap. Store as many as possible in the fridge. The rest you can store on the counter and use first before any water bottles are opened. Ice is impossible to find after the storm. 7. Reserve fridge space for storing tap water and keep the sealed water bottles on the counter. 8. Cook any meats in advance and other perishable foods. You can freeze cooked food. Hard boil eggs for snacks for first day without power. 9. Be well hydrated before the storm hits and avoid salty foods that make you dehydrated. 10. Wash all dirty clothes and bed sheets. Anything dirty will smell without the A/C, you may need the items, and with no A/C, you'll be sweating a lot. You're going to want clean sheets. 11. Toss out any expired food, clean cat litter boxes, and empty all trash cans in the house, including bathrooms. Remove anything that will cause an odor when the A/C is off. If you don't have a trash day pickup before the storm, find a dumpster. 12. Bring in any yard decor, secure anything that will fly around, secure gates, bring in hoses, potted plants, etc. Bring in patio furniture and grills. 13. Clean your environment so you have clear, easy escape routes. Even if that means temporarily moving furniture to one area. 14. Scrub all bathrooms so you are starting with a clean odor-free environment. Store water-filled trash cans next to each toilet for flushing. 15. Place everything you own that is important and necessary in a backpack or small file box that is easy to grab. Include your wallet with ID, phone, hand sanitizer, snacks, etc. Get plastic sleeves for important documents. 16. Make sure you have cash on hand. 17. Stock up on pet food and fill up bowls of water for pets. 18. Refill any medications. Most insurance companies allow for 2 emergency refills per year. 19. Fill your propane tanks. You can heat soup cans, boil water, make coffee, and other stuff besides just grilling meat. Get an extra, if possible. 20. Drop your A/C in advance and lower temperatures in your fridges. 21. Gather all candles, flashlights, lighters, matches, batteries, and other items and keep them accessible. 22. Clean all counters in advance. Start with a clean surface. Buy Clorox Wipes for cleaning when there is no power. Mop your floors and vacuum. If power is out for 10 days, you'll have to live in the mess you started with. 23. Pick your emergency safe place such as a closet under the stairs. Store the items you'll need in that location for the brunt of the storm. Make a hand fan for when the power is out. 24. Shower just before the storm is scheduled to hit. 25. Keep baby wipes next to each toilet. Don't flush them. It's not the time to risk clogging your toilet! 26. Run your dishwasher, don't risk having dirty smelly dishes and you need every container for water! Remember you'll need clean water for brushing your teeth, washing yourself, and cleaning your hands. 27. Put a small suitcase in your car in case you decide to evacuate. Also, put at least one jug of water in your car. It will still be there if you don't evacuate!  Remember to pack for pets as well. 28. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you! 30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm. 31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out. 32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates. 👍  You need water to wash dishes....👎 33 . Also if you run out of water tap your hot water heater it can have up to 30 gallons stored in there. 34 . Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer. 35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down!💩 If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!! I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!! Rule #7  And for those asking, that means "Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out..we're fine".  Whatever comes our way, we've got this as long as we stay informed and prepared.

author: Denis Phillips
text: The storm’s wind field is forecast to be rather large compared to recent storms we’ve gone through. This would bring quite a bit of surge to our coast, maybe higher than Idalia or Debby. Any shifts east will mean a greater surge threat.

author: Denis Phillips
text: PTC #9 Update:  Here is the latest track on what will become Helene. NHC now predicts Cat 3 before landfall. Notice, they did NOT move the track to the East at this point. Obviously, the Bay Area is hoping we don't see that bump to the East. Lots of analysis on the way. We're on LIVE on ABC Action News and updates on Facebook as well.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tropical Updates and Thoughts: 1. 5pm advisory is just an extension of the previous one.  There was NO change to track or shift to the East...in spite of those constant model flip flops. Please don't dwell on individual run changes...they will drive you crazy. Look for trends, or better yet, listen to the experts. 2. IF this track is correct, we will see hurricane-force gusts on the coast. We will NOT see these winds East of I-75, If the track DOES shift, then those winds would also shift inland.  Let's try something novel. Instead of focusing on every model shift and worst-case scenarios, let's deal with facts. We ALL know there can be last-minute changes, but throwing them out there like they're factual can be very confusing. I prefer to stick with what we know, instead of one of the eleventy billion possibilities that exist with each storm. 3. Thursday will be the worst day. Helene will be moving very quickly so we won't have the rainfall totals some saw with Debby.  Unfortunately, the surge could be a much bigger issue. Computer models are currently forecasting higher surge levels on Thursday night than we saw with ETA. If you live on the coast, TAKE THE SURGE THREAT SERIOUSLY.  Remember, surge is NOT rain. It's Gulf or Bay water pushed into coastal areas. 4. For our area, winds of 65-75 (gusts) near the coast...less inland. Again, based on the current track. Changes to the track would create changes to the local impacts. 5. Not gonna candy-coat this one. Even with a track forecast to be over 100 miles offshore, SLOSH models are predicting a major surge event for this area. Higher than ETA or Idallia. If it gets closer, those numbers will be higher.  Hide from the wind, run from the water.  Don't forget that one. 6. I'll have nightly LIVE broadcasts (DPL) to help answer your questions and give you the latest. Yes, I'll even cover if your flight will be canceled, if Disney will close, and even what time you can walk your dog on Thursday. Yes, you won't find a more dedicated team on air and online. We know these storms can be scary. Our hope is to give you the information you need to help keep your family safe and add a bit of levity at times as well. Everyone needs a break from scare tactics and hype. They're the worst in a stressful situation. 7. As we've been saying, if you have kids at Florida State University or FAMU and you plan on bringing them home, do so on Tuesday or early Wednesday at the LATEST. They may be safer in their dorm than on the roads. Apartments in Tally might be another story.  Do your research on their hurricane preps. I can't advise you on what to do in this capacity, but the current track probably brings the Western EyeWall very close to Tally. That's not good. Of course, the track could always change. If it does, we'll let you know. 8. We should know exactly where this storm is going within 24 hours. Keep it right here for the latest. We're here for you.

author: Denis Phillips
text: I mean, it's not wrong.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Helene?  It certainly looks that way All you need to know about our potential major hurricane that arrives on Thursday. Where is Helene to be going? How will it impact us? That's right now on DPL

author: Denis Phillips
text: Evening Tropical Update: 1. Updated tracks come out every 6 hours. I would expect no changes at 11pm. 2. Ensembles still show a ton of consistency,  but we Floridians know there can be little wobbles and turns. Anyone on the Gulf coast needs to take this storm seriously. Still, the highest threat in from the Big Bend to the panhandle...at least right now. 3. Thursday is the day for pretty much the entire state. Landfall will be later in the evening. You have all day Tuesday and Wednesday to prep as needed. This is obviously especially true for areas up North. 4. Based on current track, folks with kids at Florida State University and FAMU should probably decide what to do by Tuesday.  If the current track verifies, Tallahassee will get hit hard. All those big trees. Lots of power outages. Again, based on the track not changing. If the storm jogs East, impact would be lower. University of Florida students will see the fringe of the heaviest weather. Again, based on current track, they're probably OK hunkering down. But again, that is a decision you need to figure out based on expected conditions on Thursday.  Watch for changes! A shift East will increase the threat. 5. Surge is still a massive concern for coastal areas. I suspect evacuation orders will be issued on Tuesday for some folks. We will see Tropical Storm or Hurricane Watches issued by tomorrow morning. Helene will bring a fast moving system so that will limit rainfall in some spots. 6. I'll update overnight if we see any changes to track and model data. Again, updated track comes out at 11pm.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm Update: Still not quite Helene. No changes to track or intensity. NHC still believes it will be Cat 3 before landfall. Again, the system is not organized yet. Once it does, there could still be a relocation of the center which could change the ultimate track. This is covered in our discussions today. We'll update any changes overnight.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Overnight update: No changes to track or intensity with 11pm advisory.  Overnight models are coming in a bit to the East, but nothing Earth shattering. I'll say this again. It all boils down to where the low center is located Tuesday evening. 1. If it's over the Yucatan,  panhandle landfall seems likely. 2. If it's in-between the Yucatan and Cuba a "Big Bend" landfall is likely. 3. If it's over Cuba, a track closer to our area will probably occur. We should have our answer by Tuesday evening. If these tracks verify,  we will probably not see sustained hurricane force winds in the Bay Area. Gusts along the coast, possibly, but sustained is unlikely. Surge is still a concern as some kind of coastal evacs seem likely on Tuesday. Of course, if the track is closer to us, that's another story entirely. No real changes to our thinking. Feel free to check back on my lengthy posts from earlier tonight if you want more information. These posts also act as a sleep aid if you're tossing and turning.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | 5 AM advisory is in. Still looking at PTC 9 with winds of 35 mph. Forward motion is up 2 mph and pressure is a little down too. New forecast has no change in track with a bump up to Cat 3 west of Tampa Bay on Thursday afternoon.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | Hurricane and storm surge watches have been issued for all of our coast including Tampa Bay.

author: Denis Phillips
text: SURGE | Here is the first surge forecast from the NHC. 5-8 ft. for Tampa Bay, 6-10' for the Nature Coast and 10-15 ft. up toward the Big Bend.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | Tropical storm and hurricane watches are not only in effect for the coast for interior counties too.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Models continue to have an extremely high level of agreement on track. Even the ensembles show a high level of agreement. But I still believe it falls under the "too close for comfort" category. I'll be posting my thoughts shortly.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tuesday Tropical Thoughts: 1. PTC 9 is expected to be named Helene later today. Upper-level winds are keeping it from getting its act together so far, but eventually, it's gonna happen. So here's what I'm thinking. 2. Hurricane and Tropical Storm Watches are in effect for our area. Based on the current track, the closer you are to the coast, the more wind you will get. IF, and that's a big if, IF the storm follows the exact forecast track, we will see gusts of hurricane force along the coast and much less wind inland. If the storm goes more to the East, those numbers will be higher. As Floridians, I think we all know that is a possibility regardless of what the models say. We have seen it happen so many times before. So, my point is, do not get overconfident with a track that has low impacts on our area. It's simply too early for that. 3. Surge will be an issue for our area even if it DOES stay well offshore. Helene is forecast to be a sizeable storm and will push water this way. Yes, if the track is closer, the surge will be higher, but a surge of 5 to 8 feet is possible based on the current track. That is similar to Idalia in many places. It could be significantly higher if the track is closer to our area. It always boils down to "hide from the wind, run from the water". If evacuation orders are issued, please heed them. 4. The current track takes this major hurricane very close to Tallahassee.  Remember, the heaviest winds are usually confined to a very small area...maybe 5 miles. So any adjustment to the track East would probably minimize the winds in Tally. Anyone considering bringing their kids home should do so today or early tomorrow. Florida State University and FAMU will see 100-plus mph winds if the current track verifies.  University of Florida students would also have higher impacts if the storm goes more to the East and lower impacts if the current track is accurate. 5. I'm sure you sense my tone with this one.  I want to believe the science. I want to believe the models. I want to rely on my experience with these things. But I've lived here 35 years, and we all know storms coming in from this angle can be very tricky. I do NOT want another Ian. Ft. Myers really didn't see it coming because of the late change in track. Let me be clear, I am NOT saying that will happen with us, but I want to be darn sure that you're prepared for whatever comes this way.  If the current track is correct, that's 65ish sustained winds along the coast and much less inland. But if that track goes 50-100 miles more to the East, it's a completely different ballgame. Just be prepared, that's all I'm saying. Take it seriously. 6. You have 2 full days to get ready.  If the NHC track is accurate, we'll be just fine with wind. Yes, there will be power outages, but nothing we haven't seen numerous times. The surge is an issue, and nowadays, that's a real thing. Again, the surge ONLY impacts people on the water. Surge is NOT rainwater...it's Gulf water. 7. Rule #7. This is definitely NOT the time to freak out. It's the time to prepare for whatever comes this way. I will post my prep suggestions again this morning in case you missed it yesterday. If the forecast is close to being accurate, (and there is no meteorological reason to think it won't be) the Bay Area will be ok. If there is a change to the track, we'll give you as much time as humanly possible to react accordingly. As for the panhandle and Big Bend, take this one seriously.  This could very well be Idalia Part 2.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Today and tomorrow look great for any preps. Mostly sunny and hot today with an isolated PM shower. Wednesday we'll see some sct'd rain but we won't get into any rain bands along the coast until the evening. Winds will not be an issue through Wed PM.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | PTC 9 Is now Tropical Storm Helene. No major change in track or intensity forecast. I'll post my updated thoughts shortly.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Reposting from yesterday...especially for folks in the path of Helene. At this point, highest threat is "Big Bend" and panhandle. Add bleach to sterilize. 3. Fill every tub and sink with water. Cover sinks with Saran Wrap to keep it from collecting dust. Fill the washing machine and leave the lid up to store water. 4. The rest you can store on the counter and use first before any water bottles are opened. Ice is impossible to find after the storm. 7. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you! 30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm. 31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out. 32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates. Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer. 35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down! If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!! I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!! Rule #7  And for those asking, that means "Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out..we're fine".  Whatever comes our way, we've got this as long as we stay informed and prepared.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Quick Afternoon thoughts: 1. We now have Tropical Storm Helene. Outside of that, absolutely nothing has changed from this morning. Check out my previous posts for my thoughts. 2. It ALL boils down to where this low is once it enters the Gulf. The steering currents are pretty much locked down. We said this yesterday. If the center is over the tip of the Yucatan tomorrow, the track should go more Panhandle/Big Bend. If the center is midway between Cuba and the Yucatan the track would be more Big Bend toward the Nature Coast.  If the center is over Western Cuba, a track closer to the Bay Area is likely. The conditions driving movement aren't expected to change much, so it's pretty much where it starts its trip across the Gulf that will determine landfall. 3. I posted hurricane prep information in a post below. It's a really useful list our family has come up with over the years. 4. Lastly for Florida State University, FAMU, University of Florida, and University of South Florida students, the current forecast brings significant winds to Tallahassee on Thursday. If families decide to evacuate, they should do so no later than Wednesday afternoon. The worst of the weather won't arrive up there until later Thursday.  For Gainesville, Helene's current track will bring hurricane gusts, but the worst would be West of you. IF the track changes East, you could see more wind. That's something I'll be keeping a close eye on. I'll be updating 24/7 to help you make the best decision for you and your family.

author: Denis Phillips
text: And...he's in Port Richey. (My bet is he ends up moving North)

author: Denis Phillips
text: Here's a look at the most likely time winds will reach over 40 mph. This go around, it will be mostly a Thursday DAYTIME event for the Bay Area. That's a change. I'll have a complete update for our area as well as students at Florida State University, University of Florida University of South Florida and Florida A&M University later this afternoon.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Unfortunately, Helene is starting to get that "look". No changes to track expected when the new advisory drops around 5. We'll have it for you along with our thoughts. As always, the team is here for you 24/7. It's how we cover hurricanes at ABC Action News.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Warnings are now in effect from Pasco County Northward. Tropical Storm Warnings for the rest of the area. This will be mostly a daytime event for the Bay Area with winds strongest Thursday afternoon and evening.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 5pm Advisory: Tropical Storm Helene now has winds of 50 mph. The track is still rock solid among models. We always look for last minute changes, but at this point, the odds of a direct strike in our area in becoming less likely. Helene is a large storm however, and impacts will include winds gusting to nearly hurricane force and a Storm Surge Warning is now in effect as well. Updates 24/7. We will be posting our updated thoughts shortly and the latest right now on ABC Action News.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tropical Thoughts on Helene: 1. NO changes to the 5pm track. Models are still locked in on the panhandle. It appears very unlikely (not impossible) but very unlikely Helene will make landfall in the Bay Area. As you may have noticed, we are no longer in the cone of uncertainty. 2. While we may not see landfall here, impacts will still be high.  While I expect SUSTAINED hurricane-force winds will remain offshore, we will still see several hours of gusts to near hurricane-force on Thursday afternoon and evening. Inland areas will see less wind and rain. And what is a lower threat to our area, unfortunately, the risk continues to grow for the panhandle and the "BIg Bend" area. Helene is expected to be a major hurricane when it makes landfall Thursday evening. 3. The entire Gulf Coast of Florida is under a "Storm Surge Warning". Surge is NOT caused by rainfall. It's water pushed by the storm. Helene is a very large system and will push a lot of water. Surge levels are expected to rival Idalia and ETA in some spots. Check the map listed for areas that may impact you. If you don't live near the water, you will not see surge. 4. Let's talk about landfall.  Students at Florida State University and Florida A&M University are expected to be close to landfall. I can't suggest whether or not you have friends, family, and kids evacuate ahead of Helene, but I will advise you that if they ARE going to "get out of Dodge", they need to have that travel completed by tomorrow afternoon. You won't want to be on the roads on Thursday.  As for University of Florida, University of South Florida, and University of Central Florida students, at this point, evacuations don't appear necessary. Don't get me wrong, they could still get winds of 65(ish) mph with power outages, but the type of damaging winds the panhandle could see will be very different. 5. So here's the bottom line. Unless there is a drastic change (which is always possible, but unlikely) this is a storm with a track heading to the panhandle. Our Thursday will see winds of 65 mph, heavy rains, storm surge levels of 5-8 feet and isolated tornadoes. And even with all that, if it plays out that way, we should consider ourselves VERY fortunate. Helene will become a large powerful hurricane that will cause extensive impacts to our friends and family North of the Bay Area. 6. One last thing, and I swear I'm not just "covering by rear end", until we see where this actually bypass the Bay Area, don't let your guard down. I'm feeling very confident in this forecast, but it IS Florida Weather. Enough said.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane and Storm Surge Warnings All you need to know about our potential major hurricane that arrives on Thursday. Where is Helene to be going? How will it impact us? That's right now on DPL

author: Denis Phillips
text: Wind gusts of 50-70 mph are likely across much of the Bay Area on Thursday afternoon. (Less inland) We can lose power in an afternoon tstorm. I would expect rather widespread outages for awhile Thursday afternoon and evening as Helene passes by to our West.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Aw, you guys are awesome! Thanks for topping off MY hurricane kit.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Evening Tropical Update: 1. 0z model runs still locked in. No changes to our thinking. Watches and Warnings listed on my page. Here's what we will see on Thursday: A. Winds gusting 50-70 mph in the afternoon and evening. Much less inland B. Storm Surge Warning along the coast. Water rise will be highest Thursday evening. Don't forget, hide from the wind and run from the water. C. Power outages likely Thursday afternoon. D. Tornado risk a possibility afternoon and evening. 2. Landfall in the "Big Bend late Thursday evening as a major hurricane. 3. Tropical Storm force winds will continue all the way through Atlanta. 4. If Florida State University and Florida A&M University students are planning on evacuation before the storm arrives, please do so 1st thing in the am on Wednesday.  I think University of Florida and University of South Florida students will face less severe weather. Yes, still winds gusting 50 to 70 mph but significantly less than schools in the panhandle. 5. By Friday, this will be over for us. I'll update new numbers overnight with updated track at 11pm.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm update: Helene still has winds of 60 mph. No real change to the track. A few models are trying to nudge the track a bit East, but most are keeping the track locked in. Either way, NHC expects Helene to make landfall as a powerful Cat 3 hurricane with winds of 120 mph. Updates on ABC Action News and of course, we're on it right through landfall. We're here for you 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Similar to Debby, the east side of Helene, where we will be, will carry the threat of isolated tornadoes in its outer rain bands. The number of these is impossible to predict. Some storms produce many while others hardly any at all. We'll watch every rain band today and tomorrow closely.

author: Denis Phillips
text: HELENE | 5 AM Wed advisory is in. Track nudged a little west though still basically looking at a Big Band landfall with the center possibly tracking close to or over Tallahassee. Storms don't follow the center line so don't get too tied to this one update. No change in terms of impacts for us.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | Helene is intensifying quickly. Pressure is down 6 mb since the last update and winds are at 70 mph. Helene will be a hurricane soon as it moves into the southern Gulf of Mexico.

author: Denis Phillips

Buy Clorox Wipes for cleaning when there is no power. Mop your floors and vacuum. If power is out for 10 days, you'll have to live in the mess you started with. 23. Pick your emergency safe place such as a closet under the stairs. Store the items you'll need in that location for the brunt of the storm. Make a hand fan for when the power is out. 24. Shower just before the storm is scheduled to hit. 25. Keep baby wipes next to each toilet. Don't flush them. It's not the time to risk clogging your toilet! 26. Run your dishwasher, don't risk having dirty smelly dishes and you need every container for water! Remember you'll need clean water for brushing your teeth, washing yourself, and cleaning your hands. 27. Put a small suitcase in your car in case you decide to evacuate. Also, put at least one jug of water in your car. It will still be there if you don't evacuate!  Remember to pack for pets as well. 28. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you! 30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm. 31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out. 32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates. You need water to wash dishes... 33 . Also if you run out of water tap your hot water heater it can have up to 30 gallons stored in there. 34 . Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer. 35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down! If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!! I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!! Rule #7  And for those asking, that means "Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out..we're fine".  Whatever comes our way, we've got this as long as we stay informed and prepared.

author: Denis Phillips
text: For once, rainfall with this storm may be our lowest threat of all the issues the storm may bring. Though 3-6" is possible area-wide, all of the model data seems to indicate the lower end of this range is more likely for most.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Helene Thoughts for Wednesday: 1. Helene is about to begin its rapid intensification stage. The NHC has winds of 120 mph at landfall. I'll be honest, it could go higher. The track really hasn't moved much. There was a bit of a nudge to the West at 5am, but really nothing noteworthy. New tracks are updated every 6 hours. (5am, 11am, 5pm, 11pm) 2. All warnings issued on Tuesday remain in effect.  Impacts will start to arrive early on Thursday, but the heaviest wind and highest impacts will be later on Thursday afternoon and evening.  If the forecast track verifies, sustained hurricane-force winds will mostly remain offshore. It's certainly possible coastal areas could see gusts up to hurricane force though. Inland areas will see lower impacts but still expect tropical storm force winds. Hurricane warnings remain in effect for coastal Pasco, Hernando, and Citrus counties in our viewing area as they are right on the fringe of the stronger winds. 3. Power outages are likely Thursday afternoon and evening. Again, most will occur along the coast and in our Northern counties where the winds will be stronger. 4. If this track holds true, this is an extremely dangerous situation for folks around Tallahassee. NHC has winds of around 120 mph at landfall. The core area of extreme winds is usually only about 5 to 10 miles wide. So, the exact spot of landfall is paramount in determining who sees the worst of the winds. If you are planning on evacuating, please do so by this afternoon. It will be too late on Thursday. Students at Florida State University and Florida A&M University should begin feeling the effects of the winds on Thursday morning but the strongest winds will occur near landfall which is forecast to be around 11pm on Thursday night. (Give or take).  There is absolutely still a possibility the track changes before landfall. As Floridians, we've seen it over and over again. So just because the "line" goes one place, if you live in the "cone of uncertainty", don't let your guard down.  Keep checking in for the latest. 5. On the plus side, the storm will be moving quickly so freshwater flooding (rainfall) will be on the low side because there won't be as much time to see higher totals. Of course, many areas locally are still saturated so it doesn't take as much to flood. 6. There will also be a tornado threat on the right side of the storm. Most tropical storms and hurricanes spawn tornadoes, although typically they are small and short-lived. They shouldn't be high on the list of things that you're worrying about today. 7. Surge is probably the biggest concern for the Bay Area. If you flooded in Idalia or ETA, you'll probably flood with Helene. I have listed the forecast totals from the NHC. It's important to remember these numbers represent the "most reasonable worst-case scenario" for any given spot. That definition is right from the NHC playbook. In this case, I agree with giving worst-case scenarios. It lets people know how bad it could be and then they can decide the best course of action to help keep their families safe.  Words to live by: "Hide from the wind, run from the water". 8.  Our team will be updating 24/7 to do our best to answer all your questions. No, we don't know if your flights are canceled, Disney is open, if your cruise will be impacted, or if you can walk your dog at 4:34 pm on Thursday, but we can pretty much handle anything else you've got. Of course with 1000s of messages, it's better to read our posts...they usually cover it all. It's just impossible to keep up with all the direct messages we get when a storm comes this way. 9. Bottom line, if you live in the Bay Area, do not get lulled into a false sense of security that this storm will stay way to our West. It's a very LARGE storm and impacts will be felt far and wide. Yes, the current track keeps the worst of this storm to our West, but we've seen hurricanes throw us curves before. Let's not let our guard down this far into the game. Once it passes your East/West line of latitude, you can stop worrying of last minute changes. Again, the worst arrives on Thursday afternoon for our area (Thursday night for landfall in the Panhandle) and the surge will be highest between 6pm and 3am Thursday night/Friday morning. 10.  Sorry for the length of this post.  I can get pretty wordy.  Tonight at 7pm, we will have an hour-long DPL (Denis Phillips LIVE). Not only will it run on Facebook and streaming, but it will also run on our broadcast Channel 28 as well. Hope to see you then. Be safe. Be prepared.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Pinellas, Hillsborough and Manatee Counties have ordered a MANDATORY evacuation for Flood Zone A and all mobile homes.

author: Denis Phillips
text: For folks asking what flood zone you're in, here you go. https://www.floridadisaster.org/knowyourzone/

author: Denis Phillips
text: Helene is now a hurricane. Update to follow.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11am update: Helene is now a hurricane moving NNW at 10mph, expected to make landfall Thursday evening in the FL panhandle as a major hurricane.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Ok, now I can answer it. Yes, your flight is cancelled. (Still not sure if you can walk your dog at 4:23 on Thursday afternoon or if Disney is open)

author: Denis Phillips
text: From Florida State University Take this storm seriously if you live in Tallahassee.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Here's a list of current cancelations.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Well, this isn't good. The NHC has bumped up Storm Surge predictions to 12 to 18 feet from the Big Bend area to Citrus County. This is a surge that will rival Ian. If you live in this area and have been told to do so, please evacuate NOW. Anclote North has now been bumped up to 8 to 12 feet.That is coastal Pasco and Hernando counties. Again, evacuate if told to do so. Let me be clear. These evacuations are only for SURGE. They aren't for wind. They only pertain to people who live on the water with access to the Gulf or Bay.

author: Denis Phillips
text: I just don't think this can be posted too many times. For those prepping for Helene, hopefully this helps. Trying to prepare students riding it out at Florida State University and Florida A&M University. Any change in track East could also impact University of Florida although that is NOT in the University of South Florida students will be on the fringe of the worst of the storm. Hurricane Prep List: Here's a list I've compiled over the years. Our family has found it helpful. 1. Charge any device that provides light. Laptops, tablets, cameras, video cameras, and old phones. Old cell phones can still used for dialing 911. Charge external battery backups. ( Glow sticks also provide light for a few hours. Stand them upright in a glass or jar to make a lantern.) 2. Wash all trash cans, big and small, and fill them with water for flushing toilets. Line outdoor trash cans with trash bags, fill them with water, and store them in the garage. Add bleach to sterilize. 3. Fill every tub and sink with water. Cover sinks with Saran Wrap to keep it from collecting dust. Fill the washing machine and leave the lid up to store water. 4. Fill old empty water bottles and other containers with water and keep them near sinks for washing hands. 5. Fill every Tupperware with water and store in the freezer. These will help keep food cold longer and serve as a backup water supply. 6. Fill drinking cups with water and cover with Saran Wrap. Store as many as possible in the fridge. The rest you can store on the counter and use first before any water bottles are opened. Ice is impossible to find after the storm. 7. Reserve fridge space for storing tap water and keep the sealed water bottles on the counter. 8. Cook any meats in advance and other perishable foods. You can freeze cooked food. Hard boil eggs for snacks for first day without power. 9. Be well hydrated before the storm hits and avoid salty foods that make you dehydrated. 10. Wash all dirty clothes and bed sheets. Anything dirty will smell without the A/C, you may need the items, and with no A/C, you'll be sweating a lot. You're going to want clean sheets. 11. Toss out any expired food, clean cat litter boxes, and empty all trash cans in the house, including bathrooms. Remove anything that will cause an odor when the A/C is off. If you don't have a trash day pickup before the storm, find a dumpster. 12. Bring in any yard decor, secure anything that will fly around, secure gates, bring in hoses, potted plants, etc. Bring in patio furniture and grills. 13. Clean your environment so you have clear, easy escape routes. Even if that means temporarily moving furniture to one area. 14. Scrub all bathrooms so you are starting with a clean odor-free environment. Store water-filled trash cans next to each toilet for flushing. 15. Place everything you own that is important and necessary in a backpack or small file box that is easy to grab. Include your wallet with ID, phone, hand sanitizer, snacks, etc. Get plastic sleeves for important documents. 16. Make sure you have cash on hand. 17. Stock up on pet food and fill up bowls of water for pets. 18. Refill any medications. Most insurance companies allow for 2 emergency refills per year. 19. Fill your propane tanks. You can heat soup cans, boil water, make coffee, and other stuff besides just grilling meat. Get an extra, if possible. 20. Drop your A/C in advance and lower temperatures in your fridges. 21. Gather all candles, flashlights, lighters, matches, batteries, and other items and keep them accessible. 22. Clean all counters in advance. Start with a clean surface.
