Remember you'll need clean water for brushing your teeth, washing yourself, and cleaning your hands. 27. Put a small suitcase in your car in case you decide to evacuate. Also, put at least one jug of water in your car. It will still be there if you don't evacuate!  Remember to pack for pets as well. 28. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you! 30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm. 31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out. 32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates.   You need water to wash dishes.... 33 . Also if you run out of water tap your hot water heater it can have up to 30 gallons stored in there. 34 . Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer. 35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down! If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!! I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!! Rule #7  And for those asking, that means "Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out..we're fine".  Whatever comes our way, we've got this as long as we stay informed and prepared.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Helene 5pm Update: Not good. Winds are now up to 85 mph and is expected to become a Category 4 hurricane before landfall in the Big Bend/Panhandle area. For the Bay Area, we do NOT expect sustained hurricane force winds but we will see some gusts nearly that high later on Thursday. Surge is still our biggest concern with water rise after the storm passes us. Again, we are not talking about rainfall flooding. Surge is water pushed ashore from the Gulf. If you don't live on or near the water, you won't see issues from the surge. Updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Special DPL starts at 7pm. LIVE on Facebook as well as on our broadcast channel. We've hit the big time, baby. Wendy Ryan will be fielding your questions and making sure you know how to tape your windows. (True story)  Hope you stop by and say HI. In all seriousness, we're here for you 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Helene to become Cat 4 No way to candy-coat it, this is gonna be bad for the panhandle and the Big Bend Area. DPL is broadcasting LIVE tonight on ABC Channel 11 and our streaming platforms. Stop by and say hi. Bring your questions. That's right now on DPL

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Helene Update: 1. First and foremost, I am forecasting sustained hurricane-force winds to remain OFFSHORE of the Bay Area as Helene passes by to the West.  Sure, if the track changes, so would this forecast, but at this point, that's what I'm going with.  I'm forecasting the eye will be about 115 miles West of Tampa and hurricane-force winds will probably extend out about 75 miles. 2. Unfortunately, that won't be the case for the panhandle. Helene is expected to be Cat 4 at landfall on Thursday evening.  A surge of 15 to 20 feet. People in Tallahassee need to rush to completion of their preps. I have posted our family hurricane kit list to help out those waiting until the last minute to prepare. Students at Florida State University and Florida A&M University will see the greatest impacts. University of Florida students will see impacts, but I don't see sustained hurricane-force winds for them. University of South Florida and University of Central Florida will see tropical storm force winds but significant damage is not expected from the wind. The University of Tampa could see some surge issues in some of the student housing. 3. Timing is late afternoon and evening for wind and power outages for the Bay Area. Tropical storm force winds should begin late morning and continue through the evening. 4. Surge levels are the biggest threat. Evacuations have already been issued. Please heed the warnings if you live in Zone A flood areas. If you flooded in Idalia, you will likely see more water with Helene.  The surge will be worst late evening on Thursday. 5. The bad weather will be over by Friday morning. Things should improve quickly. 6. As always, our team will update 24/7 to help keep you and your family safe. We take our responsibilities seriously to you and the community.  We'll be there for you right through the storm.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm Hurricane Helene Update: Helene still has winds of 85 mph. The NHC forecasts Helene will have Cat 4 winds of 130 mph af landfall in the Big Bend area Thursday evening. No changes to our forecast. Sustained hurricane winds are not expected in our area. Surge will be a major issue for our coastal areas. As always, we'll update overnight and 24/7 until the threat has ended. The latest at 11 on ABC Action News.

author: Denis Phillips

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tropical storm Isaac has formed in the Atlantic. Moving away from the United States and will pose no threat to us.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Sct'd rain and some thunderstorms in these outer rain bands of Helene may produce isolated tornadoes at any time today. The severe threat is level 2 out of 5. We'll be tracking these throughout the afternoon. Tornadoes from hurricanes are usually weak and very short-lived.

author: Denis Phillips
text: HELENE | 5 AM update is in. No major changes in the track. Winds are up to 90 mph and pressure continues to drop. NHC expects Helene to be a major hurricane in the next 12 hours.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Watch is in effect for our area. Please stay tuned to your ABC Action News station for the latest update.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A tornado watch has been issued for our entire area until 8 PM. Heavy cells of rain with isolated tornadoes are possible throughout the afternoon. These tornadoes usually are short-lived.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Warning has been issued for parts of DeSoto County. Download the ABC Action News App for the latest information.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Warning has been issued for parts of Hardee County. Download the ABC Action News App for the latest information.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Warning has been issued for parts of Manatee County. Download the ABC Action News App for the latest information.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Warning has been issued for our area. Stay tuned for further updates on your ABC Action News Station.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Warning has been issued for parts of DeSoto, Hardee, Manatee until Sep 26 7:30AM. Download the ABC Action News App for the latest weather information.

author: Denis Phillips
text: HELENE | 8 am update is in. Winds now 100 mph, pressure down another 6 mb. Helene is strengthening quickly based on that pressure drop. NNE motion. No change in track with this update.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Winds are still pretty reasonable. Outer rain bands will bring higher gusts at times today, but the highest winds are still expected along the coast this afternoon and evening. Gusts between 50-60mph will be possible at that time.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Warning has been issued for parts of Highlands County. Download the ABC Action News App for the latest information.

author: Denis Phillips
text: A Tornado Warning has been issued for our area. Stay tuned for further updates on your ABC Action News Station.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Helene Update and Thoughts: 1. Helene now has winds of 100 mph. It is getting stronger and that will continue through the day. The storm is currently located around 300 miles SW of Tampa. Hurricane winds extend about 70 miles out from the center. 2. As we've been saying for a couple of days, I DO NOT believe we will see sustained hurricane force winds in the Bay Area. Gusts of 55-65 along the coast and 40-50 mph inland. The threat of water rise along the coast is BY FAR the biggest concern for the Bay Area. If you don't live on, or near, the water, the surge won't bother you. 3.  We often turn our forecasting to "nowcasting" as a storm approaches. I usually say we throw the models out the window and look out the window. With that said, it appears the NHC track might be a bit too far West. If that's the case, the track could spare the worst for Tallahassee. Remember, the extreme winds will only be about 5-7 miles away from the center.  If Helene ends up 30 miles east of Tally, the damage would be much lower for them.  It wouldn't surprise me if this track ends up very "Idalia-like". We'll get a new NHC track at 11am.  Watch for that change. 4. At landfall, intensity will probably be somewhere between 110 and 120 mph. 5. Mandatory Evac orders are in effect for Zone A and Mobile Homes in many counties.  You don't have to go very far inland to stay safe. It's just getting out of the way from the water. As always, "Hide from the wind, and run from the water". 6. We are streaming LIVE and will continue to do so all day and evening. The strongest winds and the worst of the surge will be across our Northern counties.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Important Update! In case you missed it in previous post, I would expect the NHC to nudge the track a bit to the East at 11am. If the track goes 20 miles (or more) to the East, the worst winds will miss Tally. Honestly,  I think those chances are quite likely. This is starting to look very "Idalia-like.  This would also increase the winds somewhat for University of Florida  students, but the worst of the winds would still stay NW of Gainesville  .

author: Denis Phillips
text: There may have been a brief tornado in Polk County around Waverly/Dundee around 9:15 am. Anyone have power loss or strong winds?

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11am update: Helene has strengthened to 105mph winds. The center line of the track has shifted slightly to the east. The forecast has not changed for the Tampa area as a result of this update.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Helene midday update: 1. As expected,  a little nudge to the East. This doesn't change the impacts to the Bay Area weather. Helene will make landfall in the Big Bend this evening. 2  Surge has been,  and continues to be the biggest threat to our weather. Sustained hurricane winds will stay West of our area. Hurricane winds extend 60 miles out. The eye will be at least a 100 miles West of our coastline. We will see winds of 40 to 60 along the coast late this afternoon and this evening. Less inland. A bit higher Citrus County. 3. Remember,  the highest winds are in a VERY SMALL area within 10 miles of the eye. At this point,  it looks to me like those winds will be to the East of Tallahassee. 4. I completely understand how stressful the appearance of last minute changes can be. I'm getting lots of questions about impacts for University of Florida if the track nudges East. The impacts haven't changed. The strongest winds will be NW of Gainesville. 5. This is not Ian. This storm will not take a drastic hook into the Bay Area. A subtle bump East would take this a bit farther away from Tallahassee,  but not really impact the Tampa Bay Area any differently. If there were any noteworthy changes, I promise I'd  let you know. Other than what I just mentioned,  there haven't been. 6. Our team has you covered.  Keep it right here for any updates and changes.

author: Denis Phillips
text: As expected, wind gusts are picking up and will get stronger through the evening. The biggest threat remains storm surge flooding through tonight. No change to our forecast based on Helene's current track.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 2 PM | Hurricane Helene's winds are up to 110 mph. Category 3 winds begin at 111 mph. Next full update from the NHC is at 5 p.m. Here in Tampa Bay, the strongest winds begin over the next few hours through midnight.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Winds now 110 mph. For those insisting the track is changing, it isn't. Helene will still stay far off enough offshore that sustained hurricane force winds (74+ for sixty seconds) will NOT occur in our area. Surge will become a large issue this evening along the coast. Most of our area will see winds gusting between 45 and 60 mph along the coast. Wind will be much less inland. NHC track is still a bit West of models. In my opinion, landfall will end up being in Taylor county late tonight. Updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: JUST IN | Hurricane Hunters in the eye of Helene report that winds are now up to 120 mph, making Helene a Category 3. This was forecast, and does not change our impacts here in Tampa Bay. Updates on ABC Action News all day.

author: Denis Phillips
text: See that lightning on the North side of the eye? That's a tell-tale sign the storm continues to strengthen. New update comes out at 5pm. No real change of track is expected. Could see Cat 4 before landfall in the Big Bend area. Please don't underestimate the surge potential if you live in a Flood Zone A. Hide from the wind, run from the water.

author: Denis Phillips
text: While a lot of this system has been rain free for us in Tampa Bay, that is changing. There is a very strong band with individual supercells moving south to north. These cells can produce tornadoes at any time. We're on ABC all night with the latest.

author: Denis Phillips

author: Denis Phillips
text: It's gonna be a long night for people on the coast. Surge levels could very well reach record numbers. Timing? Worst 10pm to 2am. Improving quickly tomorrow. Lost power? NEVER use a generator indoors or in a garage. If you lose power, you can watch our coverage on our streaming app. Be safe. We'll be here updating you all night long.

author: Denis Phillips
text: HELENE | A Tornado Watch is now in effect until 6 a.m. Friday. Bands will continue to work their way through the area from Helene as it pulls north into Georgia. Isolated tornadoes are possible.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Helene will make landfall in the Big Bend area in a few hours. Winds are 130 mph and a storm surge of 10-15' are possible at landfall...possibly up to 20 feet in a few spots. The Bay Area has not, and will not experience sustained hurricane force winds, but unfortunately, surge will continue to be a threat until around 2am. Please stay safe overnight.

author: Denis Phillips
text: HELENE | The 9 p.m. update is not a good one for our friends in the Big Bend. Winds are now up to 140 mph as the storm is on approach to the coast. Landfall is expected in the next 1-3 hours.

author: Denis Phillips
text: This is one of the worst weather warnings that can be issued. An "Extreme Wind Warning" has been issued for the Big Bend. It does NOT include downtown Tallahassee. At this point, the worst of the winds could very well stay JUST to the East of Tally. That's not to say they won't see extremely strong winds, but the worst could stay just a few miles out of town. Let's hope so. Updates 24/7 Florida State University Florida A&M University University of Florida

author: Denis Phillips
text: And to make matters worse, look at that spin in the eyewall. Landfall will be within a hour. Where it occurs, they will see 150+ wind gusts. Again, VERY close to Tallahassee. It's literally a matter of a few miles whether or not they see the Western Eyewall.

author: Denis Phillips
text: HELENE | The 10 p.m. update has winds at 140 mph. Landfall is expected over the next hour just southeast of Tallahassee between St Marks and Perry.

author: Denis Phillips

The 5-8' forecast from the NHC certainly verified, with many people saying they've never seen anything like this before. Some of these records broken from Helene were just set last year by Idalia, and some of these numbers come close to doubling Idalia's surge. More data will come in over the next few days and we'll share it as soon as we get it.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 2am Helene Update: Winds down to 90 mph as it moves through Georgia. The faster it moves away, the quicker our water will recede. Unfortunately, it's not NEARLY quick enough. Hope you're hanging in there. The pictures and video of flooding along the coast is gut wrenching.

The signals are much weaker with this one than what we had with Helene. I'm sure as you're scrolling, you'll see some hype about this one. Just keep on scrolling. Nothing to see here. If things change next week, we'll let you know.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
2am NHC update:
1. Up to 80% now. Truthfully,  I don't see how this doesn't impact Florida midweek. (Wednesday)
2. It's a very unusual track, but a front draped across the Northern Gulf will pretty much steer what should be Milton into our state on Wednesday.  
3. Still a bit too early on details. Track right now looks between the Bay Area and South Florida. Models will flip flop a lot the next 36 hours. 
4. Intensity ranges from a depression to a strong hurricane.  Most models are showing a Tropical Storm or Cat 1 hurricane at landfall. The GFS is the outlier showing a stronger storm. We should know by later on Sunday. 
5. I would expect the NHC to issue a PTC on Sunday.  That would have an official track.
6. Before this arrives, the blob we spoke of for days will spread rain into our area Sunday through Tuesday.  Heaviest will be South of I-4.
7. This potential storm is gonna be tricky...at least pinpointing landfall will be. Expect the unexpected and be prepared to react accordingly. Yes, that COULD mean evacuating coastal areas again. That is not out of the realm of possibilities on Tuesday for part of the state. 
8. Our team will be there for you 24/7. (Considering I'm updating at 2:07 am, I think it speaks for itself) We know you need accurate, helpful information minus the hype. That's always our goal...to be there when you need us. (And even when you're sleeping) #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: Expect a mostly sunny start today with increasing clouds later this afternoon. Highs will top out around 90 with heat indices around 100. Rain chances are low at 20%, but we bump up the rain chance to 70% by Sunday evening.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Saturday Morning Update:
1. Up to 90% as of 8am. No real changes overnight. Models continue to take the remnants of TD 11 from the Pacific and develop it as it moves across the Gulf. Next update will be at 2pm, unless NHC deems this a depression sooner.  That seems unlikely.
2. It's very likely a tropical (or subtropical) system develops and heads East toward Florida. Current thinking is landfall would be Wednesday. Intensity still could be anywhere from a depression to a hurricane.  Both the GFS and Euro are leaning more toward Tropical Storm or Hurricane strength. 
3. Unlike Helene, there is far less certainly on track. Models show anywhere from the Big Bend to the Keys. Once the system organizes in the next 24-36 hours , a consensus will develop.  I suspect the NHC would issue a PTC advisory on Sunday which would then telease a track. Until that point, don't get caught up on individual model runs and intensities posted online. Ensembles give you a better idea of range of location of possible landfalls.
4. Here's the bottom line. Everyone on the West coast of Florida should use the time you have this weekend to prepare for a POSSIBLE storm. Unfortunately,  after Helene, that's a huge challenge for many of us. IF this develops,  it will be a much smaller storm in size compared to Helene. Still, be prepared just in case. In addition,  wherever this goes, it will cut across the state instead of going up the coast. That means a much smaller area of the coast would be impacted. 
5. Parents of kids at Florida State University, Florida A&M University, University of Florida, University of South Florida, University of Central Florida, Florida Gulf Coast University University of Miami, The University of Tampa and many others are messaging me about potential impacts. I promise to keep you updated as we get more information.  There's still a ways to go before we know how this plays out.
6. Our team will update 24/7. We do our best to offer helpful factual information without the hype. The last thing you need is fear mongering right now. There's already enough nervousness to go around. Let's see how this plays out this weekend. Day by day. Use this weekend to prepare for any POTENTIAL midweek impacts.  ##FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: TROPICS | The NHC now has development chances for our next system at 70% in the next 2 days. 90% overall. Expect a numbered or named system by Monday in the Gulf, with a possible track from the NHC late Sunday or early Monday. We'll keep you posted as the updates come in!

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tropical Depression 14 has developed in the SW Gulf of Mexico. The first track from the NHC brings it to Florida's West Coast Wednesday as a strong Category 2 hurricane. More information to come throughout the day.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Saturday afternoon Update:
1. The ENTIRE west coast of Florida is in the projected path of TD#14. Reasonably though, the highest likelihood of landfall is between Hernando County and South Florida. 
2. This is an extremely unusual track. Instead of coming in from the coast, Milton will come in from the West. That makes exact point of landfall extremely important.  Areas just to the SOUTH of landfall will see the worst of the surge.  Areas North will see an offshore flow and the water will be pushed away.
3. Same with wind. Remember the highest winds with any storm are usually about 5 to 10 miles from the eye. Outside of that zone, the winds are much lower. Winds will pick up later Tuesday and landfall will be Wednesday. 
4. Models show a track pretty much from the Bay Area to Naples. Anyone in that area could see landfall. Right now, the NHC says Cat 2 on the fringe of 3.  Scary numbers, but again, those numbers will only be in an area around 10 miles from where the eye makes landfall. Winds will be much lower outside the point of landfall
5. Now is the time to prepare. We have 4 days to do what you need to do to keep you and your family safe. The margin of error in this forecast is about 150 miles, but the reality is, someone on the West coast is gonna see this make landfall.  Don't underestimate it. Impacts outside that 10 mile area will probably be 75-85 mph winds about 35 miles from the center. 55-75 outside of that. Intensity isn't the big question, exact track is.
6. Inland areas will also see impact as Milton will cut right across the state. I probably don't need to say this,  but if you find yourself in an evacuation zone, evacuate if told to do so. The surge will cover a much smaller area than Helene, but could be higher.
7. Odds are, this track will have some changes. It usually does. But until that point,  prepare as you would for a landfalling hurricane near your area. 
8. At this point, students at Florida State University Florida A&M University and University of Florida are at lower risk than University of South Florida The University of Tampa Florida Gulf Coast University and University of Central Florida  We'll have a better grasp on track tomorrow.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
For those wanting a great hurricane list, here ya go. Please share! Here's what my family does. Hopefully, we won't need all this but better safe than sorry. 
1. Charge any device that provides light. Laptops, tablets, cameras, video cameras, and old phones. Old cell phones can still used for dialing 911. Charge external battery backups. ( Glow sticks also provide light for a few hours. Stand them upright in a glass or jar to make a lantern.)
2. Wash all trash cans, big and small, and fill them with water for flushing toilets. Line outdoor trash cans with trash bags, fill them with water and store them in the garage. Add bleach to sterilize. 
3. Fill every tub and sink with water. Cover sinks with Saran Wrap to keep it from collecting dust. Fill the washing machine and leave the lid up to store water.
4. Fill old empty water bottles and other containers with water and keep them near sinks for washing hands. 
5. Fill every Tupperware with water and store it in the freezer. These will help keep food cold longer and serve as a backup water supply. 
6. Fill drinking cups with water and cover with Saran Wrap. Store as many as possible in the fridge. You can store the rest on the counter and use it before opening any water bottles. Ice is impossible to find after the storm.
7. Reserve fridge space for storing tap water and keep the sealed water bottles on the counter. 
8. Cook any meats in advance and other perishable foods. You can freeze cooked food. Hard boil eggs for snacks for the first day without power. 
9. Be well hydrated before the storm hits and avoid salty foods that make you dehydrated. 
10. Wash all dirty clothes and bed sheets. Anything dirty will smell without the A/C, you may need the items, and with no A/C, you'll be sweating a lot. You're going to want clean sheets. 
11. Toss out any expired food, clean cat litter boxes, and empty all trash cans in the house, including bathrooms. Remove anything that will cause an odor when the A/C is off. If you don't have a trash day pickup before the storm, find a dumpster. 
12. Bring in any yard decor, secure anything that will fly around, secure gates, bring in hoses, potted plants, etc. Bring in patio furniture and grills. 
13. Clean your environment so you have clear, easy escape routes. Even if that means temporarily moving furniture to one area. 
14. Scrub all bathrooms so you are starting with a clean odor-free environment. Store water-filled trash cans next to each toilet for flushing. 
15. Place everything you own that is important and necessary in a backpack or small file box that is easy to grab. Include your wallet with ID, phone, hand sanitizer, snacks, etc. Get plastic sleeves for important documents.
16. Make sure you have cash on hand.
17. Stock up on pet food and fill up bowls of water for pets. 
18. Refill any medications. Most insurance companies allow for 2 emergency refills per year.
19. Fill your propane tanks. You can heat soup cans, boil water, make coffee, and other stuff besides just grilling meat. Get an extra, if possible.
20. Drop your A/C in advance and lower temperatures in your fridges. 
21. Gather all candles, flashlights, lighters, matches, batteries, and other items and keep them accessible. 
22. Clean all counters in advance. Start with a clean surface. Buy Clorox Wipes for cleaning when there is no power. Mop your floors and vacuum. If power is out for 10 days, you'll have to live in the mess you started with. 
23. Pick your emergency safe place such as a closet under the stairs. Store the items you'll need in that location for the brunt of the storm. Make a hand fan for when the power is out. 
24. Shower just before the storm is scheduled to hit. 
25. Keep baby wipes next to each toilet. Don't flush them. It's not the time to risk clogging your toilet!
26. Run your dishwasher, don't risk having dirty smelly dishes and you need every container for water! Remember you'll need clean water for brushing your teeth, washing yourself, and cleaning your hands. 
27. Put a small suitcase in your car in case you decide to evacuate. Also, put at least one jug of water in your car. It will still be there if you don't evacuate!  Remember to pack for pets as well. 
28. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 
29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you!
30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm.
31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out.
32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates. 👍  You need water to wash dishes....👎
33 . Also if you run out of water tap your hot water heater it can have up to 30 gallons stored in there.
34 . Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer.
35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down!💩
If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!!
I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!!
Rule #7  And for those asking, that means "Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out..we're fine".  Whatever comes our way, we've got this as long as we stay informed and prepared.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Update: We now have Tropical Storm Milton with winds of 40 mph. Remember, the NHC tracks update every 6 hours. (5pm, 11pm, 5am and 11am)  One other important reminder. The cone you see is not manipulated or moved by the NHC based on expected track of the storm. It has NOTHING to do with the actual storm forecast. It is merely the average margin of error on a track by the NHC over the past 5 years. If the average error is 150 miles on Day 5 (It is), that's how far the cone extends from the forecast center of the storm. Most models are showing if there were to be a change in track, it would be South, not North. As always, we'll be updating 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Milton Notes:
1. Landfall will occur in Florida. Timing is Wednesday.  No changes are expected. 
2. A strong cold front is going to head South early next week. Our hope is, it sends dry air and shear to limit development.  Problem is, by then,  it will probably already be a hurricane.  Still, limiting max winds would be a huge help. It's a very close call in timing. It's pretty simple. The models showing a weaker storm are impacted by dry air and shear. The models that show a stronger storm aren't. 
3. Surge will occur just to the South of landfall. If you're North,  winds will blow offshore and you won't see surge. So in this case, exact track is massive. 10 miles will make a big difference.  No way this early to be that precise,  so it's a waiting game. 
4. In addition, strongest winds will be within 10-15 miles of the eye. So unlike Helene which covered a large area, Milton will be more landfall specific with impacts. It's a big coastline. Unfortunately,  someone will see this, but the overall threat of any particular area seeing landfall this early is low. The track will shift. It always does. The margin or error this far out is 150 miles. 
5. We'll be updating 24/7. Hopefully you'll find the information useful. Our goal is always to give you more than you need than not enough. The last thing we ever want to hear is "I didn't know". Now is the time to prepare. It's cliche but it's true. "Prepare for the worst, hope for the best." #FloridaStrong
Florida State University Florida A&M University University of Florida University of South Florida University of Central Florida The University of Tampa Florida Gulf Coast University University of Miami

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tropical Storm Milton 5pm Track:  Obviously, not a great track for the Bay Area. Remember, this far out, the average margin of error is 150 miles. So, the entire West coast of Florida is still in play for landfall. Latest track has bumped up intensity 5 mph making Milton a Cat 3 hurricane at landfall. Surge and strongest winds will be along and just SOUTH of where the eye makes landfall. There will be a swath of 5-10 miles that will see the extreme impacts. You've got to prepare as if where you live will be in that zone. Obviously, the odds are higher that it will be elsewhere considering the large margin of error this far out, but DO NOT underestimate or minimize potential threat with this system. We will update 24/7 and our 1st DPL will stream at 7pm on Facebook tonight and our ABC Action News Streaming Channel.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tonight at 7pm. I'll be here to give you the latest on Milton.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Milton to become a hurricane with Florida landfall
Milton is expected to become a hurricane, and possibly a major hurricane by landfall on Wednesday. However, the forecast is NOT cut and dry. Expect flip-flops along the way.  Let's chat about it. That's right now on DPL

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Evening Milton Update:
1. As expected, models are jumping around quite a bit. Dry air and a front to our North early next week will have a big impact on Milton. It's possible that dry air and shear could start to limit intensity or even weaken Milton before landfall. (Or not). We simply won't know until Monday. Expect models to continue flip flopping until then.
2. Extremely winds and surge will likely be limited to about 10-15 miles near and South of the eye. Landfall point is everything.  The worst wind and surge will be in that zone. 
3. Hide from the wind, run from the water. You have THREE days to prepare.  Whether you decide to evacuate or hunker down, you have time. Look for the prep list I posted earlier today. 
4. I have no doubt the entire area will be under a Hurricane Watch on Sunday and the coast will be under a Storm Surge Watch
5. Milton hits Wednesday.  Regardless of where along the West coast landfall occurs, it will be Wednesday. Tropical Storm force winds (40+) arrive Wednesday morning. 
6. Rainfall of 4-10" with locally heavier areas through midweek. 
7. Unlike Helene, hurricane winds WILL impact our inland counties. Weaker, of course, by the time it makes the East coast.
8. Lastly, we will continue to update for parents of students at our colleges and universities. Florida State University Florida A&M University University of Florida University of South Florida University of Central Florida The University of Tampa Florida Gulf Coast University University of Miami students could see impacts as well. Keep it right here for updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Even before Milton approaches the west coast of Florida on Wednesday, our area will see quite a bit of rain, some of which is already moving in. Plenty of it will fall at times Sun-Tue leaving us with 4"+ in some spots.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm Tropical Storm Milton Update:  New track is in. Milton is a bit stronger tonight.  No real changes to the thinking or track, though. . Milton is a small storm, which means it can strengthen (or weaken) quicker. NHC still has it as a Cat 3 as it makes landfall. Again, the average margin of error in track this far out is 150 miles. The pretty much includes most of the West coast of Florida. Hurricane Watches and Storm Surge watches will be issued for our area on Sunday. Updates 24/7 as always including overnight.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Overnight Milton Update:
1. No significant changes. 0z models still clustering between Hernando and Saraaota counties.  Hurricane and Storm Surge Watches will be issued on Sunday for our area. The Hurricane Watch will include inland areas. 
2. Dry air and shear is the wild card before landfall.  Several models (including the Euro) tries to get Milton to "eat" some of this and level off intensity. Others don't. That won't happen (if it does) until Tuesday. 
3. Here's the bottom line. A dangerous hurricane will approach the West coast of Florida Wednesday.  Do NOT take this srorm lightly. You have 3 days to prepare. 
4. In most cases, people who are not impacted by surge will not need to evacuate.  This will be a small(ish) storm where extreme winds and surge will likely be only about 10 miles out from the center. Hurricane force winds will probably extend about 45 or 50 miles out by landfall.  The surge will be highest just to the South of landfall. Levels will probably be higher than Helene in this zone. But again, the zone will be much smaller than Helene was.
5. I will post our prep list again on Sunday. It can be really helpful to those riding it out.
6. This track is not set in stone. As Floridians, we see how tracks can change quickly. Be prepared for those last minute changes. Those preps can be life saving. Power outages could easily last for days, if not weeks where landfall occurs.  Know what you're in for if you decide to ride out the storm. As always,  we'll update 24/7 in hopes of keeping you storm ready. #FloridaStrong 
Florida State University Florida A&M University University of Florida University of Central Florida Florida Gulf Coast University University of Miami University of South Florida The University of Tampa students will be impacted as well. Check with your schools to see how they are suggesting students handle preoa or evacuations.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 06z NHC spaghetti models coming in. Not much change. I suspect the NHC keeps the track about the same with little intensity change in the next advisory. That comes out at 5am. Milton still a rather small(ish) storm in terms of size. Smaller storms can intensify (or weaken) quicker if conditions are less than ideal. I don't expect much change on Sunday other than watches issued for the area. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: MILTON 5 AM UPDATE | The newest track is in. No significant changes. Milton is slowly organizing with winds now at 50 mph. The track continues to show a landfall Wednesday along the Florida West Coast. Storm Surge Watches and Hurricane Watches are expected from the NHC later today. Updates all day today as we get them. Use today to get prepared!

author: Denis Phillips
text: HEAVY RAIN | Light rain has moved through this morning, with pockets of moderate to heavy rain expected Monday and Tuesday. Wednesday is when Milton arrives, and its rains could lead to flooding. 5 day rainfall totals could exceed 12" in spots. A Flood Watch is now in effect through Thursday morning.

author: Denis Phillips
text: MILTON | The 8 am advisory has winds up to 60 mph as it continues to organize and strengthen in the SW Gulf. Next track update comes at 11 a.m.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Sunday morning update:
1. Milton is quickly getting stronger.  Winds are now 60 mph. As i mentioned last night, smaller storms get stronger (and weaker) faster. There will be shear and dry air surrounding Milton in the hours before landfall on Wednesday.  The hope is, that could weaken the storm before landfall. But at this point, that is only a hope. There is no candy coating this. An area along the West coast is going to see a powerful and dangerous hurricane on Wednesday.  
2. Given the small size (more Charley than Helene), extreme impacts will be in a much smaller area. My thoughts are, the worst will be within 10 to 15 miles of where the eye makes landfall. We usually say "Don't focus on the white line", but in this case, exact track really matters. And that exact track still has an average margin of error of over 100 miles. 
3. As for surge, the worst will also be within 15 miles along and to the SOUTH of landfall. Areas NORTH of landfall will see more of an offshore flow and will see much less surge. We often say, "Hide from the wind,  and run from the water"  We certainly lived that with Helene. Howver,  there will likely be a similar area of about 15 miles across with extreme winds as well. You don't want any part of those winds.
4. The storm will weaken as it crosses the state, but inland areas will still see hurricane force winds. The entire area will probably be under a Hurricane Watch later tonight. 
5. Landfall will be on Wednesday. You still have 3 days to prepare. If you decide to evacuate, remember traffic will be a nightmare on Tuesday. Remember Irma? Keep that in mind in your plans. 
6. I'll be posting the Hurricane prep list our family uses. Hopefully,  it will be helpful if you decide to ride out the storm at home. 
7. Students at University of South Florida The University of Tampa Florida Gulf Coast University University of Central Florida University of Florida and all other colleges and universities in Central Florida will need to keep a very close eye on this and talk to their parents about the their safety course of action. Florida State University  and Florida A&M University are probably OK with this one based on the CURRENT track.If the track changes, so would impacts.
8. In my opinion,  this storm has a Charley vibe. A small and powerful storm. It is absolutely possible Milton can start to weaken before landfall . Many models show that happening. So, the next 24 hours we watch and prepare...and hope. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text:
For those wanting a great hurricane list, here ya go. Please share! Here's what my family does. Hopefully, we won't need all this but better safe than sorry. And remember,  shelters are also an option. They fill up quickly in a big storm and some don't allow pets. Keep that in mind if you're in harms way! 
1. Charge any device that provides light. Laptops, tablets, cameras, video cameras, and old phones. Old cell phones can still used for dialing 911. Charge external battery backups. ( Glow sticks also provide light for a few hours. Stand them upright in a glass or jar to make a lantern.)
2. Wash all trash cans, big and small, and fill them with water for flushing toilets. Line outdoor trash cans with trash bags, fill them with water and store them in the garage. Add bleach to sterilize. 
3. Fill every tub and sink with water. Cover sinks with Saran Wrap to keep it from collecting dust. Fill the washing machine and leave the lid up to store water.
4. Fill old empty water bottles and other containers with water and keep them near sinks for washing hands. 
5. Fill every Tupperware with water and store it in the freezer. These will help keep food cold longer and serve as a backup water supply. 
6. Fill drinking cups with water and cover with Saran Wrap. Store as many as possible in the fridge. You can store the rest on the counter and use it before opening any water bottles. Ice is impossible to find after the storm.
7. Reserve fridge space for storing tap water and keep the sealed water bottles on the counter. 
8. Cook any meats in advance and other perishable foods. You can freeze cooked food. Hard boil eggs for snacks for the first day without power. 
9. Be well hydrated before the storm hits and avoid salty foods that make you dehydrated. 
10. Wash all dirty clothes and bed sheets. Anything dirty will smell without the A/C, you may need the items, and with no A/C, you'll be sweating a lot. You're going to want clean sheets. 
11. Toss out any expired food, clean cat litter boxes, and empty all trash cans in the house, including bathrooms. Remove anything that will cause an odor when the A/C is off. If you don't have a trash day pickup before the storm, find a dumpster. 
12. Bring in any yard decor, secure anything that will fly around, secure gates, bring in hoses, potted plants, etc. Bring in patio furniture and grills. 
13. Clean your environment so you have clear, easy escape routes. Even if that means temporarily moving furniture to one area. 
14. Scrub all bathrooms so you are starting with a clean odor-free environment. Store water-filled trash cans next to each toilet for flushing. 
15. Place everything you own that is important and necessary in a backpack or small file box that is easy to grab. Include your wallet with ID, phone, hand sanitizer, snacks, etc. Get plastic sleeves for important documents.
16. Make sure you have cash on hand.
17. Stock up on pet food and fill up bowls of water for pets. 
18. Refill any medications. Most insurance companies allow for 2 emergency refills per year.
19. Fill your propane tanks. You can heat soup cans, boil water, make coffee, and other stuff besides just grilling meat. Get an extra, if possible.
20. Drop your A/C in advance and lower temperatures in your fridges. 
21. Gather all candles, flashlights, lighters, matches, batteries, and other items and keep them accessible. 
22. Clean all counters in advance. Start with a clean surface. Buy Clorox Wipes for cleaning when there is no power. Mop your floors and vacuum. If power is out for 10 days, you'll have to live in the mess you started with. 
23. Pick your emergency safe place such as a closet under the stairs. Store the items you'll need in that location for the brunt of the storm. Make a hand fan for when the power is out. 
24. Shower just before the storm is scheduled to hit. 
25. Keep baby wipes next to each toilet. Don't flush them. It's not the time to risk clogging your toilet!
26. Run your dishwasher, don't risk having dirty smelly dishes and you need every container for water! Remember you'll need clean water for brushing your teeth, washing yourself, and cleaning your hands. 
27. Put a small suitcase in your car in case you decide to evacuate. Also, put at least one jug of water in your car. It will still be there if you don't evacuate!  Remember to pack for pets as well. 
28. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 
29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you!
30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm.
31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out.
32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates. 👍  You need water to wash dishes....👎
33 . Also if you run out of water tap your hot water heater it can have up to 30 gallons stored in there.
34 . Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer.
35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down!💩
If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!!
I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!!
Rule #7  And for those asking, that means "Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out..we're fine".  Whatever comes our way, we've got this as long as we stay informed and prepared.

author: Denis Phillips
text: It all boils down to that trough. If it's strong enough, the track goes more South. If it's weaker,  the storm goes more North. Latest spaghetti models. NHC MIGHT nudge a bit South next advisory. They usually take "baby steps" in adjusting tracks. Please use today to prepare for what will be a powerful hurricane impacting the West coast of Florida. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: New NHC track will be out shortly. Remember,  models will flip flop for another 24 hours. Look for trends, not individual runs. As we say, "The trend is your friend". And for the folks asking where Rule #7 is, now is DEFINITELY not the time to freak out. It's the time to make educated decisions and prepare for what Mother Nature has in store for us. Our team will post 24/7 to help you get through the mayhem hurricane season brings. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: MILTON 11 AM UPDATE | Tropical Storm Milton's winds are up to 65 mph. It is expected to rapidly intensity over the next 36 hours into a Category 3 storm. Models this morning have shifted a small distance to the south, so the NHC forecast track has shifted slightly south as well. The cone remains the same width however, so anyone from Naples to Crystal River should continue to prepare should future shifts happen.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Quick Milton Update:
1. As we expected, the NHC bumped the 11am track a bit to the South. Per their advisory, "further track adjustments South may be required if this trend continues". Models will fluctuate back and forth. Ian was a hard lesson for a lot of people. If you're in the cone, you could be impacted. We are WAY too early in the process to know exactly where Milton will go. Prepare accordingly and we will adjust as we go. Prepare for the worst and hope for the best. We're Floridians, we have ALL seen how fickle hurricanes can be. 
2. Wherever landfall occurs, surge will be greater than we saw in Helene. Take it seriously,  especially from the Bay Southward.
3. Inland areas will also see hurricane force winds. I expect later tonight ALL of our area will be under a Hurricane Watch.
4. Most folks know Rule #7. Rule #2 is important too. Don't get too high, or too low with model changes. They flip flop all the time. Look for trends. If we see South trends continuing for the next 12 hours, folks South of Sarasota will really need to watch this. At this point,  nothing has changed. We ALL need to remain vigilant and ready to act. 
5. Again, with a subtle shift to the South in models, schools like Florida State University  and Florida A&M University have a lower threat. University of Florida probably as well. Anyone from central Florida Southward including The University of Tampa University of Central Florida Florida Gulf Coast University University of South Florida among any other colleges and universities in the area need to closely monitor this track. 
6. Another NHC update at 5 and DPL tonight at 7.  #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: Pinellas, Hillsborough  and Pasco County Schools are closed Monday-Wednesday.  Let the cancelations begin.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton's rapid intensification is well underway, and the storm is now a Category 1 Hurricane with winds of 80 mph. Next track update comes in around 5 p.m.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton's rapid intensification is well underway, and the storm is now a Category 1 Hurricane with winds of 80 mph. Next track update comes in around 5 p.m.
