author: Denis Phillips
text: While many models continue to trend a bit more South, the GFS continues to keep its track more North. Let me be clear. No one is "out of the woods" yet. We've seen hurricanes change track six hours before landfall. We are 3 days out. Thats a long time out to try and pinpoint landfall within 20 or 30 miles. Still, I expect NHC might bump a bit more South at 5. Keep preparing for the worst and hope for the best. We got this. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: Perfect example of why focusing on individual model runs will drive you crazy. Euro back North closer to Tampa again with latest run. 3 days out seasoned Floridians know it just goes this way. Prepare the best you can. Have a plan ready to enact. Monday is the day we'll need to make decisions on evacuations. Bottom line, we'll update 24/7 and do our best to make this excruciatingly difficult time a bit less challenging.

author: Denis Phillips
text: MILTON | Sunday 5 PM update is in. No major changes with this one as some of the models that were trending south earlier are now back north. NHC will be conservative with track changes to keep it consistent. Milton is now stronger with winds of 85 mph and will continue to get stronger through the night. It'll approach the west coast of Florida Wednesday as a major hurricane. No new watches for Florida with this update.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Hurricane Milton Update and Thoughts:
1. Hurricane Milton is rapidly intensifying. It's moving into a part of the Gulf where conditions are nearly perfect for intensification. Chances are high Milton will be a Cat 4 (or possibly Cat 5) within 24 hours.  After that, and before landfall, Milton will run into significant shear and should begin to weaken. However, even with the forecast weakening, Milton will be a powerful hurricane when it makes landfall in Florida. Not IF, WHEN it makes landfall in Florida.  The left picture is a simulation for tomorrow. The picture on the right is before landfall.  See what the 40 knot shear will do to the storm? So, what does that mean for impacts?
2. The margin of error with the NHC track this far out is about 100 miles.  Models are pretty much from just South of Tampa through Sarasota County. The GFS is still the outlier near Cedar Key. Remember, the point of landfall IS important as the worst of the surge will be along, and just to the South, of that landfall point.  While Milton is a small storm in size, it is expected to grow in size as it weakens due to the shear we spoke of earlier.
3. Hurricane Watches and Storm Surge watches will be issued either tonight at 11pm or Monday at 5am. I have little doubt evacuation orders will begin on Monday as well. The exact location of evacuations is still in doubt as models are still doing their flip-flopping as they often do. It should be noted the latest NHC track did NOT move.  As I mentioned, they take "baby steps' in any change of track so I suspect they're waiting for new model runs to determine any track changes at 11pm. 
4.  Again, there is no way to candy-coat this. A powerful hurricane WILL hit Florida on Wednesday. The worst of the wind will be in about a 10-mile stretch just to the East and South of landfall.  Hurricane winds will probably extend about 50 to 60 miles out by then. Tropical storm force winds will cover most of the state.
5. Inland areas will also get hurricane-force winds. This track takes hurricane-force winds into Polk County as well. All inland counties will also be under Hurricane Watches.
6.  If you have a generator, NEVER use it indoors or in the garage.  If you are told to evacuate, do so.  Uber may offer rides to people trying to get to shelters. Many lost their cars from Helene and need help getting there. If you are planning on going to a shelter, go early. They will fill up. Also, make sure they allow pets if you have a pet.  
7. Bottom line, not everyone will need to evacuate. Hide from the wind and run from the water are words to live by.  Hopefully by midday on Monday, we'll know where this is going and you can finish your preps and start to hunker down...or get out of dodge.
8. Lastly, local colleges and universities are handling evacuations differently. Check with your school to see what they have planned.  Florida State University Florida A&M University University of Florida University of South Florida The University of Tampa University of Central Florida Florida Gulf Coast University

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Milton is now a hurricane with Florida landfall
Milton is expected to become a major hurricane by landfall on Wednesday. However, the forecast is NOT cut and dry. Expect flip-flops along the way.  Let's chat about it. That's right now on DPL

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton has winds of 85 mph. Next track update will be at 11pm. Spaghetti models are pretty much useless right now as there is absolutely no agreement. Hopefully that changes with new recon data coming in and we'll have an answer for you by morning. I apologize we can't give you more right now. I know you depend on this information to make plans to keep your family safe. It's gonna be a long week for all of us and devastating for many. Timeline is Wednesday afternoon and evening. #Floridastrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm Hurricane Milton Update: Milton now has winds of 90 mph. The NHC predicts this will become a powerful Cat 4 hurricane on Tuesday. The track is "essentially the same" although the NHC does slow down the forward speed a bit. The slower it goes, the more time shear will have to weaken it a bit. But so there are no misunderstandings, Milton will be a powerful hurricane when it makes landfall on the West coast of Florida. We'll continue to update overnight as we get new model runs and information.

author: Denis Phillips
text: UPDATE: Milton now has winds of 90 mph. The storm is clearly going through rapid intensification and will likely be a Cat 4 hurricane within 24 hours. It is possible the storm could strengthen to Cat 5 before slowly weakening a bit before making landfall on Wednesday. The official NHC forecast still has Milton a "Major" Cat 3 hurricane upon landfall. No track change at 11 as models are still divided on where the storm is going to end up. Updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Overnight Milton update:
1. No changes to 11pm track. Unfortunately,  new spaghetti models are STILL split. I have no doubt at 5am a Hurricane Watch will be issued for our area. A Storm Surge Watch will also be needed. 
2. Milton is going through rapid Intensification.  It is expected to become a Cat 4 hurricane later in Monday or Tuesday. 
3. Winds don't really pick up until Wednesday morning. You still have 2 days to prepare. If you are in a mandatory evacuation area, you should do so. Shelters will be open with transportation if you're unable to leave. 
4. Power outages will be widespread and will likely last for days. Consider that if you're planning on riding out the storm. I posted prep ideas earlier today. Scroll down to find it if you're looking for ideas.
5.  Unfortunately,  this storm will have major impacts on many people. The extreme effects of surge and wind will be worst just to the South of the  landfall location. A surge of up to 12 feet is possible in these locations.  
6. It's gonna be a long  week. It's gonna be an awful week for a lot of folks. We're in this together. Neighbors helping neighbors is how we make it through. #FloridaStrong 
7. Local colleges and universities have plans for their students. Please contact them for details. Florida State University and Florida A&M University will probably be ok. University of Florida is on the fringe of the really heavy stuff. University of South Florida The University of Tampa University of Central Florida will all likely see hurricane force winds. Florida Gulf Coast University  could as well. Be safe everyone.

author: Denis Phillips
text: MILTON | The 5 AM track is in. No major changes here. Milton is now stronger with winds of 100 mph. Continued increase in strength is likely today. Milton will likely reach Cat 4 intensity north of the Yucatan late today or early Tuesday. NHC notes the track of the storm is highly uncertain as it approaches Florida, as is the timing of when exactly that landfall will happen. Added to this, Milton will be entering strong wind shear as it approaches the Florida west coast likely causing some weakening in the system. Despite this, Milton is still likely to be a major hurricane as it makes landfall.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | As of 5 AM Mon Oct 7, our entire area is under a hurricane watch. Hurricane conditions are likely late Wed.

author: Denis Phillips
text: NEW | Hurricane hunter aircraft find Milton stronger. Winds are now at 120 mph. Milton is a dangerous Cat 3 hurricane. A slow motion just south of east continues. No track changes with this update.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Hurricane Milton update:
1. Milton is now a major hurricane with winds of 120 mph. It will continue to strengthen today. 
2. Much of the Bay Area will experience hurricane force winds on Wednesday.  The latest NHC track is pretty much an update of the previous one. Time is running out for any significant track changes. Now is the time to finalize your hurricane preps and plans. 
3. The worst of the wind will be confined to about a ten mile radius from where landfall occurs. So exact track matters. Same with surge. Worst of the surge will occur along and just South of the point where landfall occurs. 
4. Water is always the biggest threat to safety with a hurricane.  You can hide from the wind, you can't hide from the water. If you're told to evacuate, do so.
5. Expect widespread power outages for days across a large part of the area. 
6. At this point, the worst of the wind will be between Tampa and Sarasota. The chances for a significant change in track is dropping the closer we get to landfall. 
7. This is as real as it gets. Milton will face increasing shear as it approaches the West coast of Florida. Hopefully,  this will allow some weakening as the storm approaches.  Still, chances remain high Milton will be a powerful hurricane at landfall. 
8. The Hurricane will cross the state so expect hurricane conditions across our inland counties as well. 
9. A bit of hope here. Milton is a small storm. Hurricane force winds only extend 25 miles out. The worst are only 5 to 10 miles. So most will not see the extreme winds. It all boils down to the exact track. 
10. Florida State University and Florida A&M University should be ok. Unless there is a strong shift North, University of Florida students will be on the fringe of hurricane force winds. The University of Tampa University of South Florida and University of Central Florida students will likely see hurricane force winds. Florida Gulf Coast University students may as well. I know there are many other colleges and universities. I can't post them all. I'm sure you can figure others out based on this track.
11. NOW is the time to finish your preps.  Traffic will be pretty terrible on the roads. Give yourself a lot of time to get to your destination if you're evacuating. Most folks away from the water will choose to hunker down. Use the Hurricane prep list I posted to help prepare. 
12. There is no candy coating this. It's gonna be bad for some folks. Let's try to remember we are ALL in this together.  Stress levels are high. Let's do our best to help each other before, during and after the storm. We'll need cooperation to get through this. It's time for the Indian Burial grounds to work their magic. We need it. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane hunter finds Milton stronger. Pressure is dropping fast. Winds now up to 125 mph. Milton will be a Cat 4 storm later today.

author: Denis Phillips
text: With Milton now stronger, the NHC has upped the max intensity forecast to 155 mph near the Yucatan. The track for the next 36 hours has also nudged south. This now shows it's possible for the eye to make landfall in the Yucatan before turning northeast toward Florida.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
For those wanting a great hurricane list, here ya go.  Please share with people who might need it. 
 Here's what my family does. Hopefully, we won't need all this but better safe than sorry.
 And remember,  shelters are also an option. They fill up quickly in a big storm and some don't allow pets. Keep that in mind if you're in harms way! 
1. Charge any device that provides light. Laptops, tablets, cameras, video cameras, and old phones. Old cell phones can still used for dialing 911. Charge external battery backups. ( Glow sticks also provide light for a few hours. Stand them upright in a glass or jar to make a lantern.)
2. Wash all trash cans, big and small, and fill them with water for flushing toilets. Line outdoor trash cans with trash bags, fill them with water and store them in the garage. Add bleach to sterilize. 
3. Fill every tub and sink with water. Cover sinks with Saran Wrap to keep it from collecting dust. Fill the washing machine and leave the lid up to store water.
4. Fill old empty water bottles and other containers with water and keep them near sinks for washing hands. 
5. Fill every Tupperware with water and store it in the freezer. These will help keep food cold longer and serve as a backup water supply. 
6. Fill drinking cups with water and cover with Saran Wrap. Store as many as possible in the fridge. You can store the rest on the counter and use it before opening any water bottles. Ice is impossible to find after the storm.
7. Reserve fridge space for storing tap water and keep the sealed water bottles on the counter. 
8. Cook any meats in advance and other perishable foods. You can freeze cooked food. Hard boil eggs for snacks for the first day without power. 
9. Be well hydrated before the storm hits and avoid salty foods that make you dehydrated. 
10. Wash all dirty clothes and bed sheets. Anything dirty will smell without the A/C, you may need the items, and with no A/C, you'll be sweating a lot. You're going to want clean sheets. 
11. Toss out any expired food, clean cat litter boxes, and empty all trash cans in the house, including bathrooms. Remove anything that will cause an odor when the A/C is off. If you don't have a trash day pickup before the storm, find a dumpster. 
12. Bring in any yard decor, secure anything that will fly around, secure gates, bring in hoses, potted plants, etc. Bring in patio furniture and grills. 
13. Clean your environment so you have clear, easy escape routes. Even if that means temporarily moving furniture to one area. 
14. Scrub all bathrooms so you are starting with a clean odor-free environment. Store water-filled trash cans next to each toilet for flushing. 
15. Place everything you own that is important and necessary in a backpack or small file box that is easy to grab. Include your wallet with ID, phone, hand sanitizer, snacks, etc. Get plastic sleeves for important documents.
16. Make sure you have cash on hand.
17. Stock up on pet food and fill up bowls of water for pets. 
18. Refill any medications. Most insurance companies allow for 2 emergency refills per year.
19. Fill your propane tanks. You can heat soup cans, boil water, make coffee, and other stuff besides just grilling meat. Get an extra, if possible.
20. Drop your A/C in advance and lower temperatures in your fridges. 
21. Gather all candles, flashlights, lighters, matches, batteries, and other items and keep them accessible. 
22. Clean all counters in advance. Start with a clean surface. Buy Clorox Wipes for cleaning when there is no power. Mop your floors and vacuum. If power is out for 10 days, you'll have to live in the mess you started with. 
23. Pick your emergency safe place such as a closet under the stairs. Store the items you'll need in that location for the brunt of the storm. Make a hand fan for when the power is out. 
24. Shower just before the storm is scheduled to hit. 
25. Keep baby wipes next to each toilet. Don't flush them. It's not the time to risk clogging your toilet!
26. Run your dishwasher, don't risk having dirty smelly dishes and you need every container for water! Remember you'll need clean water for brushing your teeth, washing yourself, and cleaning your hands. 
27. Put a small suitcase in your car in case you decide to evacuate. Also, put at least one jug of water in your car. It will still be there if you don't evacuate!  Remember to pack for pets as well. 
28. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 
29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you!
30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm.
31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out.
32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates. 👍  You need water to wash dishes....👎
33 . Also if you run out of water tap your hot water heater it can have up to 30 gallons stored in there.
34 . Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer.
35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down!💩
If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!!
I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!!

author: Denis Phillips
text: Based on the  Mon Oct 7 5am track, strong tropical storm winds won't begin to move in until Wednesday afternoon. The first half of Wednesday will feature some wind and probably a lot of rain but conditions likely won't get bad Wednesday until the evening.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Hunters finding "flight level"  winds at 146 knots. Milton is already 150 mph at the surface. I would expect another special NHC advisory soon.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton has rapidly intensified to a Cat 4 storm with 150 mph winds. It is still expected to weaken prior to landfall on Wednesday but make no mistake, it will still be a powerful hurricane when it impacts our area.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Impressive hi-res satellite image of Cat 4 Milton as it approaches the northern Yucatan. Pressure continues to drop and winds continue to increase.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Mandatory evacuation orders for Hillsborough county zones A & B along with all manufactured/mobile housing beginning today at 2:30pm. (I am waiting to hear if Pinellas goes mandatory) Manatee and Sarasota both mandatory A and B. Sarasota could go "C" as well.

author: Denis Phillips
text: TPA will close 9am on Tuesday.

author: Denis Phillips
text: PIE (St. Pete/Clearwater airport) will close at 12:30 on Tuesday and remain closed through Thursday.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Here is the link for latest evacuations and closures.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11am Update: Milton continues to rapidly intensify. Likely to become a Cat 5 hurricane, but still forecast to weaken prior to landfall. The new track brings the storm a bit more to the North. Honestly,  at this point, any adjustment of track 10 or 20 miles in unpredictable this far out. The message stays the same. A powerful hurricane will make landfall on the West coast of Florida late on Wednesday or early Thursday

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Milton Afternoon Update: Milton has strengthened almost as quickly as any storm in history. Only Wilma and Felix have strengthened faster. 80 knot increase in 24 hours. Unreal. Now what?
1. Shear should cause Milton to weaken as it approaches the state. Still, Cat 3 is likely when it makes landfall.
2. Where? Area between Sarasota and Hernando County appear most likely. Again, the exact point of landfall is unpredictable. Wobbles will also happen. The point of landfall and South will see the worst of it.
3. Surge values of 8-12 feet are likely at landfall near the eye. Winds near the eye will be 100-120. Hurricane force winds will probably extend about 45 miles out. 
4. Widespread power outages expected.  Never use a generator IN your home or garage. 
5. Milton will cross the state and bring hurricane conditions to inland areas including Polk County and Orlando.
6. If you are in a mandatory evacuation zone,  do so. 
7. If the storm slows down its forward speed, there IS the opportunity for additional weakening. There is always hope. But the reality is chances are high part of the Bay Area will see major hurricane impacts and a direct hit. 
8. Unfortunately, this storm is the real deal. The one we always knew was possible, but dreaded even thinking about it. So, now is the time to act. You still have 1 1/2 days. Follow evacuation warnings. Make your final preps. 
9. On a personal note, I know how scared and anxious people are. We should be. This storm is a beast. We need to work as a team to get through this. Storms can bring the best out in people. Neighbors helping neighbors. It's the only way. Our team will be here 24/7 to get you as much information as possible to help keep you and your family safe. Let's do this..TOGETHER. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton has rapidly intensified to a Category 5 storm. Wow. No other words come to mind. Well, actually they do, but I can't post them.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Lots of folks are asking about Rule #7. (Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out). Here's the deal. We ALL know how serious this storm is. Now is NOT the time to freak out. Now IS the time for action. We have work to do. Work to prepare ourselves.  Work to help our friends and neighbors. Work to comfort our children. Making important decisions is hard. Making those choices while freaking out is harder. You can do this!! We have a day and a half to get ready for Milton. Hopefully, the shear we're forecasting will knock him down a peg or 2. (I think it will) We are a tight knit community.  We WILL get through this,  TOGETHER. ❤️

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton explosively intensifies to 175mph winds.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Scary. No other way to describe it. Winds of 175 mph. Still, the forecast of weakening as it approaches our coastline remains intact. Without getting too sciency,  Milton will go through ERC's (Eyewall replacement cycles) will cause fluctuations in strength.  As the storm approaches,  it will run into 35-40 mph shear. That will weaken Milton. How much? I'd say a couple categories. Remember, this is a VERY small storm. Hurricane winds only extend about 25 miles. Yes, it will get larger,  but the worst of the wind will be in a very small area. Unfortunately, that area is likely to be in our area. And again, surge is still a huge threat. Regardless, a powerful hurricane is almost assuredly going to make landfall in our area. Don't take it lightly.
#FloridaStrong 
Florida State University University of Florida Florida A&M University University of South Florida University of Central Florida The University of Tampa Florida Gulf Coast University  Keeping local colleges and universities in the loop for parents out of state.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Pinellas County. Mandatory evacuation A, B and C.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Hurricane Warnings are now in effect for the entire Bay Area. A Storm Surge Warning is now in effect for our entire coastline. This means hurricane conditions and an 8-12 foot Storm Surge are expected within 36 hours.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 5pm Hurricane Milton Update: Winds are now an unbelievable 180 mph. Milton is one of the strongest hurricanes ever to form in the Gulf. Hurricane and Storm Surge Warnings are now in effect. The 5pm track really hasn't changed much. There will always be wobbles and slight adjustments, but a track that impacts our area directly is highly likely. Unfortunately, no way to candy coat this.  It's about as bad as it gets. The only favorable scenario is Milton SHOULD weaken due to shear before landfall. NHC has it as a Cat 3 by then. Stay tuned for updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Uncle. Updated surge numbers from the NHC. They have raised max potential surge from 8-12 feet to 10 to 15 feet. Water will be the biggest threat with this storm. The wind will be weakening somewhat at landfall. The surge levels will be the worst along and just to the South of where the eye makes landfall. Most of the area will see winds of 80-95 mph with a small area near the eye actually getting the max winds.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Evening Milton Thoughts: (DPL at 7pm on Facebook AND broadcast tv.)
1, No mincing words. Milton is one of the strongest storms ever to form in the Gulf.  However, it WILL weaken as it approaches Florida. It will STILL be a powerful hurricane, but not nearly as strong as it is now.  
2. Surge will still be massive along and just to the South of landfall. The map you see is "worst case scenario" which is up to 15 feet is some spots.
3. The track is STILL not set. You can see some models keep this to the South.  Anyone from Citrus County to Fort Myers cannot take this storm lightly. We have seen changes FAR too many times to ignore that possibility. (Charley, Irma, Ian...just to name a few)
4.  If you are told to evacuate, do so.
5. Power outages will be widespread overnight Wednesday night and into Thursday. Many of these outages will last for days, if not weeks. Unfortunately, I suspect we are going to be seeing a lot of blue tarps in the future.
6. You still have a FULL day of time to prep or evacuate. It's not too late.
7. As for colleges and schools, Florida State University and Florida A&M University look like they'll be ok.  University of Florida will be on the fringe of the heavy stuff. (Think 55-65.)  University of South Florida The University of Tampa University of Central Florida and Florida Gulf Coast University will see the brunt of these winds. (70-85)  UT could also see a surge along the river. That could very well be the biggest threat to local schools. Please use these areas as a guide for other colleges and universities not listed. (I can't list them all)
8. Now is NOT the time to freak out. It's the time to step up and do what needs to be done to protect you and your family. We have work to do. We have plans to make. We have people to care for and nurture. We have neighbors to check on. Times like this will require the best in ALL of us.  I have faith we will rise to the occasion as we always do. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Hurricane Milton LIVE update
We are LIVE right now with the latest on Hurricane Milton.  As of this moment, it's one of the strongest hurricanes ever to from in the Gulf.   Let's chat about it. That's right now on DPL

author: Denis Phillips
text: 9pm Milton Update: Pressure is down to 897 making Milton the 5th most intense hurricane ever to form in the Atlantic. The eye is only FOUR miles wide. Hurricane force winds only extend about 25 miles out. As we said yesterday, small storms can rapidly strengthen (and weaken) faster than larger storms. Let's hope when it hits shear in the Northern Gulf later tomorrow the weakening will begin. Updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 0z spaghetti models coming in. New NHC track out shortly.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton DOWN to 165. More than likely going thrown and ERC. (Eyewall Replacement Cycle). The inner eyewall collapses (and weakens the storm temporarily) while the new eyewall forms. It usually expands the wind field and makes the storm larger within 12 -24 hours. We will see. New track out shortly.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm Hurricane Milton Update: Winds DOWN to 165 mph. This is due to what we call an ERC or Eyewall Replacement Cycle. A second eyewall forms outside the 1st one and the inside eyewall dies. The process temporarily weakens the hurricane. Unfortunately, the key word is temporarily. It expands the wind field and usually regains its strength. Either way, the track is about the same although the NHC insists model trends to the South appear unreliable. We will see. Either way, a destructive hurricane is coming to the West coast of Florida late Wednesday. The "cone of uncertainty" is still located between Cedar Key and Ft Myers. Updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 0z models continue to nudge Milton a bit to the South. The GFS has moved from Cedar Key to just South of Tampa in the past 12 hours. That's a big swing.  Nothing drastic with the other models,  but a 15 mile difference could mean a huge change in high impact area. As I've been saying, the width of extreme winds could easily be less than 10 miles from the eye. So a swing (or wobble) of 15 or 20 miles would make a huge difference in end result. It happens all the time with Gulf landfalling hurricanes. We'll see what the NHC says at 5am. (I'm sure I'll be up...you?) #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton weakens to Cat 4 hurricane with winds of 155 mph. ERC (Eyewall Replacement Cycle) will be complete soon. At that point,  it's possible Milton could strengthen again. Either way, it's still a powerful hurricane with landfall expected on the West coast Wednesday night.  A new track will be released at 5am.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Tue 5 AM advisory is in. Intensity is the same and the track is basically where it was before with maybe a tiny nudge south though I wouldn't read too much into it. Timing is also adjusted now indicating landfall early Thu morning.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton continues to drift north of east as a strong Cat 4 hurricane. Indications are it is building a new eyewall. When this is done it could again gain strength.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Milton continues to show fluctuations in intensity. Winds are at 145 mph with a pressure that's up a bit since the last update. No changes in track.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Reposting:
Lots of folks are asking about Rule #7. (Don't freak out unless I'm freaking out). Here's the deal. We ALL know how serious this storm is. Now is NOT the time to freak out. Now IS the time for action. We have work to do. Work to prepare ourselves.  Work to help our friends and neighbors. Work to comfort our children. Making important decisions is hard. Making those choices while freaking out is harder. You can do this!! We have a day and a half to get ready for Milton. Hopefully, the shear we're forecasting will knock him down a peg or 2. (I think it will) We are a tight knit community.  We WILL get through this,  TOGETHER. ❤️

author: Denis Phillips
text: By far, the biggest impact the most people will see from Milton is power outages. This will be a HUGE power disruption event and some may not have power for days following the storm.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Important Milton Update: (We've got some stuff to cover here folks, strap in!)
1. Ok, here is the latest. As you can see, we are starting to get some agreement on the models. (Finally). They show landfall from the Skyway to the Southern end of Manatee County.  The NHC track pretty much mirrors this. That being said, most Floridians know "wobbles" are 100% unpredictable. No model (or human) can actually predict where they occur. However, most Floridians know those wobbles often are East of the center and have a tendency to go the the right. We've seen it SO many times before. (Charley, Irma, Ian, Idalia, and even Helene 2 weeks ago)
2. Hurricane winds will likely extend about 50 miles from the eye when it approaches our area. Right now, hurricane winds extend 30 miles from the center. Milton will get bigger over the next 36 hours. It's currently experiencing an ERC (Eyewall Replacement Cycle) which temporarily weakens the storm. That's why the winds are down.  Once complete, it's certainly possible Milton can strengthen again. Although, I DO NOT expect it to regain its strength from yesterday.  That's just my opinion. Could it?  Sure.  But I don't think it happens for a number of reasons.  Either way, Milton WILL be a powerful hurricane when it impacts our area. For the record, the NHC now has it as a Cat THREE hurricane at landfall. That's lower than yesterday which is great news!
3. Surge is still a MASSIVE concern. Highest surge totals will be at, and just to the South of the point of landfall. So a Manatee County surge would limit the height of the surge in Pinellas and the Bay. However, just a small tick to the North and those areas would be right in the thick of it.  If you're in an evacuation zone, DO SO.  No mincing words here. People who live along the coast and do not evacuate are risking their lives.  The threat of wind is MUCH lower than the threat of water with this (and most storms).
4. Let's talk about winds.  In my opinion. most of the area will see winds between 65 and 90 mph. VERY strong winds. Power outages will be widespread. Trees will fall. Some roofs will have issues as well. Lots of blue tarps are expected in the coming weeks. However, the extreme winds will be in a very small area. I'm talking 5 to 7 miles. They will probably occur near the eye and in the NORTHERN eyewall.  Shear IS going to weaken Milton. The storm will look cut in half at landfall. However, the Northern eyewall, which will be over Pinellas and Hillsborough counties, will contain extreme wind gusts.  Not sustained, but gusts to nearly 100 mph. This is something we will watch very closely.  A change or wobble in track could easily move this band 20 miles North or South.  Again, this is unpredictable and why it's important to keep following us here as well as on ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay  Once the storm moves inland, areas in Polk and Desoto counties will also see hurricane-force wind gusts. I suspect they even reach Orlando as well.  
5. I have posted and will post again, a great list of things to have ready as the storm nears. You still have ALL DAY today and tomorrow morning to get things done. The strongest winds don't arrive until Wednesday evening and overnight. 
6. Rule #7. Now it NOT the time to freak out. It's the time for action. We have work to do to protect our families. We have work to do to protect our neighbors, our friends, our community. The stress level is massive. You CAN do this. We still have a day and a half to get ready for Milton. Floridians are strongest when things get the worst. We WILL get through this. And we will do it, TOGETHER.  Updates 24/7. 
7. As for colleges and universities, if kids are still planning on coming home today, expect massive delays on the roads. Take that into consideration if you're planning on having your kids leave school to go home.  Florida State University and Florida A&M University will be fine.  University of Florida should be fine as well.  University of South Florida, The University of Tampa, and University of Central Florida will all experience hurricane-force wind gusts.  UT will also have a significant surge risk. Do NOT let your kids stay if they are in a mandatory flood zone along the river. At this point, Florida Gulf Coast University is on the fringe of the heavy winds but a track change could change that. Make sure they stay vigilant.
#FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11am Update: Milton now at 150 mph winds. Intensity will continue to fluctuate through the day, but with eyewall replacement cycle (ERC) complete, further strengthening is possible.  Keep it here for updates 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Here's a look at most likely arrival time for Tropical Storm winds. (Greater than 39 mph)

author: Denis Phillips
text: 2PM Update: Unfortunately, Milton has restrengthened to 155 mph making it nearly Cat 5 again. Winds are still forecast to weaken prior to landfall, but regardless,  Milton will strike our area as a powerful hurricane overnight Wednesday/Thursday.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Yikes. Thats a very bumpy ride

author: Denis Phillips
text: Latest spaghetti models. You have to think NHC bumps track a bit South at 5.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Here's the 11am NHC track for Milton. Now look at the latest model runs. I would be pretty surprised if the NHC doesn't nudge the track South at 5pm. Milton made a SE wobble earlier today, and those little movements 700 miles away, are much larger by the time they get to their destination. It's the old example I use where you drive a car, and barely turn the steering wheel. Short term, not much change in path. Long term, it's larger. So a small change in movement, now, is bigger later. That little SE jog it took today could mean a track more South by tomorrow. Let's check out the new NHC track at 5 and reconvene here a bit later. Deal?  #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: 5pm Milton Update: As expected, because of today's "wobble" to the Southeast, the NHC has bumped the track of Milton a bit to the South. Unfortunately, Milton is now a Cat 5 hurricane again with winds of 165 mph. The storm is an extremely dangerous hurricane that will bring catastrophic surge to the West coast of Florida. Of course, this change increases the threat of higher surge to areas from Sarasota Southward. Updates and analysis 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Important Milton Update:
1. As expected, after Milton's "wobble" today, the track of the hurricane has been shifted South.  As Floridians, we have come to expect these unpredictable wobbles. So yes, there can be more. In fact, upon approach, they are the norm. This increases the threat of surge to our Southern counties. It certainly does NOT eliminate the surge threat to the Tampa Bay area. However, yes, if this updated track verifies, the surge levels will be lower in the Bay and off of Pinellas County.
2. Milton is once again a Cat 5 hurricane. Winds are back to 165 mph and the pressure has tanked again. It is back to record-breaking status. This storm is a beast. DO NOT take a subtle change to track as a lower threat to our area. There will still be hurricane-force winds. There will still be 5 to 10 inches of rain with some areas seeing more. With the shear expected, I believe the Northern eyewall will be the dominant feature of the storm where the squalls will be the heaviest. That's you, Pinellas, Hillsborough, Pasco, and Pinellas counties. 
3.  Manatee and Sarasota counties would see higher surge risks with a Southern track. That would also increase the risk of a massive surge for areas as far South as Fort Myers.  Remember, the cone of uncertainty STILL runs from Pasco County to Fort Myers.
4. Milton will go across the state and bring hurricane wind conditions to much of Polk County and Orlando. Expect widespread power outages in these areas as well.
5.  I'll be on at 7pm to 8 pm with DPL so I can answer your questions and give you a better idea of how things are changing minute by minute. 
6.  Yes, this new track lowers the chance of a catastrophic surge to the Tampa Bay area. It doesn't however, eliminate it.  Don't lower your guard if you live on the water. There will STILL be a significant surge regardless of how this track plays out!! 
7. Time to finish up those preps. It's gonna be a LONG 36 hours. Widespread power outages will occur tomorrow night. We'll be there for you every step of the way. 
#FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Hurricane Milton LIVE update
We are LIVE right now with the latest on Hurricane Milton.  We had a track change today. How will that impact the Bay Area?   Let's chat about it. That's right now on DPL

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Major Category 5 Hurricane Milton Update:
1. The new track comes out at 11pm. I wouldn't be surprised it we see another subtle nudge to the South. But in general, things should stand pat.
2. Regardless of track, this is an extremely powerful hurricane. The surge will be highest just to the South of where the eye crosses the coast. It is very possible if the track goes well to the South of the Bay, we could see a "reverse surge" which could drain much of Tampa Bay itself.
3. The Northern eyewall will be hovering across Pinellas, Hillsborough and Pasco counties.  This will give rough weather (heavy rain with winds 65 to 90) late evening and overnight.  Massive power outages are expected.
5. I'll have another update after 11 with the new track.  As for Florida State University and Florida A&M University, they'll be fine.  University of Florida should be ok as well.  University of Central Florida University of South Florida and The University of Tampa will get hurricane-force winds.  UT is under mandatory evacuation so my gut is, that most have left.  Florida Gulf Coast University will be on the fringe of the rough stuff unless the track goes South.

author: Denis Phillips
text: 11pm Hurricane Milton Update: Milton now has winds of 160 mph. It's still a powerful Category 5 hurricane. The updated track looks to be a bit South of the 5pm advisory, with projected landfall near Sarasota. The cone reaches from Pinellas County to Fort Myers. A track like this would increase surge levels across our Southern Counties and all the way down to Fort Myers.  We'll continue to update overnight and 24/7.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Overnight Milton Update:
1. Milton is an extremely powerful Cat 5 hurricane with winds of 160 mph. Slow weakening should begin through landfall. Even still, it is quite possible Milton will be the strongest hurricane to hit the Bay Area in over 100 years. Regardless of the exact track, this will be a high-impact hurricane with massive power outages, winds gusting over 100 mph, surge and freshwater flooding. Don't underestimate its strength!
2. Yes, the NHC has nudged its track to the South over the past 12 hours. But a lesson we should all have learned over the years is last minute changes in track are not uncommon in Florida. We can list a dozen that have done so. Even Helene with a great forecast track was headed right toward Tallahassee and bumped about 30 miles to the East at landfall. A 30-mile change in track with this storm could have HUGE implications for folks in our area.  Do not let your guard down. 
3. IF the current forecast (shown above) verifies from the NHC, the surge will be much lower in the Bay than forecast. The numbers you have seen are "worst case scenario" and the NHC shows these to prepare you for what COULD happen if the track were to change or shift.  It should be noted that IF the track is correct, and landfall were to occur closer to Sarasota, the highest surge would be South of Sarasota. 
4. Milton will cut across the state and bring hurricane-force winds to our inland counties. Polk County will see winds gusting greater than 80 mph overnight Wednesday. Again, the strongest winds will be within about 10 miles from the center of the storm. It should be noted Pinellas and Hillsborough counties could see extremely heavy rain and winds gusting 80-90 mph even IF the track goes more South. If it doesn't, those numbers could be higher.
5. I suspect most folks are done prepping. You've either left, or you haven't. Now it's time to ride it out.  We'll be updating 24/7 in hopes of getting you the information you need during this stressful time.  We WILL get through this together.  It's what we do.  #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: BREAKING | New Milton is now forecast to make landfall as a Cat 4 storm. Track has been bumped a bit NORTH as global models continue to show landfall closer to the mouth of the Bay in Manatee County.

author: Denis Phillips
text: Heads up! The heaviest rain and highest threat of flash flooding will likely happen 40-60 miles NORTH of the center. This is outside of the cone. The impacts from hurricanes extend well away from the center. Don't focus just on the cone or the point of landfall.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
At this point,  every little jog or wobble will matter. Trying to use models to pinpoint a track change of a few miles is impossible.  So, for the next 18 hours,  we do what is called "nowcasting". We, like you,  track every little change or nuance of the path.
Our area has prepared the best we can for the past 2 days. Most of the significant impacts begin in about 12 hours and will continue overnight. These include:
1. Winds across much of the warned areas gusting as high as 100 mph at its worst. Remember, the extreme winds will be confined to a very small area near the eye. Hurricane winds STILL only extend 30 miles out. 
2. Surge is such a close call. Ultimately, last minute wobbles will determine landfall and impacts. These wobbles are completely unpredictable, altbough they often bump toward the East. The worst of the surge will be along, and just South of landfall.  However, overall surge will have impacts far and wide.  If you live on the water,  you still have time to evacuate away from harms way. 
3. At this point, the heaviest rains will be NORTH of the center. Totals of 6-10" are expected in these areas with higher local totals.
4. For nearly a century, the Bay Area has just missed impacts from major hurricanes. Unfortunately, that streak ends today.  We have prepared the best we can. Exact landfall will matter for surge. However, impacts will be felt across the entire area. Of course, some worse than others. You still have time to finish up preps for a few hours before the worst starts to come in this evening.
5. Many will lose power overnight. Those outages could last for days or even weeks in some places. Our radio partners will simulcast our broadcast through the day to allow you to get important information for the next 24 hours or longer.
6. We have prepared for the worst. Now we hope for the best. In the coming weeks we will lean on each other for help and support. Our communities will come together to help those in need. That will be a lot of folks. Our team will be there for you 24/7. Today, tomorrow  and until every last person in our neighborhoods are back on their feet. Updates 24/7. #FloridaStrong

author: Denis Phillips

author: Denis Phillips
text:
For those wanting a great hurricane list, here ya go.  Please share with people who might need it. 
 Here's what my family does. Hopefully, we won't need all this but better safe than sorry.
 And remember,  shelters are also an option. They fill up quickly in a big storm and some don't allow pets. Keep that in mind if you're in harms way! 
1. Charge any device that provides light. Laptops, tablets, cameras, video cameras, and old phones. Old cell phones can still used for dialing 911. Charge external battery backups. ( Glow sticks also provide light for a few hours. Stand them upright in a glass or jar to make a lantern.)
2. Wash all trash cans, big and small, and fill them with water for flushing toilets. Line outdoor trash cans with trash bags, fill them with water and store them in the garage. Add bleach to sterilize. 
3. Fill every tub and sink with water. Cover sinks with Saran Wrap to keep it from collecting dust. Fill the washing machine and leave the lid up to store water.
4. Fill old empty water bottles and other containers with water and keep them near sinks for washing hands. 
5. Fill every Tupperware with water and store it in the freezer. These will help keep food cold longer and serve as a backup water supply. 
6. Fill drinking cups with water and cover with Saran Wrap. Store as many as possible in the fridge. You can store the rest on the counter and use it before opening any water bottles. Ice is impossible to find after the storm.
7. Reserve fridge space for storing tap water and keep the sealed water bottles on the counter. 
8. Cook any meats in advance and other perishable foods. You can freeze cooked food. Hard boil eggs for snacks for the first day without power. 
9. Be well hydrated before the storm hits and avoid salty foods that make you dehydrated. 
10. Wash all dirty clothes and bed sheets. Anything dirty will smell without the A/C, you may need the items, and with no A/C, you'll be sweating a lot. You're going to want clean sheets. 
11. Toss out any expired food, clean cat litter boxes, and empty all trash cans in the house, including bathrooms. Remove anything that will cause an odor when the A/C is off. If you don't have a trash day pickup before the storm, find a dumpster. 
12. Bring in any yard decor, secure anything that will fly around, secure gates, bring in hoses, potted plants, etc. Bring in patio furniture and grills. 
13. Clean your environment so you have clear, easy escape routes. Even if that means temporarily moving furniture to one area. 
14. Scrub all bathrooms so you are starting with a clean odor-free environment. Store water-filled trash cans next to each toilet for flushing. 
15. Place everything you own that is important and necessary in a backpack or small file box that is easy to grab. Include your wallet with ID, phone, hand sanitizer, snacks, etc. Get plastic sleeves for important documents.
16. Make sure you have cash on hand.
17. Stock up on pet food and fill up bowls of water for pets. 
18. Refill any medications. Most insurance companies allow for 2 emergency refills per year.
19. Fill your propane tanks. You can heat soup cans, boil water, make coffee, and other stuff besides just grilling meat. Get an extra, if possible.
20. Drop your A/C in advance and lower temperatures in your fridges. 
21. Gather all candles, flashlights, lighters, matches, batteries, and other items and keep them accessible. 
22. Clean all counters in advance. Start with a clean surface. Buy Clorox Wipes for cleaning when there is no power. Mop your floors and vacuum. If power is out for 10 days, you'll have to live in the mess you started with. 
23. Pick your emergency safe place such as a closet under the stairs. Store the items you'll need in that location for the brunt of the storm. Make a hand fan for when the power is out. 
24. Shower just before the storm is scheduled to hit. 
25. Keep baby wipes next to each toilet. Don't flush them. It's not the time to risk clogging your toilet!
26. Run your dishwasher, don't risk having dirty smelly dishes and you need every container for water! Remember you'll need clean water for brushing your teeth, washing yourself, and cleaning your hands. 
27. Put a small suitcase in your car in case you decide to evacuate. Also, put at least one jug of water in your car. It will still be there if you don't evacuate!  Remember to pack for pets as well. 
28. Check on all family members, set up emergency backup plans, and check on elderly neighbors. 
29. Remember, pets are family too. Take them with you!
30. Before the storm, unplug all electronics. There will be power surges during and after the storm.
31. Gas up your car and have a spare gas container for your generator or your car when you run out.
32 . Use plastic cups and paper plates. 👍  You need water to wash dishes....👎
33 . Also if you run out of water tap your hot water heater it can have up to 30 gallons stored in there.
34 . Put water in balloons and store it in the freezer.
35 . If it's yellow let it mellow, if it's brown Flush it down!💩
If you can, take a video of your house and contents....walk room to room--open cabinets/drawers and closets.  This will help if you need to make a claim later.  It will show proof of items and help you list all the items (help your memory, so you don't forget anything)...I highly recommend it!!!
I also heard you should freeze a cup of water, and place a coin on top after it is frozen...keep this in your freezer to help you gauge the temperature if the power goes out.  If the coin stays on top, the food is staying frozen.  If the coin falls into the water, the freezer thawed out and most food will likely need to be thrown away.  This is super helpful is you have to leave and come back, as it may appear everything is still frozen, but if the coin is in the cup--you will know!!

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Our team is doing our best to keep you up to date with every wobble and turn. Greg just posted this update on the surge threat. 
Such a close call for the Bay! The 5AM forecast was nudged north by just a few miles and the SLOSH model forecast for surge in the Bay increased from 3ft. to over 6ft in some cases in eastern and southern Hillsborough County while the NW part of the Bay near Oldsmar may see nothing. 
Any more wobbles to the north will send these numbers WAY up. Any more wobbles to the south will send these numbers WAY down. 
All we can do is watch and track. It's still not too late to get out of the way if you are in an evacuation zone. Run from the water. Hide from the wind.

text: 4 PM position is in. Winds at 125 mph. Milton is a Cat 3 hurricane moving NE at 17. Pressure in the eye continues to rise indicating Milton is weakening. The eye wall is open, also an indication of a weakening system. The storm is still likely to make landfall as a major hurricane.

author: Denis Phillips
text: IMPORTANT Milton Update: Winds are now down to 125. Obviously, that's a good thing. Here's my concern. The NHC has predicted more of a turn to the NE with their Manatee County track. At this point, that has not happened. The surge threat remains high across the ENTIRE area including (and South) of Pinellas county. IF Milton delays its turn, the surge level will be higher in the Bay. Power outages are now increasing.  It's about to get real folks. We're updating on air and online 24/7. You can download our app ABC Action News so if (when) you lose power, you can still watch our coverage. #Floridastrong

author: Denis Phillips
text: 5PM track update is in!  The NHC sees the wobble, track has been adjusted NORTH. Now have it coming across NW Manatee and Southern Hillsborough. Winds down to 120 mph. Surge threat for the Bay has increased. Strong winds will be north of the track across Pinellas, Hillsborough and Pasco Counties.

author: Denis Phillips
text:
Milton update and thoughts:
1. Unfortunately, our streak of 103 years is over. The Bay Area is being hit by a major hurricane. Winds are down to 120 mph and is weakening. Unfortunately, the surge will be a big issue for much of the coast. The NHC has adjusted the track closer to the Bay. They now forecast a surge of 6 to 9 feet in the Bay. That is lower than the 10-15 feet surge originally predicted.  I suspect the SOUTHERN Bay will see more water than the Northern side.
2. The storms looks as expected. The northern half is very squally, and the Southern half has practically no rain. This will continue. If you live South of this line, there won't be much rain. If you live North of the line, there will be a TON of rain.  8 to 12 inches of rain is likely.
3. As for winds, they are weaker, but it is STILL a Category 3 major hurricane. The winds will be very intense in about a 5 to 7 miles stretch near the eye. Outside of that area, hurricane winds extend only 35 miles out. Most folks will see winds gusting 75-90 near the track.  65 to 85 are you get farther away from the eye. It is quite possible an "Extreme Wind Warning" will be issued for the area right near the eye.  That is a warning for winds sustained of 115 mph. It will be small, but extremely powerful. 
4. This is an emotional time. Anxiety is through the roof.  We are ready. We've done the preps. We've evacuated areas along the coast. This is what we've been dreading for decades. Together, we will get through this. I refuse to let a storm named Milton get the best of me. I'm sure you feel the same way. Neighbor helping neighbor. That's the way it starts. @Floridastrong

author: Greg Dee
text:
Mickey Fay sent me this pic of a waterspout she spotted in Destin when up there last weekend. It was her first waterspout spotting! Nice pic! 
For those that are new to the area, waterspouts are very common over the warm waters around Florida. They can happen around summer storms or even winter cold fronts. Although most of the time they are accompanied by a shower or thunderstorm, sometimes they can form under fair-weather clouds. The majority of these are EF-0 intensity and stay harmlessly out over the water. They should be given space if in a boat. 
Occasionally, these can move onshore. Although they typically weaken quickly when they do, minor damage is possible near the beach lofting beach chairs and umbrellas along with other smaller objects.

author: Greg Dee

author: Greg Dee
text:
GEEK ALERT! Ok, this is a little "in the weeds," but I think it's worth the explainer. Other than the main models we show you, such as the American (GFS) and EURO, each model actually has multiple models inside it. These are called "ensemble runs" and are typically displayed together.
An ensemble in this case is a variation on the main model run. You take the data that goes into the forecast, tweak one of the parameters and run the model. Then you do the same thing over and over, each time tweaking something else. Each time the result is a slightly different solution. 
When plotted together, the result looks similar to a spaghetti plot. There is a difference though. In a spaghetti plot, each line is a completely different model. In an ensemble, each line is a slightly different result from the same model. Reading them is similar. The wider the spread, the less confidence. The lines also show you the possible variations that could occur in that model as parameters change. 
Below is the latest ensemble run of the EURO. Notice although the lines are spread out wide indicating quite a bit of uncertainty in the current EURO forecast, most of them are east of Florida. This indicates that even if some of the parameters around the storm change, it's still more likely than not, that this stays east of the state. A track to the east would mean we basically get nothing from the system here in the Bay area with less rain than usual in the afternoons because we'd be located on the drier side of the storm.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Well well well....those EURO ensemble tracks...they're now looking a lot more like the American model.
What does that mean? The EURO track has shifted west, back with the American model. This now puts the system to our west this weekend, increasing our rain chances. A track more of the Gulf now also increases the chances for a stronger system as it will track over much warmer water. Folks from the Keys to the Emerald Coast (PCB, Destin, Ft. Walton) need to watch this system as it will impact us this weekend & likely into next week if it tracks up toward the Panhandle.
FYI, for now, neither of the models do anything crazy with the intensity but a tropical storm is definitely in the realm of possibility. Even if the system tracks toward the Panhandle, watches or warnings for the Florida west coast are possible as some impacts from the storm would extend well east of the center. 
Stay tuned! We'll see if this new EURO shift holds overnight.

author: Greg Dee
text:
THU PM UPDATE | I realize you've probably been seeing way to many spaghetti lines, model plots, etc. It's confusing & overwhelming. I'm only going to show you one here. This is one of my favorites because it takes ALL the different runs from the EURO, AMERICAN, UKMET & CMC models & looks at their tracks. It then uses simple shading to show you where most of the models track. This is a great way to look at it b/c when it comes to model forecasts, the most accurate one is ALWAYS the average of all of them. The green/yellow shading here shows where most of the model tracks take this system right now.
You'll notice the track is along Cuba & into the eastern Gulf. I really can't see this system even begin to organize until it gets away from Cuba. That means it will already be near Florida before organization can even begin. That's great for us b/c IF (there is still a chance it doesn't) it does organize & strengthen it'll already be close to us & won't have much time to get very strong before it heads back over Florida somewhere near the Big Bend. A track very close to our coast would also actually be good b/c the more of the storm hangs over land the slower it will likely organize & strengthen. 
So what do we get? Saturday will probably be the BEST day of the weekend. I'm not saying it's going to be dry, it's August. Expect sct'd storms but compared to Sunday, Saturday looks GREAT. Sunday on the other hand will likely be cloudy & showery at least with an increasing potential for simply a lot of rain on Sunday. Gusty winds? Sure! Nothing worse than a typical summer storm, but definitely a day for indoor activities.
The interesting part is that all the models now take the storm over Florida & back over the Atlantic near Jacksonville & potentially strengthen it again to be a concern for the Carolinas. 
Still a low threat, still nothing to worry about. Think summer thunderstorm-type rain on Sunday at this point. 
See y'all in the morning!

author: Greg Dee
text:
FRI AM UPDATE | Here is the latest on the tropics and our weekend.
1.) NHC has now increased the chance for development in the eastern Gulf this weekend to 80%. The system will be tracking over or close to Cuba all day today.
2.) FRIDAY | Expect advisories along with watches and warnings for the Florida west coast today or very early Saturday. Although the system won't develop until Saturday or even Sunday, because it may reach tropical storm strength, the NHC is likely to begin issuing advisories on a Potential Tropical Cyclone (PTC) Friday or very early Saturday. This allows them to issue tropical storm watches and warnings for the Florida coast. These will be necessary as this system does have the potential to reach tropical storm strength. The next name on the list is Debby.
3.) SATURDAY |  Looks like a normal August day with some changes moving in very late in the day. Look for sunny skies early then sct'd showers developing during the afternoon. Late in the evening, more substantial areas of rain may begin to move in from the southeast, especially south of I-4. Much of this may not happen until after dark Saturday so get your outdoor plans done early. Although it may get breezy, wind will not be an issue on Saturday.
4.) SUNDAY | This will ALL depend on exact track and intensity so let's just assume the system stays west of our coast and moves north toward the Big Bend. Let's also assume it reaches low to moderate level tropical storm strength (sustained winds of 40-60 mph). With this in mind, we likely get clouds and lots of rain or periods of heavy rain on Sunday. A tornado watch is possible depending on the intensity of the storm (this is the normal procedure on the east side of tropical systems that are interacting with land). Will it be bad? Nope. Think windy, gusty thunderstorm at the worst. There will be at least some minor water rise at the coast so areas that easily flood may see water on Sunday. Though winds may not be a huge issue, gusts of 50-60 mph can knock down tree branches and thus cause isolated power outages. Rainfall will also be an issue as amounts of 2-6"+ (depending on the strength and track of the storm) could cause some areas to see localized flooding. You know, based on previous rain events, if that's you, and your street floods during a summer thunderstorm, it may definitely flood during these rains too. 
5.) MONDAY | Based on current track data, this system will be moving away from Florida on Monday. I think we could still see some showers early in the day from this and the east coast from JAX to MIA could see some sct'd storms Monday midday or early afternoon. The storm will move off the coast of NE Florida and the models are in pretty good agreement that it will gain back some or maybe even much more intensity. This could be a bigger issue for the Carolinas from SC to the Outer Banks of NC (OBX). Some models have it intensifying to a strong tropical storm or maybe even a hurricane off the Carolina coast.

If you can, move outdoor work plans to later in the day or earlier in the morning to avoid the hottest part of the day, mid-afternoon.

author: Greg Dee

author: Greg Dee

author: Greg Dee
text:
SAT AM UPDATE | Good morning! Some changes with what is now TD 4 overnight.
TRACK CHANGE | Check out the first graphic, the blue one from the NHC. Friday afternoon the forecast was for the center of TD 4 to be located on the north coast of Cuba just emerging over the Gulf Saturday AM. I've drawn in the actual position. See the difference? TD 4 is tracking more west & is farther south than expected. Though the difference doesn't seem like much, it's actually about 70 miles farther south than expected. This means big changes in the forecast down the line. 
FORECAST CHANGE | Because of this farther south track, the storm will turn north later & thus make it farther west into the Gulf. A track farther west means more time over the water & a stronger storm. This is why the intensity forecast is now up to 70 mph from the NHC & why a hurricane watch is up for the Big Bend of Florida. Interestingly, none of the models right now bring this system to that strength, but since the water in the Gulf is so warm, NHC may be trying to get ahead of any unexpected strengthening.
OUR AREA | A track farther west & a stronger system (remember yesterday's forecast kept the storm very close to the west coast) does bring some changes for us. First, the stronger winds will now be only along the coast. It's unlikely you'll get anything strong east of I-75. Stronger systems have the strongest winds closer to the center so if the center stays well off-shore, we won't get as much wind, except in Citrus & Hernando counties since you'll be closer to the landfall location in the Big Bend.   
Because the storm delayed its turn north, the timing has now changed too. Not only will Sunday be a wet day for many, but the first half of Monday will also be stormy since the storm is not expected to come ashore in the Big Bend until very early Monday morning. I expect Monday morning to feature a couple of rain bands near the coast that could be dropping heavy rain. Isolated tornadoes will also be possible in any rain bands that setup over our area. 
Speaking of rainfall, it will definitely now be more sct'd. I still expect areas of heavy rain, but with us being farther east in the storm, there will be more "banding" to the rain with dry & possibly even sunny areas in between rain squalls. It's in these rain squalls that you'd find some of the stronger wind gusts. 
WIND | There probably isn't much of a change to the wind intensity over our area. It's a wash: Stronger system, but farther west. Inland counties probably won't get much of any wind (except in isolated tornadoes) while the coast may see gusts of 50-60 mph which is what we were expecting yesterday. Citrus County, closer to the center may get stronger gusts IF the system does indeed make landfall in the Big Bend Monday AM. 
WATER | A stronger system will also mean more surge potential. A system moving SW to NE at landfall in the Big Bend will push more water toward the Florida west coast. Tides MAY be in our favor here. There is a high tide at the Gandy Bridge on Sunday around 4 PM & the next high tide is Monday at 6 AM but this tide is 1.5 ft. lower. Monday morning would be the time to watch for any coastal flooding. The next higher high tide is Monday at 4 pm but by then the storm will likely be too far away to cause flooding in our area. 
CONCLUSION | Is this going to be bad? No. I think the threat level for us is the same. The system will be stronger, but farther away. The character of what we'll get has changed from a more solid, windy rain to more of a squally storm, with periods of rain followed by some breaks. This forecast could also continue to shift west. If TD 4 keeps turning north more slowly, I'd expect additional forecast track changes

author: Greg Dee

author: Greg Dee
text:
It'll be interesting to see if the track of TD 4, soon-to-be Debby, continues to shift a little more west. The 11 am NHC forecast track is on the east side of where the models are clustering this morning. Now, there is a little bit of a back-and-forth "sprinkler effect" with the model tracks, so consistency is key, but we may not be done adjusting this one. 
A track farther west keeps more of our area out of the actual storm core, which is good. The Nature Coast still needs to watch this but the landfall location is farther NW than Idalia so that's certainly good for keeping the worst stuff farther away from Citrus County.
As far as surge, some minor surge is unavoidable. Anytime a developing & strengthening system turns NE in the Gulf, that does push water east into the west coast of Florida. I think surges will be minor but water levels a couple feet above normal tide levels can be expected with the current forecast.

author: Greg Dee
text: Hello Debby. The National Hurricane Center has upgraded Debby to a Tropical Storm. No real changes to the track but they do bump up winds 5 mph upon landfall which would upgrade Debby to a hurricane.

author: Greg Dee
text:
FYI, for those wondering, the "center line" of the forecast cone puts Debby more than 100 miles west of Indian Rocks Beach on Sunday. Tropical storm winds (40 mph+) are expected to extend 100 miles SE of the center. This means our coast MAY barely get into some minimal sustained tropical storm winds Sunday PM overnight into Monday morning. The nature coast will see the strongest winds as Debby will be turning NE back toward the coast Sunday night into Monday morning. Even there, sustained winds will likely be around 50 mph at the most, though likely weaker. 
If Debby does become a hurricane, the current forecast calls for hurricane force winds to extend 20 miles southeast of the center. This is no where near our area. On this forecast track no one in our region will be even close to hurricane force winds. Most of you who live more than 20 miles from the coast will NOT see any tropical storm winds either. You may however see wind gusts over 40 mph in any thunderstorms that do form. Isolated tornadoes would obviously bring stronger winds. 
Updates to follow!

author: Greg Dee
text: Spending a little time in Orlando. Excited to meet up with some friends from the southern hemisphere & enjoy some of the best things Florida has to offer. We didn’t invite Debby to the party originally but the storm decided to show up anyways!

author: Greg Dee
text: DEBBY 5 AM | No changes. Debby is slowly strengthening with 50 mph winds. No changes to our forecast with the core of the storm staying well west of our coast. Showers and storms pick up in coverage later this morning. Localized flooding is possible with a Flood Watch for the entire area. A tornado threat will increase later today as well.

author: Greg Dee
text:
SUN AM UPDATE 
TRACK | There are no changes to the track of the storm. Debby is expected to move north along the Florida west coast today & up toward the Big Bend Monday morning where the storm may become a hurricane. 
WINDS | Tropical storm force winds begin in our area, along the coast, south to north starting this morning. The NHC forecast calls for winds of 39-57 mph to extend 130-140 miles southeast of the center. If Debby goes right down the middle of the current cone, then it will track about 100 miles west of Indian Rock Beach. This means that those of us within 30-40 miles of the coast may briefly get into sustained winds of 39 mph+ but likely below 50 mph (with higher gusts). 
Strong tropical storm force winds (57-73 mph) are expected to extend only 30 miles east of the center. This means that no one in our area will see sustained winds greater than 57 mph. 
Hurricane-force winds (74 mph+) are only expected to extend 15 miles from the center. No one in our area (Citrus to Sarasota County) will get hurricane winds. 
RAIN | The heaviest & most consistent rain will be along the coast. East of I-75 the rain will be sct'd until you get to the east coast where there will be just sct'd showers. Check out the included rainfall forecast map to get an approximation of how much your area will see. Amounts will range from 10"+ along the coast to only 2-4" in eastern Polk & Highlands Counties. Your location between those two extremes determines how much you get. Flooding is possible from heavy rain & is most likely in areas west of I-75.
SURGE | A overall low-level rise of 2-4' is likely with Debby today & tomorrow during times of high tides. This means that a normal high tide could be 2-4' higher than normal. Minor coastal flooding is possible.     
TORNADOS | We are under a level 2 out of 5 risk of severe storms today. There will be two areas that may see tornadoes today. Some will be in the main shield of the storm, near the coast. This area sometimes sees quick spin-ups. The other region will be those east of I-75. Here we're likely to see some sun during the day. The sun will heat things up causing sct'd thunderstorms to form in bands, east of the storm. Any of these thunderstorm bands may be strong & can produce a quick tornado. These tornadoes are usually EF-0 or EF-1 but can cause damage.  
CONCLUSION | Will this system be bad? No. What will "my town name" get? Please use the maps & info about to guestimate where you fit in all of this. Your town will get rain & a little wind, is the answer for really everyone. Overall Debby is definitely not a storm you should completely disregard, but it's also not something that will be significant for Tampa Bay. Just be careful when getting around in the rain, don't drive through flooded areas, & play it smart out there. For the majority of people, you'll get rain, a little gusty wind & likely even some sun in-between.

author: Greg Dee
text: FLOOD RISK | These numbers change with every update, but this gives us a general idea of the 4-8" rain forecast we have moving forward, with a few spots seeing a bit more or a bit less. author: Greg Dee
text:
MON 2 AM | This is one of my favorite plots for storm tracks as it combines ALL the models and colors in where all the models overlap the most. The green and especially the yellow color corresponds to where the models overlap (agree) the most. Right now that's the panhandle around Apalachicola. Below that is the intensity forecast which brings this up to hurricane strength, possibly cat 3. 
The swath however for Invest 97L is still quite wide. Blue areas in this track plot indicate outlying model tracks that still exist & those stretch from the Florida west coast to southeast Louisiana indicating that there is still much unknown in the track forecast & that shifts are still possible & even quite likely. 
More to come.

author: Greg Dee
text: NEW | NHC increases chance of development in the Gulf. A tropical system in the Gulf is now likely by mid-week. All interests in the eastern half of the Gulf of Mexico need to monitor the progress of this storm closely
