text: As of early Monday morning, best guess at landfall timing is Thur PM somewhere on the Gulf coast

author: Greg Dee
text:
Lots of spaghetti plots in overnight. Notice that NW turn after landfall? Upper low pressure coming out of the Rockies will pull this system north and then NW. 
One concern: These setups can sometimes lead to rapid intensification as the hurricane gets closer to the low. The low pressure helps to evacuate (move) air out of the eye of the storm faster leading to faster air motion through the center and thus stronger winds and lower pressure.

author: Greg Dee
text: Here is a quick look at the tropics including some new overnight spaghetti plots on that system in the NW Caribbean that's headed for the Gulf.

author: Greg Dee
text: Folks, at this point there are no specific town by town impact forecasts. We're all in the same type of weather

author: Greg Dee
text:
The spaghetti plots this morning are pretty clustered which indicates a high amount of agreement between the models. This track can still change if the center of the storm forms in an unexpected location, thus throwing off the forecasts.
Looking at the lines you may be thinking "oh, it's going to stay west. We'll miss this one." That's not quite true.
The lines here show where the center of the storm will track. The center is only a small portion of a much larger storm that will extend hundreds of miles from that center. Yes, the most destructive winds will stay off-shore from us BUT we will be well into the "envelope of impacts," on the eastern, stronger side of the storm. 
Another way to look at this is to compare the forecast tracks to that of Debby (which is in red on this map). Debby was actually forecast to track almost the identical distance & ended up a little closer to us. We certainly got a ton from that storm in terms of rain, gusty winds & multiple tornado warnings. Flooding was a huge issue due to heavy rain. It's very likely that some of these impacts will again be felt in our area from the storm this week.
The only question will be how much. Debby was a weaker hurricane. Weaker storms are more broad. A stronger hurricane, say Cat 3 or Cat 4 could potentially be more compact meaning the impacts would not extend as far from the center as a Cat 1 hurricane or tropical storm. 
As the center forms & we get a better idea of the exact track & more importantly, a broad idea of potential intensity we will then be able to better speak about the impacts to us. 
Generally, the closer you are to the coast, the more you'll likely see & the more intense those impacts will be.   
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text: FORECAST | Weather looks good today and tomorrow and much of Wednesday too. It will get a bit hot today and tomorrow and we could see a couple of pop-up afternoon showers

author: Greg Dee
text: For Tampa Bay area, the storm is done by Friday PM with just sct'd pop-up storms through the weekend.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Mon AM intensity forecasts are all trending higher with this system. Several models now bring this into major, Cat 3, strength with some suggesting even a Cat 4 storm is possible. 
This is obviously much stronger than Debby earlier in the season & HUGE concern for areas in the Panhandle where this thing, for now, is expected to track. Widespread power outages, of long duration, are likely from Panama City to Apalachicola to Tallahassee due to the extensive tree cover.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Here is an early look at potential rainfall from this system as is tracks up the coast to our west. The closer to the coast you are the more rain you will get. 
WARNING: This is VERY preliminary and could swing up or down by a lot if the storm goes closer to the coast (amounts could be 2-3x these) or moves farther west (amounts would be much lower with some getting nothing). 
Wed PM to Fri AM is the window for the heaviest rain.

author: Greg Dee
text: NEW | Minor update from the NHC increasing the 2 day development chances to 80%. Advisories could be issued on this as early as tomorrow in order to facilitate watches/warnings for the Gulf coast.

author: Greg Dee
text: We know a lot more about this system, soon to be Helene, than we did over the weekend but many Dee-tails still need to be hashed out. Here’s a quick update. ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text:
Model data continues to come in on what will be Helene. A significant shift is possible once the center forms, for now, we have solid guesses on where that formation will take place & where it will then go. 
As of now, this map is a great way to look at the possible shifts. The reds & orange indicate the areas where most of the models are suggesting the center of the storm goes. That's a track toward the Big Bend or Apalachicola. 
Notice however that the blue colors to extend as far east as Tampa Bay & far west as Pensacola indicating that some model tracks are outside of this center area. This is why I think we are not in the "all-clear." Even a track a little closer to us would greatly increase the impacts. 
Last thing: WATER. Even a storm that does not produce "bad" conditions here, the flooding could be MUCH worse than the wind in our area. Remember ETA? We didn't get much wind or rain with it, but we did get A LOT of water. This could be the case again.

author: Greg Dee
text: NEW | NHC will begin issuing advisories on Potential Tropical Cyclone #9 at 11 am. Track coming with these.

author: Greg Dee
text: 11am Update: PTC 9 is forecast to strengthen over the next few days as it moves northward into the Gulf. More updates on the way for potential impacts on Florida.

author: Greg Dee
text: Key message from the 11 am PTC #9 Advisory. All of the Tampa Bay area will be east of the center.

author: Greg Dee
text:
The first advisory on Potential Tropical Cyclone #9 is out & there is a lot to note here. 
1.) Based on some of the spaghetti plots, the NHC is definitely a bit east of the models (closer to us) & has the storm move more NNE as it makes landfall in the Big Bend on Thursday. Some of the models are closer to Apalachicola & call for a turn to the north or even NNW at landfall.
2.) Motion direction is important as any "east" component to the storm as it landfalls to our north would increase the surge threat along our coast. They do note in the advisory that this track may change a lot depending on where the center of the storm forms.
3.) The tricky thing here is intensity. NHC has definitely split the difference between the weaker forecast of the EURO & the stronger forecast of the GFS, which in some runs brings this to a strong Cat 3 or even Cat 4 hurricane. This intensity forecast is the least reliable part of hurricane forecasting.  
4.) The problem with this track is that it is horrible for our coast. An eastward-moving storm, strong, approaching from SW to NE & making landfall north of Tampa Bay is a huge concern for flooding. The storm's southwest winds on the right side of the track would push water toward the coast. Add to this the relatively shallow nature of the Gulf in this area & you run into flooding along the coast & everywhere around the bay as well. 
5.) You're going to see some surge model data from the NWS Slosh model online today. I've included them here for Cedar Key, Clearwater & St. Pete. These maps show the potential water & surge levels late Thursday afternoon & evening. These water levels are upwards of 7 ft. above the highest astronomical tide observed, a.k.a. SERIOUS FLOODING. Think ETA or worse. This would extend from Manatee County, all of the Bay around Tampa, Oldsmar down to Appollo Beach & then up the coast into the Nature Coast. 
6.) I want to caution that these early surge forecasts are subject to change, significantly based on track & intensity. But even if the actual flooding is HALF of what is forecast here, then we're still looking at significant water inundation in areas that are susceptible to flooding. If you flooded during ETA you have a high chance of flooding again in this storm if the track doesn't change. 
7.) If this flood forecast remains then I expect significant calls for evacuations near & along our coast. Watches & warnings are also likely for our area by Tuesday PM. 
8.) The storm will be accelerating as it moves past us so I think this is at most a Thursday daytime event & then it's out. 
The next set of new model data comes out between 2-3pm. Look for an update after that.

author: Greg Dee
text: I'm becoming more concerned about the surge threat from this system. Don't believe any specific surge forecasts just yet but if you live in a flood zone, check for updates often.  ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text: Tropical storm conditions, at least, are likely in our area on Thursday. Here is the most likely arrival time for tropical storm winds in our area on Thursday.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Morning GFS & EURO data are in. Landfall location is almost identical: Taylor County (Florida Big Bend). This really lines up with many other models. It's interesting & reassuring that there so much agreement on the track.
The big difference is the intensity. The GFS is really putting out a very strong hurricane while the EURO is much weaker, & honestly more in-line with other models who are closer to Cat 2 intensity versus only a couple who go into Cat 3 or Cat 4. Unfortunately, this is the least reliable part of a storm forecast so I don't want to read into this either way. It's better to expect something stronger & prepare for it only to get something that's actually weaker. It's much more difficult to react the other way around. 
This track does still carry on BIG surge concern I think anyone that's flooded with previous storms in the last 10 years in our area should absolutely plan on seeing flooding with this one. Flooding is very hard to forecast but there are certainly indications that this one could be worse for some than any storm in the last several years if the track & intensity plan out as these models think. Again, much better idea to over-prepare for flooding only to come back to a dry property that's been secured than to come back to an unexpectedly flooded one where many more things are impacted than could have been.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Here is the #1 reason why this storm could be such a big water mover. It's forecast to be HUGE. Tropical storm force winds will extend 260 miles east of the center & 160 miles west. 
420 miles wide! 
This thing has the potential to move A LOT OF WATER. 
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text: PTC #9 Update:  Here is the latest track on what will become Helene. NHC now predicts Cat 3 before landfall. Notice, they did NOT move the track to the East at this point. Obviously, the Bay Area is hoping we don't see that bump to the East. Lots of analysis on the way. We're on LIVE on ABC Action News and updates on Facebook as well.

author: Greg Dee
text: Quick chat about PTC 9 & its impacts on our area. Feel free to ask questions! I cannot speak to the specific conditions in any specific town.

author: Greg Dee
text: 11pm Update: Still not quite Helene. No changes to track or intensity. NHC still believes it will be Cat 3 before landfall. Again, the system is not organized yet. Once it does, there could still be a relocation of the center which could change the ultimate track. This is covered in our discussions today. We'll update any changes overnight

author: Greg Dee
text: Flood watches have been issued for parts of Central Florida & Tampa Bay Wed PM to Fri AM. Many will get 3-6", especially close to the coast. Locally heavy amounts over 10" are possible.

author: Greg Dee
text:
TROPICS | TUE 5 AM UPDATE
So much new info with this update! Please read.
1.) No change in track. Landfall still looking likely in the Florida Big Bend late Thursday, maybe in Taylor County. 
2.) The NHC did bump up the intensity a little now calling for a Cat 3, major hurricane west of Tampa Bay at 2pm on Thursday & landfall as a Cat 3 in the Big Bend as well. Most of the models cluster around Cat 2/3 intensity in the NE Gulf, Cat 4 is not impossible at this point. 
3.) The core of hurricane winds is likely only to extend 30-40 miles away from the eye so no one in the Tampa Bay area is likely to see sustained hurricane-force winds if this track holds. Gusts to hurricane force will be possible along the coast.  
4.) Hurricane watches have now been issued for the Florida west coast from Englewood to the Big Bend. Tampa Bay is included in these watches. Watches indicate that hurricane conditions are POSSIBLE in the next 48 hours. I expect our coast to be upgraded to hurricane warnings tomorrow even though most of us will not get into full hurricane conditions.
5.) We will likely see sustained strong tropical storm winds along our coast. Anyone west of I-75 may get into a period of sustained winds above 55 mph Thursday PM. Tropical storm watches have been issued east into Polk County. Hurricane watches have been issued for the interior of the Nature Coast.
6.) Winds will not be an issue until Thursday. This is just a Thursday storm. Tropical storm winds will develop along the Suncoast Thursday morning & spread north through the day. Strong tropical storm winds will impact coastal counties only Thursday afternoon & evening.
7.) The 5 AM advisory includes the first look at the official NHC surge forecast for the Florida west coast. The Suncoast may see water rises of 4-7' above normal tides while Tampa Bay & the Pinellas coast may see 5-8'. Surge levels may top 6-10' across the Nature Coast. Parts of the big bend may see surge levels above 10-15 ft. 
Surge forecasts from some of the models have come down a little since yesterday but that could be because of intensity forecast fluctuations. We are still expecting significant surges along the coast. Evacuations will likely be ordered today. If you are ordered to evacuate please take that order seriously & do so. Water from the surge will be the most damaging thing our area will see from this storm. 
8.) Rainfall will likely range in the 3-6" range with some places locally getting 10"+. Inland flooding from heavy rain will absolutely be a possibility, especially where some of the heaviest rain bands will set up. Unfortunately, it's very difficult to predict exactly where that will be. If you're near a body of water, including a creek or river, please monitor water levels throughout the storm & be ready to move. If you've flooded in a storm before, you may flood in this one & may want to get out of the way before the rain even begins.       
More updates to come through the day. Time to prep folks, Thursday will be a stormy day.

author: Greg Dee
text:
I've gotten some questions about how the forecast for this storm compares to Idalia last year. The current forecast is on the left, Idalia's peak forecast is on the right. Right now the amounts either match that forecast or exceed it. 
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text:
As far as Tampa Bay, if the storm stays on-track, sustained hurricane winds will stay in the Gulf the whole time. There will however be a window from Thursday afternoon to Thursday evening when winds may be sustained above 58 mph near the coast. Gusts in the dark orange may exceed hurricane force at times. 
East of I-75 tropical storm winds are also likely but not quite as strong. Here winds will likely not reach anywhere near hurricane strength. 
Whether you're closer to the coast & getting strong tropical storm winds or farther east where winds will be lighter, all areas should prepare for sct'd power outages due to down tree branches & trees Thursday. For some these outages will last into the weekend.

author: Greg Dee
text: Here is an in-depth update on all the new info on PTC 9, what will be Helene later today. This includes hour-by-hour forecast for the Tampa Bay area.

author: Greg Dee
text: Today and tomorrow look great for any preps. Mostly sunny and hot today with an isolated PM shower. Wednesday we'll see some sct'd rain but we won't get into any rain bands along the coast until the evening. Winds will not be an issue through Wed PM.

author: Greg Dee
text: Winds for Tampa Bay will not be the main issue with this storm. Most will see sustained winds of tropical strength with some stronger gusts. Our in-house GRAF model keeps all the strong hurricane-force winds out over the Gulf as Helene passes Tampa Bay. The Big Bend of Florida will get most of the strong winds with this one up into north Florida & South Georgia.

author: Greg Dee
text:
NEW PTC 9 is now tropical storm Helene. Hurricane hunter aircraft have found the storm is now a tropical storm with winds of 45 mph. Pressure continues to trend down & is now at 1000 MB. 
1.) Track overall remains the same though there have been a couple of minor changes. Some of the models are decidedly back to the west a little this morning. This has caused the NHC to nudge their forecast a little west too closer to Tallahassee versus Cedar Key. This back & forth may continue or this could be the beginning of a trend. We'll just have to see what subsequent updates do.
2.) Intensity forecast remains the same. There may be an argument for a stronger system if it does trend west as it would be over warmer/deeper water compared to a track closer to the Florida west coast. This difference however is small. Forecast still calls for a major hurricane at landfall.
3.) With this new update there has been a significant change in the wind forecast for the west coast of Florida. This new update now keeps all of the hurricane & strong tropical storm winds (above 58 mph) out over the Gulf with much of the Florida peninsula seeing winds of 39-58 mph sustained along with higher gusts. Obviously farther north into the Big Bend there would absolutely be hurricane-force winds near & to the right of the point of landfall on Thursday afternoon/evening. 
4.) No major changes to watches with the addition of tropical storm warnings to the Keys as they will get the storm first.
5.) No change to the max possible surge forecast as of this update.

author: Greg Dee
text: We now have tropical storm Helene. Here’s all the new information with the 11 AM update in a quick 90 second video.

text:
Pasco county schools OPEN Wednesday & CLOSED Thursday. Sorry kids! 🤣
From PCSD: 
We want to inform you about important updates due to the upcoming storm expected to impact our county on Thursday. Pasco County Emergency Management has requested that we open five schools as emergency shelters. Here are the details regarding school closures and activities:

author: Greg Dee
text:
Tue 2 PM thoughts
1.) Not much has changed with the modeling for this system. We've certainly seen our share of systems with some wild models swings from day to day. Helene isn't one of those. 
2.) Track guidance continues to show the Big Bend of Florida. At times it's closer to Wakulla county and others it bounces back toward Taylor or Dixie counties. That's normal and a single jump in one direction or another doesnteqn anything has changed. These model forecasts are not that precise, especially days out, so the consistency here has certainly been reassuring. 
3.) The intensity forecast also has not changed much. As typical, there is a spread among the models, but the average has always been around strong Cat 2 / weak Cat 3 for days now & that continues today. 
4.) Helene is far from a hurricane right now. It does have a small, well defined center but that center is exposed with little in the way of organized thunderstorm activity around it. That's typical for a storm of this intensity. These things take time.

author: Greg Dee
text: Unfortunately, Helene is starting to get that "look". No changes to track expected when the new advisory drops around 5. We'll have it for you along with our thoughts. As always, the team is here for you 24/7. It's how we cover hurricanes at ABC Action News.

author: Greg Dee
text: Hurricane Warnings are now in effect from Pasco County Northward. Tropical Storm Warnings for the rest of the area. This will be mostly a daytime event for the Bay Area with winds strongest Thursday afternoon and evening.

author: Greg Dee
text: 5pm Advisory: Tropical Storm Helene now has winds of 50 mph. The track is still rock solid among models. We always look for last minute changes, but at this point, the odds of a direct strike in our area in becoming less likely. Helene is a large storm however, and impacts will include winds gusting to nearly hurricane force and a Storm Surge Warning is now in effect as well. Updates 24/7. We will be posting our updated thoughts shortly and the latest right now on ABC Action News.

author: Greg Dee
text:
5 PM advisory has the following changes to watches and warnings:
CHANGES WITH THIS ADVISORY:
A Storm Surge Warning has been issued from Flamingo to Indian Pass,
Florida, including Tampa Bay and Charlotte Harbor.
A Hurricane Warning has been issued from Anclote River to Mexico
Beach, Florida.
A Tropical
Storm Warning has been issued for the Gulf coast of Florida from
Flamingo northward to Anclote River, including Tampa Bay, and west
of Mexico Beach to the Walton/Bay County Line.
A Tropical Storm Watch has been issued for the east coasts of
Florida and Georgia from the Palm Beach/Martin County Line northward
to the Savannah River, and for Lake Okeechobee.
-----------------------------------------
No major changes to track and intensity with this update. This is not a surprise since the afternoon model updates really hadn't changed either. This is a good sign as it indicates Helene may not be a storm that surprises. Let's hope it stays that way. 
There have also not been any changes to the surge forecast for the Florida coast with Tampa Bay still expecting a 5-8 ft. surge. 
The chances of seeing tropical storm winds along the Florida was coast are now above 60% indicating they are likely. Tropical storm winds are winds of 39-73 mph. Gusts may be stronger than this right along the water. 
Helene is starting to look a whole lot more like a tropical system. Thunderstorms are developing all around the storm. I suspect that tomorrow we will see a very different system emerging into the Gulf, quite possibly even a hurricane. 
You'll see a lot of "weather excitement" about the storm on social media tomorrow. Just remember, everything is going according to plan so far. Rapid strengthening is absolutely expected with this system. It's a tropical storm now & likely will be a major hurricane in 24-36 hours. Don't get caught up in the emotion. Respect the awe, but remember that we are ready & we have time to prepare and react. 
We got this! ✌️
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text:
Take a look at this time series of Helene's winds based on the latest hurricane center forecast issued Tue Sept 24 at 5 pm. The maps show the storm's position at 4 pm, 7 pm & 10 pm Thursday. 
Though tropical storm-force winds will impact the majority of the Florida peninsula, strong tropical storm winds (orange) will stay out in the Gulf & only impact the Big Bend region.  Hurricane-force winds (red) will stay even farther out in the Gulf & will only be felt near the point of landfall Thursday evening.  These hurricane winds will only impact Florida. Tropical storm winds however will likely make it into southeast Alabama & definitely into South Georgia Thursday night into Friday where power outages are possible.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Take a look at this. This is our in-house computer model called the GRAF. It runs very often throughout the day constantly updating the forecast. This image show the time of maximum wind gusts with Helene around the Tampa Bay region on Thursday. 
It looks like the worst time for wind will be 3 pm to 9 pm for Tampa Bay & surrounding areas. East of I-75 winds are likely to gust 40-60 mph leading to sct'd power outages. West of I-75 winds could gust 60-70 mph leading to a higher chance of power loss. After about 9 pm winds will begin to diminish quickly south to north & the whole wind event will likely be over after midnight Friday morning leaving us with hurricane-free weather on Friday (there will still be some sct'd rain).

author: Greg Dee
text:
With wind gusts over 50 mph on Thursday all across central Florida, power outages will be possible. Right now, the latest model data from our system indicates a low to moderate chance of power outages across central & west-central Florida Thursday. These would most likely be due to large tree branches falling on power lines. In some cases, strong winds & saturated soils would also lead to downed trees causing additional & more extensive outages. Isolated tornadoes are not captured in this forecast & could cause additional, though very isolated, issues. 
The safest locations in Florida from outages will be communities along the east coast of Florida on the I-95 corridor.

author: Greg Dee
text: WIND | Great info here! Here is an hour-by-hour look at winds across central Florida and Tampa Bay on Thursday. Strongest winds will be 2 pm to 10 pm. The storm is done after midnight into Friday AM.

author: Greg Dee
text:
What about the winds & power outage potential in your area? Those answers, right here, in 90 seconds. 
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text: Wind gusts of 60-70 mph are likely across much of the Bay Area on Thursday afternoon. We can lose power in an afternoon tstorm. I would expect rather widespread outages for awhile Thursday afternoon and evening and Helene passes by to our West.

author: Greg Dee
text: 11pm update: Helene still has winds of 60 mph. No real change to the track. A few models are trying to nudge the track a bit East, but most are keeping the track locked in. Either way, NHC expects Helene to make landfall as a powerful Cat 3 hurricane with winds of 120 mph. Updates on ABC Action News and of course, we're on it right through landfall. We're here for you 24/7.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Our in-house computer model, GRAF, is definitely on the east side of some of the model tracks. Though it's not necessarily the track that will happen, it does show what a jog closer to the west coast of Florida will mean in terms of winds.
The strongest winds (no matter where this tracks) around Tampa Bay and the Florida west coast are most likely Thursday evening. Though areas east of I-75 will likely see gusts to tropical storm strength, those west of I-75 will likely see gusts into hurricane strength, especially along and west of the highway 19 corridor. This will cause power outages during the storm

author: Greg Dee
text: Similar to Debby, the east side of Helene, where we will be, will carry the threat of isolated tornadoes in its outer rain bands. The number of these is impossible to predict. Some storms produce many while others hardly any at all. We'll watch every rain band today and tomorrow closely.

author: Greg Dee
text:
WED 5 AM UPDATE
Lots of BIG info here with the latest update. 
1.) Helene is a very different storm from where it was yesterday at this time. It's organized, it's strengthening and the pressure continues to drop as the winds increase. We could have a hurricane in the next 6-12 hours. A major hurricane is possible in 24-36 hours.
2.) The latest track from the hurricane center nudges the storm a little to the west. This is an interesting move as the EURO, GFS, and some of our spaghetti models are all a little east of the NHC official track now. I wouldn't read too much into this. This "windshield wiper" effect of going west then east then west is normal as the NHC really likes the average of the models so as new runs come in and the average changes, the NHC will nudge their forecast in that direction. 
Remember, the storm WILL NOT follow the center line of any one forecast. It wobbles west to east and its path will not be a straight line. The landfall location will likely be CLOSE to where the NHC is indicating but it will not be at the exact point that any one update puts the center at. At this point, wobbles make only a tiny difference 
3.) The NHC has upped the intensity to 120 mph west of Tampa Bay on Thursday afternoon. A track a little farther west puts it over warmer, deeper waters, thus increasing the possibility of a stronger storm.  More of the models are now also into Cat 3 intensity so I think they're just following the trends here. 
4.) On this track the strongest core of winds remains out in the Gulf, at least per the NHC forecast. I do want to caution though that this is only if the NHC forecast verifies exactly. Out in-house GRAF model has been pretty insistent on a landfall closer to Cedar Key. If that were to happen then gusts along the Florida west could reach hurricane strength in that 70-80 mph range with a brief period of sustained winds around 60 mph right at the water. These strongest wind gusts would basically be in the Highway 19 corridor and points west. Gusts of 60-70 mph seem very possible Highway 19 to I-75 and then gusts of 50-60 mph east of I-75 with lesser winds the farther east you go toward I-95.
5.) The NHC has not made any changes to the surge forecast. The surge is more of a product of just a large storm pushing around a ton of water. Yes, stronger on-shore winds would bring in more water but even if wind is less, a water rise is still likely because of the shallow nature of the eastern Gulf and the shape of the coast. 
6.) Rainfall is an interesting variable with this system. First, fresh water flooding from rainfall seems less likely with Helene compared to Debby for two reasons. 
a.) The storm is moving faster.
b.) The storm is much stronger which pulls a lot of the heaviest rain closer to the center.
Still, you have to figure, that there will be rain bands along the coast and at least a period of solid rain Thu PM somewhere west of I-75. East of I-75 the rain may be much more sct'd and the formation of a solid rain band here is not a guarantee. This could be a situation in which some towns in the interior get 6" of rain from this because a rain bands set up right over them while 5 miles away folks get 1" of rain the whole storm. Tricky forecast for sure. 
That's a lot to think about but overall not much change from where we were yesterday. As time goes on we get more granular info on the Dee-tails of what we'll see, but the overall picture hasn't really changed. Helene will be strong, its impacts will be felt, but it will also be quick. The storm is done for our entire area shortly after midnight on Friday. Friday looks like a pretty normal day, just a little breezy. 
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text: Helene is well on its way to becoming a hurricane later today. Here is a detailed view of the impacts on Florida with a special focus on Tampa Bay.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Two days ago the NHC inundation model for surge was showing A LOT of red & orange. As more & more data on the storm begins to come in, we're getting a more clear & realistic picture of what we're most likely to see in terms of flooding.
Today the NHC inundation map shows more yellow & orange indicating inundation levels of 3-6 ft across much of Pinellas & Hillsborough counties.
Red areas (9 ft.+ of inundation) don't really appear on this map until you get to areas north of Clearwater Beach. Coastal areas of Pinellas, Pasco, Hernando & Citrus counties will see the highest threat from surge with higher water levels the farther north you go. The worst surge flooding in our area will definitely be in Citrus County & Crystal River. Do not stay if you are in these areas. Move to higher, drier ground.        
The maps below show the NHC SLOSH surge model for Citrus, Hernando, Pasco, Pinellas, Hillsborough, Manatee & Sarasota counties. These images are current as of Wed, Sept 25 at 7am. For updates maps go to: https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/.../graphics_at4.../094338.shtml...

author: Greg Dee
text:
Sunrise at Ballast Point in Tampa this morning. The calm before the storm.

author: Greg Dee
text: NEW | Helene is intensifying quickly. Pressure is down 6 mb since the last update and winds are at 70 mph. Helene will be a hurricane soon as it moves into the southern Gulf of Mexico.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Surge and tides are related. A 6 ft surge at low tide is obviously less impactful than a 6 ft. surge at high tide. Tides vary quite a bit by location. Here is St. Pete for tomorrow. Low tide happens around 6 pm Thu evening. I think until then surge is not a huge issue. The problem is after that as we're heading toward a midnight high tide and begin a strong on-shore flow. This is when the surge could be an issue across our area, after dark. 
Look up the tide for your area here: 
https://tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov

author: Greg Dee
text: St. Pete tide gauge showing tides running about 1 ft. above predicted levels. Is anyone else seeing higher-than-usual tides? That's pretty amazing since the storm is over 500 miles away from Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text: For once, rainfall with this storm may be our lowest threat of all the issues the storm may bring. Though 3-6" is possible area-wide, all of the model data seems to indicate the lower end of this range is more likely for most.

author: Greg Dee
text: Our in-house computer model, the GRAF, is a little closer to our coast with the track compared to the NHC so these gusts are higher than forecast, but this is a good general idea of when the winds will be worst across west-central Florida tomorrow. It looks like 2pm to 10pm will be the most-stormy period.

author: Greg Dee
text: Helene is intensifying & should be a hurricane later today. This information is current as of the Wed 5am NHC track update.

author: Greg Dee
text: 11am update: Helene is now a hurricane moving NNW at 10mph, expected to make landfall Thursday evening in the FL panhandle as a major hurricane.

author: Greg Dee
text: It's interesting to see the NHC favor the western models in its last 11 AM track update. This is definitely not a good situation for Tallahassee as it places the city potentially in the eye or the much stronger right-front quadrant of the eye wall. Anyone in the Leon County area needs to prepare for widespread, long-duration, power outages. Tree cover is dense in this area & significant tree damage could be a result of this type of track.

author: Greg Dee

author: Greg Dee
text: Even before a storm, we make time for us. I needed this moment for my sanity and the peace it put in my heart. ❤️

author: Greg Dee
text:
NEW | Surge forecast increased to 12-18' in the Florida Big Bend. 8-12' in the Nature Coast south to Anclote. 
RUN FROM THE WATER. 
Hurricane Helene is a dangerous storm with the potential to produce life-threatening surge along the Big Bend & Nature Coasts. This is a serious storm.

author: Greg Dee
text:
The latest satellite image clearly shows Helene is building an eye well. This is a healthy, strengthening hurricane that is well on its way to become much stronger in the next 24 hours. 
Respect this storm. If you are told to evacuate because of sure, heed the warnings. 
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text:
Many of you have asked how the NHC track compares to the EURO & GFS. Here is the comparison. The 11 am NHC track is west of both the GFS & EURO who are both consistent in bringing the storm into Taylor County, Florida, which is southeast of Tallahassee on the east side of Apalachee Bay.
It will be interesting how the next NHC track, due out at 5 pm, responds to this difference. 
I'll keep you guys updated!

author: Greg Dee
text:
My co-worker Larissa Scott TV captured this image of me as I was ready to head home this afternoon after a long shift at work.
The last few days have been very stressful for all of us. The anxiety of not knowing everything about the impending storm truly weighs on us all as we struggle to make the best decisions to keep ourselves & the ones around us that we love so much, safe. 
I feel the weight of the decisions you all need to make & how much you hang on to each word in my updates to help make those difficult choices. I cannot choose for you, but I know you're going to do what's best. 
I think about this every time I make a post or step in front of the camera on ABC Action News. It's a responsibility I take seriously. My job is fun on the 360 days a year we see sun. But for a handful of those days, I recognize the weight of my responsibility & feel the true calling of what I chose to do as a career. 
To help deal with the stress, & to keep my mind clear, I've been biking to & from work each morning. It's only a short 18 minutes. But for that period of time, I'm forced to put my phone down, move my legs, focus on my balance, both in my body & mind, & feel the fresh air around me. I arrive home or at work with a clearer mind & a renewed feeling of purpose. It's really made a difference. 
I hope that you have my "bike ride" equivalent in your life that you can also take part in this week, before, & after the storm. Remember to take care of not just your physical health but also your mental health to keep you & those around you safe & strong during this stormy time. 
We got this Tampa Bay! I will be there for you through every part of this storm.

author: Greg Dee
text:
A little over 24 hours from landfall & the similarity between the GFS and EURO in the Helene forecast is incredible. 
The agreement is strong both in position tomorrow evening AND intensity. The models are locked in. 
Now, will Helene do what it's predicted so we'll to do?
The models say Taylor County, Florida landfall.

author: Greg Dee
text:
BREAKING | 5 PM Wed update increases landfall intensity to Cat 4 in the Florida Big Bend Thursday evening. Helene is a dangerous storm with current winds of 85 mph & is forecast to strengthen over the next 24 hours. Any last-minute preparation in the Big Bend needs to be completed by Thursday morning. Anyone living at the coast in the surge zone needs to get out of the way of this storm. 
Helene is forecast to be a life-threatening hurricane bringing very high surges to the northeast Gulf.
For the west coast of Florida, south of Cedar Key, this does not change the forecast much. We're still expecting tropical storm conditions for Tampa Bay with the potential of gusts to hurricane force closer to the water. Surge numbers around Tampa Bay remain the same as 5-8 ft. but may not be closer to the higher number.

author: Greg Dee
text: Hurricane Helene 5pm Update: Not good. Winds are now up to 85 mph and is expected to become a Category 4 hurricane before landfall in the Big Bend/Panhandle area. For the Bay Area, we do NOT expect sustained hurricane force winds but we will see some gusts nearly that high later on Thursday. Surge is still our biggest concern with water rise after the storm passes us. Again, we are not talking about rainfall flooding. Surge is water pushed ashore from the Gulf. If you don't live on or near the water, you won't see issues from the surge. Updates 24/7.

author: Greg Dee
text: Surge forecast for Tampa Bay and Nature Coast remains the same. The NHC however has INCREASED the surge forecast to 15-20' between Carabelle and the Suwannee River. This is an incredibly high, & dangerous surge.

author: Greg Dee
text:
I know there's been a ton of talk about the surge for the Big Bend with this story. It's definitely scary. But I do want to focus on the Bay area now where surge will be much lower.
In fact, take a look at the surge forecast below for Tampa Bay & then north into Citrus. The numbers for Pinellas & Hillsborough County have gone down. There will still be some water but I'm seeing no red & even much less orange on this map. In fact, I'm seeing a lot of blue (1 ft of water above the ground) & yellow (3 ft. of water above the ground) which is a huge improvement over what we saw on this map earlier in the week. I'll continue to monitor this overnight & tomorrow morning 
Yes, flooding here is still a threat & if you're in a zone that was called to evacuate, you should absolutely leave. But the flooding that we see here will be significantly lower as compared to the Big Bend.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Let's talk about wind in the Bay area. The hurricane center track puts the storm 140 miles west of Tampa Bay. The EURO & GFS take the storm closer to Tampa Bay but still 100 miles to our west. 
From the forecast, when Helene is a Cat 4 storm, the hurricane winds will extend 50 miles southeast of the center. The Cat 4 winds will only be 5-10 miles from the eye.  This means that in the NHC scenario & closest tracking forecast from the EURO & GFS models, no one in our area from Citrus down to Sarasota counties will see winds sustained at hurricane force. 
The data indicates that most will see gusts in the 60-70 mph range west of I-75 & generally gusts less than 60 mph east of I-75. These wind gusts are strong enough to down tree branches, trees & powerlines. They are NOT strong enough to cause widespread structural damage. In the last two storms that came through our area with such winds, any serious damage that occurred (outside of flooding) was in areas where winds pushed trees or downed large tree branches onto cars or structures. Most of you WILL NOT see that.
For the majority of folks around the Bay area, tomorrow will be a cloudy, warm-to-hot day, with periods of some rain & periods of gusty winds. But overall, for most of you, tomorrow will not be bad or horrible. It'll definitely be a day to stay in, wait things out, & then resume normal life on Friday.
Hope this helps ease your mind about the wind. See you guys tomorrow morning. ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay Good Morning Tampa Bay starts live at 4:30 am!

author: Greg Dee
text: 11pm Hurricane Helene Update: Helene still has winds of 85 mph. The NHC forecasts Helene will have Cat 4 winds of 130 mph af landfall in the Big Bend area Thursday evening. No changes to our forecast. Sustained hurricane winds are not expected in our area. Surge will be a major issue for our coastal areas. As always, we'll update overnight and 24/7 until the threat has ended. The latest at 11 on ABC Action News.

author: Greg Dee
text: Whew! The "I" storm has formed in the Atlantic. It's moving away from the United States and will pose no threat to Florida or the rest of the US.

author: Greg Dee
text: Sct'd rain and some thunderstorms in these outer rain bands of Helene may produce isolated tornadoes at any time today. The severe threat is level 2 out of 5. We'll be tracking these throughout the afternoon. Tornadoes from hurricanes are usually weak and very short-lived

author: Greg Dee
text:
HELENE 5 AM UPDATE
Here's what's new overnight:
1.) Helene is now stronger. Pressure is down 5mb since the last update & winds are up to 90 mph. Helene continues to intensify. There are indications based on its satellite appearance that Helene is developing that classic hurricane look with a well-defined eye surrounded by a large area of heavy rain & wind. The NHC expects Helene to become a major, category 3 hurricane in about 12 hours & continue intensifying in the following 6 hours as it approaches a Big Bend landfall. Helene may be a Cat 3 or Cat 4 storm on landfall.
2.) The storm is beginning to turn toward Florida. Since yesterday the motion has been mainly to the north. Now the storm is moving NNE. We'll have to watch closely how much eastward motion the storm gets into today. The more it heads east the stronger the impacts on the Florida west coast & the rest of the Florida peninsula. 
3.) Comparing the NHC track to some of the models, the NHC track is definitely in the center of the spaghetti plot & WEST of our two main computer models, the GFS & EURO who for the last 24 hours have been in unusual agreement that Helene will make landfall in Taylor County. Surprisingly both also indicate the same intensity too, a major hurricane. I can't remember such consistent agreement between the two over such a long period in previous storms. Even our own in-house model, the GRAF, which was a bit too far east yesterday, is now also indicating a Taylor County landfall. A landfall in Taylor County would save Tallahassee & Leon County from the worst of the storm. Tallahassee would still get significant wind but the most damaging winds are in the NE quadrant which would pass east of the city in a Taylor County landfall scenario. 
4.) There have been no changes to the tropical storm & hurricane watches & warnings.
5.) There has been no change to the surge forecast. The NHC is still forecasting a life-threatening surge along the Nature & Big Bend coasts. No one should remain in locations where a surge of 8' or more is forecast. Water, especially moving water, can be extremely destructive, moving & destroying homes & buildings in its way. It's still not too late to evacuate these areas this morning as the surge is not expected until later this evening & tonight. The surge forecast has not changed for Tampa Bay & areas south. Flooding is still likely in these areas though the actual levels will depend on storm intensity & how close the storm comes to land. Low tides are generally early in the evening with a high tide around midnight in the Tampa Bay area.
6.) The strongest winds with the storm will be located near the point of landfall & could extend up to 30 miles from the center on the east & southeast side. West of the eye the winds will be much lighter as this is the weaker side of the storm. The east side of the storm, the stronger side, will bring tropical storm winds to much of the Florida peninsula starting this morning across Southwest Florida spreading north through the day. The winds south of the Big Bend however will stay at tropical storm strength all day with the strongest winds not arriving until after 4pm & lasting until 10 or 11 pm. Gusts over 60 mph are possible generally west of I-75 this evening. Right at the coast gusts may reach near hurricane strength with the most likely places to see that from central Pinellas County coast north into the Nature Coast. 
7.) Sct'd rain has already been moving through the area with a large rain band over the Gulf slowly moving on-shore across SW Florida along the west coast. This rain band needs to be watched carefully as any rain storms today could also carry a tornado threat. We are in a level 2 out 5 risk of severe storms today. We'll be watching for isolated tornadoes now through the storm's landfall tonight. 
As far as rainfall amounts, because of the sct'd nature of the rain across the Florida peninsula, widespread freshwater flooding from rain is not expected. Some areas may get enough rain to see some flooding but those should be more isolated than they were with Debby earlier in the season. Even up toward the Big Bend, rainfall totals will not be excessive as the storm will be moving faster than previous systems.

author: Greg Dee
text: A tornado watch has been issued for our entire area until 8 PM. Heavy cells of rain with isolated tornadoes are possible throughout the afternoon. These tornadoes usually are short-lived.

author: Greg Dee
text: Winds are still pretty reasonable. Outer rain bands will bring higher gusts at times today, but the highest winds are still expected along the coast this afternoon and evening. Gusts between 50-60mph will be possible at that time.

author: Greg Dee
text:
This is the tide gauge at Port Manatee. It's very telling of what's happening now & may be a good indication of what's to come tonight. 
The dark blue line shows the typical water levels. Peaks are high tides, and troughs are low tides. As you can see, we're near a high tide now &  will see a low tide at around 5pm with another high tide around midnight. 
The red line is where the water level has actually been measured. Notice that the actual level is tracking pretty close to the forecasted level. This suggests that the model is a good approximation of what the water will do later tonight.
Tonight is what worries me the most. The forecast level at Port Manatee is over 6 ft. with a high tide around midnight. This is major flooding for this area. Surge levels as high as 8 ft. are possible in the northern part of Tampa Bay tonight. This surge of water would happen quickly & in almost all cases, after dark. If the power is out where you are, you may not see the water coming. This is why it's so important to get out of the way of the water if you live in a flood zone. Don't take a chance. Sleep in a dry place where you don't have to worry. 
ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text:
SUN OCT 6 | 7 AM UPDATE
Big day for Milton updates today. By the end of the day watches are likely for portions of the Florida coast. We may also get our first glimpse at some surge forecast data. 
Check out each map for a caption explaining what you're seeing. I will not reference every map here. 
NHC aircraft is en route to the storm now. Missions will be flown regularly & data will be updated in the models. 
We are 72 hours from landfall. This far out the average error of the best forecast (the NHC forecast) is 105 miles. The track is not set in stone. 
Here's what's happening now:
1.) Milton continues to intensify & organize. Winds will increase with subsequent updates & Milton will become a hurricane later today or early on Monday. The storm is not moving very quickly. This should persist for the next 1-2 days before a rapid increase in forward speed begins on Tuesday as Milton is impacted by a frontal system to its north.
2.) TRACK | There have been no significant changes to the track from the models or from the NHC. Remember, the NHC track cone shows all possible locations of the CENTER of the storm at each time interval. The center of the storm stays in this cne 2/3 of the time. The cone is not unique to each storm, it's drawn based on past statistics. On this morning's map, if the storm goes into Naples, our area still sees impacts as the storm is much larger than its center. If it goes up toward Cedar Key will get impacts too. 
3.) SIZE | Milton will be a MUCH smaller storm than Helene. The current forecast from the NHC has tropical storm winds extending 120 mi. away from the eye of Milton on either side as it approaches the coast. For comparison, tropical storm winds extended 345 mi. from the eye of Helene as it approached the Big Bend. In 2004 Charley had tropical storm winds that only went out to 85 mi at landfall & hurricane winds went out only 25 miles. Milton will be closer in size to Charley than Helene.  
4.) SURGE | Smaller hurricanes move less water. Smaller hurricanes cause less widespread wind damage. There will be significant surge & wind damage near the point of landfall, but because the storm will be small, the most severe impacts will be highly concentrated in an area near the point of landfall & just to the right. The exact track the eye will take is crucial & unfortunately, no matter how many tracks you look at, no one will be able to pinpoint that track so precisely. 
5.) TAMPA BAY | Milton does pose a significant threat to Tampa Bay. A landfall in Pinellas or Pasco County would not be a good situation for those around the Bay. Southwest winds would push significant water into the Gulf. The levels would likely exceed anything we saw with Helene in areas that are at & south of the point of landfall. A landfall in Pinellas County would bring a reverse surge to areas to the north such as Pasco & Hernando. Track is everything for wind & surge. 
6.) INTENSITY | NHC is conservative with intensity. Milton will have free reign to intensify quickly through Tuesday. Some of the NHC's new models even intensify it significantly past cat 3. The issue is what happens as it approaches Florida. The environment around the storm will become much less supportive of quick strengthening. Forward speed will be increasing as Milton feels the impacts of a trough of low pressure to its north. Wind shear north of the storm will become more significant & there are indications that drier air will start to wrap in around the storm. Some of the models suggest that this environment will be so toxic to the storm that even as it moves away from Florida over warm Atlantic waters it could continue to weaken. There are honestly too many moving pieces in the intensity forecast in the last 24 hours before landfall. I think it's best to prepare for a Cat 3 or Cat 4 storm.
7.) TIMING | This is all Wednesday. Although we'll see periods of rain today, Monday & Tuesday, there will be no direct impacts from the storm through sunset Tuesday. The earliest reasonable time for the arrival of minimal tropical storm winds per the NHC forecast is around 8 pm on Tuesday. You'll have all day Tuesday to make last-minute preps before the storm. Other than some rain, Tuesday will be ok for all forms of travel. I would suspect all airlines will fly OUT on Tuesday but flights IN may be canceled for any airplane that is scheduled to stay in Tampa/St Pete overnight as airlines would not want their planes on the ground in the storm Wed AM. I DO NOT KNOW WHAT WILL HAPPEN TO YOUR FLIGHT (unless it's on Wed. All Wed flights will be canceled).
8.) WIND | Depending on intensity, Milton has the potential to cause much more wind damage in our area than Helene. Because of its increasing forward speed, wind damage will spread farther inland along & just to the right of the track. Again, the swath will be VERY narrow, but some of the winds may be very strong. Remember, the storm is forecast to have a total width of around 250 miles, but most of that will be tropical storm winds. Hurricane-force winds are only forecast to extend around 25 miles from the center. The majority of us WILL NOT see hurricane force winds. The PEAK winds will only be 5-10 miles from the eye. Unfortunately, that swath of strong winds is impossible to predict precisely. If the track doesn't change, I plan on boarding up my house on Tuesday. I live 2 miles from the Bay. 
9.) FINAL THOUGHTS | Run from the water, hide from the wind. If you live in a flood zone & are in an evacuation zone that gets called, please leave. I suspect the number of people running from the water in this storm will be MUCH higher than any previous storm in recent memory because of our Helene experience. That's a good thing but it could also mean that traffic may be an issue at some point. If you don't live in flood zone & are away from the coast, you can shelter in-place from the wind. The only structures that are unsafe are trailers, mobile homes & manufactured homes. These need to be evacuated no matter where they are if you're in the path of the storm. 
Milton poses a significant threat to our area. Today will be the final day of watching & tracking, by tomorrow morning we'll have to start acting & planning. Be patient, & approach this reasonably. Identify your risks & plan for not just the storm but also the AFTER. Some areas may experience significant, long-lasting, power loss. Internet & water may also not be available for several days. Even if your area does not sustain much damage, other than trees & tree branches, you may still be impacted by a lack of some of these services. Hopefully though, with a smaller hurricane, these services will be restored more rapidly following the storm. 
I'll keep yo updated 24/7.

author: Greg Dee

author: Greg Dee
text:
SUN OCT 6 | 1 PM UPDATE
Things are definitely trending in an interesting direction. Here's what new as of the 11 AM update from the NHC.
1.) Hurricane hunter aircraft have found that the center of Milton is farther south than they expected. They also found the storm is moving ESE instead of east. It's a small change but when you extrapolate that over 3 days of movement, it makes a HUGE difference for folks down the line as it would cause the track to shift farther south as well.
2.) The models are now picking up on this position change & the models are responding. Most of the spaghetti models have now shifted south of Tampa Bay. This is great news for anyone around the Bay as on the north side of the storm you'd get less wind & the wind would push water OUT of the way & into a Gulf, a reverse surge! We want that. Unfortunately, this also means that farther south the threat of a surge increases especially down into the Suncoast & Southwest Florida. Anyone from Sarasota to Naples needs to now follow this storm even more closely. Milton will be a much smaller than Helene so the area that will see the highest surge & the worst winds will be much smaller than it was with Helene & almost entirely to the right of the point of landfall. 
3.) The Hurricane center suggests that further shifts to the south in the track are possible in future updates. The hurricane center never goes "all in" on a model track change. They do the right thing by looking at trends over several updates & nudging their track in that direction. That's the right way to do it. 
4.) Hurricane hunter aircraft have found that Milton now has an eye wall. It's not a hurricane yet but will likely be soon. Now that the eye wall has formed, rapid strengthening is possible. A couple of these models bring this to Cat 4/5 north of  the Yucatan before some weakening occurs as it approaches Florida because of increasing upper-level winds around the storm (shear) & dry air coming in from the NW. Don't freak out if you see some BIG numbers from this storm in the next 24-48 hours. 
5.) As far as what anyone will see from the storm will really depend on the track of the eye because Milton is expected to be rather small. Hurricane winds may only extend 30 miles away from the eye in any direction & tropical storm winds may only reach out 130 miles away from the center. In Helene TS winds extended 345 miles. The "maximum sustained winds" you see listed on the graphics about the storm only occur within 5-10 miles of the eye. This means that only a small portion of our area will see hurricane-force winds. Those strongest winds are almost always on the right side of the eye as it's making landfall. If you're on the north side of the eye you'll get much less wind & if you're more than 50 miles away from the eye you'll likely just get tropical storm-force winds. 
The majority of the area will see winds equivalent to what we saw in Ian, Irma & Helene. Tropical storm winds with gusts over 60 mph for most. Only those right near the eye & to the right of the track near the eye will see any kind of nasty winds. Now, if you do get near the eye, nasty winds are absolutely possible as is damage from them. With the fast motion of this storm strong winds to the right of the eye can absolutely extend inland too, along the track. 
Can all this change? Oh yea! Wobbles, eye wall replacement cycles, the trough of low pressure to the north of Milton & its exact strength still play a role. We all need to prepare & we'll get a better idea of who exactly needs to be prepared when watches are issued along our coast & inland later today. This is why I'm updating so much. I want you to have the latest info. 
More updates to follow, 24/7/

author: Greg Dee
text:
SUN OCT 6 | 6 PM UPDATE
Some thoughts this evening before I head to bed:
1.) The NHC track shows little change for our area. Maybe a little farther north inland & a little farther south in the Gulf but the centerline is still about where it was at 11 am. 
2.) The spaghetti plot is now split with some models around the Bay & points north while a group of models is still eyeing Southwest Florida.
3.) The EURO & GFS are also no help with the GFS being back rather far north & the EURO bringing the storm into the Bay. 
4.) I cannot tell you what your area is going to get because I don't know where the eye is going to track since all the models are split. I can only say one thing: everyone in Tampa Bay will get tropical storm winds on Wednesday. I can't be more specific right now.
5.) The intensity forecast is interesting. The models have been so bullish with strengthening the storm to Cat 4 or 5 in the southern Gulf tomorrow & Tuesday that the NHC made a remark about it in their 5 PM update. They have increased the peak intensity to Cat 4 in the Gulf. Milton could even reach Cat 5 briefly. 
Beyond that the forecast is tricky & more optimistic for us. Upper-level winds (shear) will increase & Milton is forecast to weaken quickly as it approaches the west Florida coast. 
The NOAA hi-res HARF model shows Milton as a formidable storm Mon/Tue with a well-defined eye. But when the same system approaches our coast on Wed it looks VERY different. That eye is gone & the storm is lopsided with rain on the north side & dry it on the south & southwest. This would act to spread the winds of the storm over a larger area but also cause them to be overall weaker. The surge would not necessarily act the same way. The NHC will issue their forecast for surge tomorrow or late tonight when watches are posted. 
6.) Overall I can't say that anyone is "in or out" of this storm. The model data is not clear, the intensity forecast is complicated, & the shape & size of the storm is in question as it approaches. Hopefully, tomorrow will bring clearer answers. It usually does. 
See you in the AM!

author: Greg Dee
text:
MON OCT 7 AM UPDATE
Lots of new info from the NHC with this update.
1.) Milton continues to intensify as forecast. It is now a Cat 3 storm. It is a tiny hurricane with a small pin-hole eye. Hurricane-force winds only extend up to 25 miles away from the eye. 
2.) Hurricane watches have been issued for the west coast of Florida. Not everyone in this area will see hurricane conditions. Only those near & south of the eye will actually see hurricane-force winds. Outside of this area most will just see tropical storm winds during the storm, & lots of rain. 
3.) Everyone along the coast is under a surge watch. The surge forecast numbers are BIG. But, unlike Helene, the surge will not impact everyone. The highest suggest will likely only happen in place where & just to the south of, the eye makes landfall. North of the eye the wind will be off-shore pushing the water out into the Gulf & away from the coast. The worst case scenario is a landfall north of the Bay in Pinellas or Pasco Counties. This would cause a record-setting surge in the Bay that could be up to 5 ft. higher than what we saw with Helene. Of course, if the landfall is south of the Bay then the storm could push water out of the Bay & we'd see a reverse surge & a drop in water levels. Unfortunately at this point I cannot say where this point of landfall will be. 
4.) This is not Helene. There is a lot more uncertainty with this track. There will be changes, likely until almost the point of landfall. I understand this is incredibly frustrating & unnerving. I'm with you & feel the same way. There's a lot we don't know about the storm but we have lots of time to prepare. Take the time you need the next 2 days to prepare & plan. If you're in an evacuation zone because of a flood zone, listen to the order. Evacuate. If you're not in an evacuation zone, you're ok to shelter in your home. Be prepared for power loss but you'll most likely be ok. Only mobile & manufactured homes are unsafe. 
More to come soon. 
#milton #hurricanemilton

author: Greg Dee
text:
Monday, Oct 7 | 1 PM Update
Wow! What a morning for Milton! 
The storm has gone through the 3rd fastest intensification cycle (by wind speed) ever observed in the Atlantic Basin, only behind Felix (2007) & Wilma (2005). 
1.) Milton is a dangerous Cat 5 hurricane with winds of 160 mph. 
2.) The storm may brush the northern coast of the Yucatan but that will make little difference in its future track or intensity.
3.) Satellite presentation shows an impressive storm with a small pin-hole eye. The hurricane winds only extend 30 miles from the eye.
4.) The track should continue ESE & then turn more E & then ENE as a trough of low pressure approaches Milton from the NW on Tuesday. An increase in forward speed should also begin. Spaghetti plot is south of the NHC track but the NHC says they don't trust this & have maintained a track north of Tampa Bay on Wednesday evening. 
5.) The intensity of the storm over the next 36 hours will mainly be controlled by eyewall replacement cycles. This is the normal life cycle of a hurricane. The eyewall collapses & a new one develops. This can cause the intensity to go down & then come back up once a new eyewall develops. 
6.) As the storm approaches Florida strong upper-level winds will likely begin to impact Milton in a negative way, stretching the storm toward the northeast & gradually weakening it. Just how much it weakens before reaching the west coast of Florida is hard to pin down. It seems likely that Milton will weaken to at least a Cat 3 storm. 
This weakening trend does show up in ALL the model data (included in this post). What is worrying is that this weakening only begins 12-18 hours before landfall making this a very close call for the Florida west coast. 
7.) The surge forecast has not changed. That all these numbers will happen. The forecast is for the worst-case scenario & will only happen at & south of the point of landfall with the highest surges only 20-30 miles south of the eye. North of the eye the surge will be much lower as the winds blow off-shore.
8.) Mon & Tue are perfectly fine days for last-minute preps. There will be some showers around but no severe or dangerous weather. Even Wednesday morning, though wet & rainy, will likely be safe. It's after 2 PM on Wed that conditions will quickly go down-hill, depending on the speed of the storm. As of now, I'd say that 4pm is the cut-off for being out of a safe place on Wednesday. 
PERSONAL NOTE:
Milton needs to be taken seriously. The current forecast is the worst case track for Tampa Bay. A wobble or last-minute turn is still very possible, but it's too close to call so it's absolutely best to act & pretend like the worst will happen & be surprised with something much less. 
PLEASE evacuate if you're told to do so. Places that can flood in this storm will be told to evacuate, & they should. You do not however need to leave the state or even the area. It's ok to be here for the winds (unless you're in a mobile or manufactured home). That means you may only need to travel a couple miles to a shelter or friend's or family house to get into an elevated area away from the water. Please don't stay for this storm if you're told to evacuate. If you're not in an evacuation zone then you can stay & you will be ok. Just remember, following the storm, some areas will see widespread & possibly long-lasting power & water outages.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Potential impacts broken down from the US National Weather Service Tampa Bay Florida:
POTENTIAL IMPACTS
-----------------
* SURGE:
Prepare for life-threatening surge having possible catastrophic
impacts across Nature Coast, West-Central Florida, and Southwest Florida.
 Potential impacts in this area include:
    - Widespread deep inundation, with storm surge flooding greatly accentuated by powerful battering waves. Structural damage to buildings, with many washing away. Damage greatly compounded from considerable floating debris. Locations may be uninhabitable for an extended period.
    - Near-shore escape routes and secondary roads washed out or severely flooded. Flood control systems and barriers may become stressed.
    - Extreme beach erosion. New shoreline cuts possible.
    - Massive damage to marinas, docks, boardwalks, and piers.
      Numerous small craft broken away from moorings with many lifted onshore and stranded.
* WIND:
Prepare for life-threatening wind having possible devastating impacts across  Nature Coast, West-Central Florida, and Southwest Florida.
Potential impacts in this area include:
    - Structural damage to sturdy buildings, some with complete roof and wall failures. Complete destruction of mobile homes. Damage greatly accentuated by large airborne projectiles. Locations may be uninhabitable for weeks or months.
    - Numerous large trees snapped or uprooted along with fences and roadway signs blown over.
    - Many roads impassable from large debris, and more within urban or heavily wooded places. Many bridges, causeways, and access routes impassable.
    - Widespread power and communications outages.
* FLOODING RAIN:
Prepare for life-threatening rainfall flooding having possible
extensive impacts across Nature Coast, West-Central Florida, and Southwest Florida. Potential impacts include:
    - Major rainfall flooding may prompt many evacuations and rescues.
    - Rivers and tributaries may rapidly overflow their banks in
      multiple places. Small streams, creeks, canals, and ditches may become dangerous rivers. Flood control systems and barriers may become stressed.
    - Flood waters can enter many structures within multiple
      communities, some structures becoming uninhabitable or washed away. Many places where flood waters may cover escape routes.
     - Streets and parking lots become rivers of moving water with
 underpasses submerged. Driving conditions become dangerous. Many road and bridge closures with some weakened or washed out.
* TORNADOES:
Prepare for a tornado event having possible limited impacts across West Central and Southwest Florida. Potential impacts include:
    - The occurrence of isolated tornadoes can hinder the execution of emergency plans during tropical events.
    - A few places may experience tornado damage, along with power & communications disruptions.
    - Locations could realize roofs peeled off buildings, chimneys
      toppled, mobile homes pushed off foundations or overturned,
      large tree tops and branches snapped off, shallow-rooted trees knocked over, moving vehicles blown off roads, and boats pulled from moorings.

author: Greg Dee
text:
MILTON MON OCT 7 | EVENING UPDATE
Milton is a powerful Cat 5 hurricane this evening. Though the storm does pose the most serious threat to the Tampa Bay area that we've seen in decades, the storm will not be its current self when it's approaching our coast. 
1.) The latest track from the NHC makes no adjustment in position but does continue to slow the system down with landfall now not expected until maybe 1 am Thursday morning. 
2.) Because Milton is stronger than forecast now, it's going to be stronger at landfall with a Cat 3 intensity expected.
3.) The intensity forecast however is tricky as Milton is still forecast to enter an area of strong wind shear (Strong upper level winds) as it approaches the west coast of Florida 12-18 hours prior to landfall. The storm may look very intense even 24 hours before landfall but should then quickly begin to weaken as it enters this wind shear. The current intensity forecast calls for weakening. The model forecast included with this post has the approximate time of landfall marked. This is only for the Mon Oct 7 pm advisory track. Notice all the models are in weakening mode at this time. Just how much of that will happen will just need to be seen.
4.) It's important to note that not everyone will see hurricane-force winds with this storm. Most of us will see winds of tropical storm strength. The strongest, maximum sustained winds, will only occur within 2-5 miles of the eye. The hurricane force winds will only extend around 45 miles from the eye. Those in the path of these winds will see destructive winds & widespread power outages. This will be in a small swath & most will not be in it. 
5.) The surge forecast has been updated & increased. It is the worst-case scenario & is the biggest threat from Milton because water is more powerful & will occur over a much wider area than the strongest winds. If you are in an evacuation zone & are asked to leave you absolutely need to leave. Please go. Do not stay. You do not need to leave the area. You may just need to travel a couple of miles to a safer location. Don't leave the state. 
6.) You do not need to evacuate for wind unless you live in a mobile or manufactured home. Otherwise, you can shelter in place. Very few, if anyone really, will see winds that are strong enough to cause serious structural damage. Our homes are built for this. 
7.) Check out the graphics for wind arrival times. Tropical storm winds arrive late Wednesday morning but the serious strong winds don't get here until after 4 pm on Wednesday. The strongest hurricane winds arrive late Wednesday evening & Thursday morning (before 3am). The storm will then weaken & winds will go down as it continues to cross the state. The winds will be over for most of us by midday Thursday. 
Tampa Bay, we got this! Focus, plan & act. We still have all day Tuesday to make necessary preps & even the first half of Wednesday, though wet, will not be horrible. We've done this before, we've been here before, & we will be here after the storm, stronger than ever. Protect your family & your property. You know what to do. We'll be fine. This is not our first hurricane. 
See you in the morning!

author: Greg Dee
text: Monday evening Milton forecast & track discussion. One more day to prep. Milton is a serious threat but it's also one we can & know how to prepare for.

author: Greg Dee
text: 9pm Milton Update: Pressure is down to 897 making Milton the 5th most intense hurricane ever to form in the Atlantic. The eye is only FOUR miles wide. Hurricane force winds only extend about 25 miles out. As we said yesterday, small storms can rapidly strengthen (and weaken) faster than larger storms. Let's hope when it hits shear in the Northern Gulf later tomorrow the weakening will begin. Updates 24/7.

author: Greg Dee
text: Milton DOWN to 165. More than likely going thrown and ERC. (Eyewall Replacement Cycle). The inner eyewall collapses (and weakens the storm temporarily) while the new eyewall forms. It usually expands the wind field and makes the storm larger within 12 -24 hours. We will see. New track our shortly

author: Greg Dee
text: 11pm Hurricane Milton Update: Winds DOWN to 165 mph. This is due to what we call an ERC or Eyewall Replacement Cycle. A second eyewall forms outside the 1st one and the inside eyewall dies. The process temporarily weakens the hurricane. Unfortunately, the key word is temporarily. It expands the wind field and usually regains its strength. Either way, the track is about the same although the NHC insists model trends to the South appear unreliable. We will see. Either way, a destructive hurricane is coming to the West coast of Florida late Wednesday. The "cone of uncertainty" is still located between Cedar Key and Ft Myers. Updates 24/7

author: Greg Dee
text:
TUE OCT 8 AM MILTON UPDATE
Not many changes here. Here's what's new.
1.) Milton is going through an eyewall replacement cycle. The eyewall will rebuild today. This is a normal part of strong hurricanes & causes changes in intensity up or down.
2.) Milton has begun slowly moving north of east as forecast. Official movement is ENE as it makes a slow turn toward Florida. 
3.) NHC keeps the landfall intensity at Cat 3. There is a TON of uncertainty about this part of the storm at landfall. The NHC has admitted they are on the high-end of the intensity forecast. This is the right way to do it. Prepare for a stronger storm & get surprised by a weaker one. That keeps more people safe. Most models drop this to a strong Cat 2 or even a Cat 1 at landfall. It's definitely possible. 
4.) Landfall time keeps jumping around. Last night it was late Wed now it's early Thursday. Either way, it looks like the two hours around midnight Wed into Thursday is the best guess at the landfall time.
You have all day today to prepare. There will be some rain around this afternoon but nothing severe. There is still plenty of time to secure your property. For those in evacuation zones who were called to leave, you have the time to do it today. 
Even tomorrow won't be horrible. There will be more rain, & maybe some isolated tornadoes, but the hurricane winds & stronger rain bands likely won't begin to impact us until after 4pm on Wednesday.
We got this Tampa Bay! Focus, plan, act! We've gone through these before. Plan for tree damage & power outages that could last past the weekend for many. The recovery for some will take time, but we will recover. 
More updates throughout the day.
