author: Greg Dee
text:
Milton Midday Update | Tue Oct 8
11am track & advisory is in on Milton. There are more graphics here than words. Feel free to get into the images for some hour-by-hour info. Pay attention to the timestamp in the upper right of each map. 
1.) Milton is a little stronger this morning now that an eyewall replacement cycle has ended & new eyewall has formed. Though the winds are up to 150 mph the latest pressure from the aircraft is 930mb which is a little higher. Milton may be leveling off for a bit. The eye is 3x larger than the one we had yesterday, at 12 nautical miles. 
2.) Milton is slowly being pulled north as it heads east away from the Yucatan & toward the west coast of Florida. Today is your last full day to prepare for the storm. Take advantage of it by securing your property & protecting your family. 
3.) There is little change to the track & the spaghetti models have basically come into agreement with the NHC track. At this point, significant changes to the track are unlikely. Milton will make landfall on the west coast of Florida near Tampa Bay early Thursday morning. 
4.) The timing keeps getting adjusted. NHC now has landfall around 2am Thursday with a position near Davenport at 8am Thursday as still a Cat 2 hurricane. 
5.) The surge forecast hasn't changed & the latest track would point to the highest surges south of the Bay into Manatee & Sarasota county. Here, the surge from Milton may be DOUBLE what it was in Helene or more. Wobbles north or south would change this forecast & a wobble north into southern Pinellas county would push more water into the Bay. Surges over 10 ft. are possible from the south part of the Bay through the entire Manatee & Sarasota County coasts. You should not be in a flood zone during this storm. Please run from the water.  
6.) The NHC is a bit high on the intensity forecast at landfall with a wind speed of 125 mph though honestly not by a ton. I'd say the average model intensity at landfall now is around 115 mph which is still Cat 3. A strong Cat 2 with winds of 110 or 105 mph is possible too. There is little difference between 110 Cat 2 & 115 Cat 3 winds. 
Gusts at landfall & near the eye could exceed 100 mph. Watch the northern & NW part of the eyewall of this one. There may be strong winds in the northern half of the eyewall & this part of the storm could easily track over Pinellas & Hillsborough Counties. 
By far the biggest concern for those not in flood zones is power outages. Trees will be blown over, uprooted or snapped in half. They will fall on homes, cars, fences etc. This is where most of the damage will come from outside of the surge. This area of heavy tree damage will only happen in an area maybe 40-60 mi. wide. Right now the NHC things hurricane winds on landfall will only extend 35 mi from the storm. When it's over Polk County hurricane force winds will only extend 20 miles from the center. You really will have to be right next to the eye to see the strongest winds. Those outside of this area will only see tropical storm wind gusts.      
I would expect that tree damage in some areas will lead to outages that last more than a week. Roads will likely be impassible over a large area on Thursday. Make sure you have enough to live on for 5 days if riding it out at home. 
Charge your batteries, phones, and laptops. Remember some laptops can charge other devices via cables. After the power is out, you may find your home unusually quiet. Be sure to download your favorite playlists in your music app, or maybe some audio books. These can be streamed to a battery-operated Bluetooth speaker with the phone screen off, preserving a bit of battery while cutting into the silence. 
Focus, plan, act. We've done this before, we'll get it done again! The recovery may be slow at first but things can be cleaned up, homes rebuilt & power restored. So many storms have had us going through this drill before that I really do believe that we are ready for the real deal! All those dress rehearsals will make coming out of this one easier. 
Stay strong, Champa Bay!

author: Greg Dee
text:
MILTON EVENING UPDATE TUE OCT 8 7PM
1.) Milton is back to Cat 5 with pressure that continues to fall in the eye. This has caused winds to increase to 165 mph. 
2.) Track is back to ENE but earlier today, while a new eyewall was forming, Milton took a jog, a wobble, to the east. This has shifted the track east too & thus a landfall a little farther south from the previous track. 
3.) Though this improved the surge prospects for Tampa Bay, potentially lowering the surge, it increase the threat for surge across Manatee & Sarasota Counties. The surge forecast has been increased in these areas to 8-12 ft. The surge forecast has been increased by 1ft for Ft. Myers too. 
4.) Timing with Milton hasn't changed much. A landfall of around 2 AM on Thursday still seems reasonable. Milton is still forecast to be weakening as it approaches the coast but a major hurricane is still likely. If Milton continues to maintain Cat 5 intensity it could still be a Cat 4 as it approaches the coast. Future fluctuations in strength are very important to this forecast. 
5.) Milton will become larger as it approaches the coast & weakens spreading its winds over a larger area. Even though the center is now forecast to move farther south, hurricane force winds are still likely to extend north toward Tampa & the I-4 corridor along with Pinellas County. These winds would then move ENE south of I-4 impacting Polk, Manatee, Sarasota, Hardee, DeSoto & Highlands counties.
There's still a lot to be determined with Milton but the track continues to narrow as the storm gets closer. The NHC will have another track update at 11pm tonight & one at 5am tomorrow. You'll see the 11pm post here. I won't do a long update here as I work at 3 am but expect another long update after the 5 am track is released tomorrow. 
Focus, plan, act. Have a good night.

author: Greg Dee
text:
SAT OCT 5 | MORNING UPDATE
NHC continues to push the chance of development in the southern Gulf up. It's now up to 70% in the next 48 hours. A broad area of low pressure has already developed in this area & although it's not fully organized into a tropical system, NHC reports its already producing near Gale force winds. 
The first set of model spaghetti plots is very much similar to the main global models with & maybe, on average, a bit farther south. These will change a lot until the center becomes more well-defined. Multiple NOAA aircraft missions into the storm will likely help inject some higher-quality data. These missions are scheduled today so expect the forecasts to maybe do some re-adjusting as that comes in over the next 24 hours. 
Looking back to the models with Helene, it seemed like the EURO & GFS overall did better together than any of the spaghetti plots. The tropical models were all pointing to a track west of Tallahassee while the EURO & GFS were insistent on Taylor County. In the end, it was a Taylor County landfall so with this one I'm willing to believe the GFS & EURO more at this point. 
It's hard to believe the intensity forecasts as it is early but the range really hasn't changed much recently. We're still looking at anything from a strong tropical storm to a Cat 2 hurricane at this point. The point of landfall is crucial. North of the center is where the heaviest rains will fall with this one & we're likely to see a "reverse surge." South of the center there will be a surge with the on-shore winds & possibly less rain. 
The timing hasn't changed. This is a Wednesday storm. It moves in Wednesday morning on the west coast of Florida & exits into the Atlantic by Wednesday evening. The whole thing is done by late Wednesday night & Thursday the weather will be good. 
More updates to come!

author: Greg Dee
text: TROPICS | The NHC now has development chances for our next system at 70% in the next 2 days. Expect a numbered or named system by Monday in the Gulf, with a possible track from the NHC late Sunday or early Monday. We'll keep you posted as the updates come in!

author: Greg Dee
text: Tropical Depression 14 has developed in the SW Gulf of Mexico. The first track from the NHC brings it to Florida's West Coast Wednesday as a strong Category 2 hurricane. More information to come throughout the day.

author: Greg Dee
text:
Folks, more Dee-tails on this later but I wanted to get this out early. TD 14 poses a significant threat to Florida Wednesday. The track here is very important in terms of impacts & the intensity forecast is always the trickiest but from what we're seeing here this would be a "board up the house" type of storm for whomever the eye comes close to.
The track is not definitely not set. This is a first draft. The cone over Florida is VERY wide indicating a lot of uncertainty. This is not as clear as what we had with Helene.
Remember, the cone represents the area where the eye goes 2/3 of the time. The impacts will extend far beyond the cone.
Please make sure you are frequently checking in on this storm & its progress. Watches and warnings will be needed for the Florida west coast by Monday morning though may be issues as early as Sunday.

author: Greg Dee
text: Breaking. TD 14 is tropical storm Milton.

author: Greg Dee
text: Update: We now have Tropical Storm Milton with winds of 40 mph. Remember, the NHC tracks update every 6 hours. (5pm, 11pm, 5am and 11am)  One other important reminder. The cone you see is not manipulated or moved by the NHC based on expected track of the storm. It has NOTHING to do with the actual storm forecast. It is merely the average margin of error on a track by the NHC over the past 5 years. If the average error is 150 miles on Day 5 (It is), that's how far the cone extends from the forecast center of the storm. Most models are showing if there were to be a change in track, it would be South, not North. As always, we'll be updating 24/7.

author: Greg Dee
text: GFS vs EURO on Wed for #Milton. Slight timing & intensity difference but the position & track is almost identical. Reminiscent of #Helene, which these two models NAILED. #Milton poses a serious threat for the west coast of Florida. #flwx ABC Action News - WFTS - Tampa Bay

author: Greg Dee
text:
MILTON | OCT 5 2024 | 4 PM UPDATE
Here are some thoughts on Milton.
Obviously, things have moved very quickly over the last 24 hours. It's very important that you share all information about this storm with others as people's attention may be down because of the weekend & continued clean-up along the coast. At this point, I would stop all repair efforts along the coast. Don't put up anything new until this threat passes by. It's not worth the risk of ripping it all out again.
TD 14 was quickly upgraded to Tropical Storm Milton this morning. The storm is expected to be a slow mover over the next couple of days before beginning to accelerate toward Florida Monday. At this point the only way it exits the Gulf is through the state.
TRACK TRACK TRACK. It's CRUCIAL! The "winds" that you see listed on the storm's graphics are the maximum winds measured in the storm by the hurricane hunter aircraft. These winds are only VERY close to the center. As Milton becomes stronger, which it will, the strongest winds will CONTRACT or move close to, the center. This means that, for instance, if the winds reach 110 mph, that 110 mph wind will only be found within 5-10 miles of the eye on the right front side of the eye wall. The rest of the storm will feature winds that are MUCH less. The wind speeds drop quickly as you move away from the eye with, by far, the largest area of the storm being composed of tropical storm force winds, even if it's categorized as a hurricane. This is why the exact track is so important.
We are 4 days out from the storm. The NHC forecast is historically the most accurate. At this range, the average error is 125-150 miles. That's a HUGE range & will make an ENORMOUS difference in what any area experienced. A 150 mile difference could mean anything from Cat 3 winds & damaging surge to 50 mph winds & no surge at all, maybe even a reverse surge. We simply don't know where the eye will go, & for this one, unlike Helene, every mile the eye wobbles or moves will absolutely make a difference in who gets what & how much. I can't tell you what you'll get just yet.
Milton's position & track is basically the one we want to see the least for Tampa Bay. This kind of approach would definitely move water & the wind could be an issue too. The NHC has Milton making landfall with winds of 110 mph, a strong cat 2 but cat 3 starts at 111 mph. 
It's best to assume, & prepare for, Milton making landfall as a major category 3 hurricane or stronger & getting surprised with a weaker storm. 
The average error in intensity 4 days out is around 15 mph. That makes a big difference. The EURO keeps Milton a tropical storm while the GFS makes it a cat 2 hurricane. Some of the models make it stronger but overall I'd lean toward the GFS when it comes to intensity, it did better with Helene in that regard. 
It's also important to understand that we are still very early in the tracking process. I realize the cone looks huge & covers the whole state, but please understand that the worst part of the storm will only be 40-50 miles wide. The other 400 miles of storms will be substantially less strong. So although most that are in the cone now will get something from this storm, the VAST majority of people will not see the maximum impacts of the storms. Preparations however are best done for the worst assuming you're doing so out of an abundance of caution & that it's most likely that what your particular area gets will not be the worst. 
This is not the time to board up & really put plans into actions just yet. We're still in the watch & track phase. This is the time to get regular updates & maybe make some early plans so that when watches & warnings are issued you can put those plans into action. 
Do you have your hurricane kit? 
Did you use something from that kit in Helene that you need to replace? 
Do you know where all your window covers are? 
What about the hardware to attach them? 
Are your batteries fresh? 
Have any non-perishable food items expired? 
Have you backed up all important documents to the cloud? 
Do you have physical copies of those documents protected from wind & water so that they can be accessed if the internet is out & those backups are not accessible?
These are the types of things you could have been doing in May. If you didn't & are feeling some nervous energy, use it to get the basics covered now. 
I'll be posting updates & insights throughout the day & weekend.

text: Tropical Storm Milton 5pm Track:  Obviously, not a great track for the Bay Area. Remember, this far out, the average margin of error is 150 miles. So, the entire West coast of Florida is still in play for landfall. Latest track has bumped up intensity 5 mph making Milton a Cat 3 hurricane at landfall. Surge and strongest winds will be along and just SOUTH of where the eye makes landfall. There will be a swath of 5-10 miles that will see the extreme impacts. You've got to prepare as if where you live will be in that zone. Obviously, the odds are higher that it will be elsewhere considering the large margin of error this far out, but DO NOT underestimate or minimize potential threat with this system. We will update 24/7 and our 1st DPL will stream at 7pm on Facebook tonight and our ABC Action News Streaming Channel.

author: Greg Dee
text:
SAT OCT 5 | 10 pm
Quick check of the spaghetti plot this evening yields no extra clarity. Models jumping all over the place. It’s interesting to see that although many are clustering north, there continues to be some that point more south into South Florida with a break in-between. That’s interesting. Typically, if there is little agreement, you get a “shot gun” type spread in the model tracks, with the lines equally spread over a wide area. Here we seem to have two groupings. A track more to the south, into the Everglades is a bad deal for south Florida but it definitely helps those on the west coast. Time will tell.

author: Greg Dee
text:
I know you’ve been seeing a ton of Milton maps out there, some even zoomed all the way down into the Bay area. All of them showing positions & lines. That middle line is really a hurdle for our brain making it difficult to ignore. The line connects points, the positions from the NHC which are drawn out in time. The positions are nearly places in the middle of a line that shows the width of the forecast cone which highlights the area the eye stays in 2/3 of the time. The lines get longer as the cone gets wider as the average error grows to 150 miles this far out. The lines are drawn at time intervals & which ones are shown is decided by the person making the map. 
You see, maps are all inherently wrong. There is no perfectly accurate map. That’s because maps are 2D & the world is in 3D. No matter how you draw a map, & which type of map you choose to use, you’re always throwing something off, distorting some truth, skewing reality. Of course, only the map maker knows these faults. Nowhere on the map does it say what to watch out for. Our brains analyze the image & naturally follow the cone mid-way through, drawing that track line down the middle whether it’s there or not. 
I would really prefer that the NHC forecast just look like this. The cone, & position markings for the lines drawn at specific intervals indicating where the eye may be 24, 48 or 72 hours from now. No mid-point line, no position icons. 
Why? Well, the storm doesn’t move in straight lines. It doesn’t perfectly cut right down the middle of the cone as if it knew the average of the spaghetti plot & just chose the most likely route. Storms are erratic. They’re not on a track, & certainly not on a line. They wobble & slide, take two steps forward and a half step back. They’re chaotic, random & full of imperfect motion. The map, with its cone, positions & center line try to smooth all that out, take out the chaos, the imperfections, the uncertainties, sometimes to a fault. Smoothing the twist & turns, eases our brains into a satisfying level of organization & reason that convince us that what we’re seeing is a true projection of reality, the actual track. 
Nothing can be farther from the truth. The truth is the storm will take its own path from its current position to some future location off the Florida east coast. At times its path will follow the GFS, at others the HWRF, then the EURO only to finish on a little UKMET just to keep it interesting. 
I’ll be here updating you on it all. Until then, don’t stress out about the exact track. No matter which track you see drawn on a map, I can guarantee the storm will not take any of them exactly.  
#milton #hurricane #storm #florida #tampa #tampabay #severeweather #SevereWeatherPreparedness #SevereWeatherAlert

author: Greg Dee
text: 11pm Tropical Storm Milton Update:  New track is in. Milton is a bit stronger tonight.  No real changes to the thinking or track, though. . Milton is a small storm, which means it can strengthen (or weaken) quicker. NHC still has it as a Cat 3 as it makes landfall. Again, the average margin of error in track this far out is 150 miles. The pretty much includes most of the West coast of Florida. Hurricane Watches and Storm Surge watches will be issued for our area on Sunday. Updates 24/7 as always including overnight.

author: Greg Dee
text:
The 11 pm advisory comes with a track update. There are no major changes.
Generally the NHC does not believe that Milton will ever make any kind of southward dip in its track like some of the models are indicating. 
The official forecast keeps Milton on a eastward motion over the next couple of days before turning more NE toward the west coast of Florida. Intensity forecast seems to be consolidating on a stronger storm. The NHC has chnaged little in its down intensity forecast keeping this a cat 2 hurricane even a bit after landfall somehwere on the Florida west coast. 
Next track update is at 5am.
